topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Maximum Flow Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: MAXIMUM FLOW ALGORITHMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> A **flow network** is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where each edge $(u, v) \in E$ has a non-negative **capacity** $c(u, v) \geq 0$. A **flow** is a real-valued function $f: V \times V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that satisfies two properties: **Capacity Constraint** ($f(u, v) \leq c(u, v)$) and **Flow Conservation** (for all $u \neq s, t$, the sum of flow in equals the sum of flow out). The **maximum-flow problem** asks to find a flow of maximum value from a designated **source** $s$ to a designated **sink** $t$.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipe Network"

Imagine a city water-distribution system modeled as a graph:

- **Pipes** $\rightarrow$ Directed edges $(u, v)$
- **Pipe Diameter / Maximum Throughput** $\rightarrow$ Capacity $c(u, v)$
- **Water Flowing per Second** $\rightarrow$ Flow value $f(u, v)$
- **Reservoir (Source)** $\rightarrow$ Vertex $s$ that *generates* flow
- **City Outlet (Sink)** $\rightarrow$ Vertex $t$ that *absorbs* flow
- **Junctions** $\rightarrow$ Intermediate vertices where flow is conserved (water in = water out)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** No pipe can carry more water than its diameter allows (**capacity constraint**), and at every junction (except the source and sink) the total water entering must equal the total water leaving (**flow conservation**). The *Maximum Flow* is literally the maximum amount of water that can travel from reservoir to outlet per second without bursting any pipe.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Source | $s$ | Vertex where net flow is generated |
| Sink | $t$ | Vertex where net flow is absorbed |
| Capacity | $c(u, v)$ | Maximum permissible flow on edge $(u, v)$ |
| Flow | $f(u, v)$ | Actual flow assigned to edge $(u, v)$ |
| Net Flow Out of $s$ | $\vert f \vert$ | Total value of the flow |
| Antiparallel Edges | — | Not allowed; must be resolved via splitting |
| Skew Symmetry | $f(u, v) = -f(v, u)$ | Required for residual accounting |

### 1.3 Antiparallel Edges — A Critical Pre-processing Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **flow network**, it is mathematically required that for any pair of vertices $(u, v)$, **at most one of the two directed edges** $(u, v)$ or $(v, u)$ may exist. This is called the **no-antiparallel-edges** condition. If the input contains both $(u, v)$ and $(v, u)$, we must introduce an intermediate vertex $u'$ to break the cycle. This pre-processing step adds at most one vertex and one edge, preserving the asymptotic complexity of all algorithms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Properties of a Flow

A function $f: V \times V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is a **valid flow** if and only if it satisfies:

1. **Capacity Constraint:** For all $u, v \in V$,
$$f(u, v) \leq c(u, v)$$

2. **Skew Symmetry:** For all $u, v \in V$,
$$f(u, v) = -f(v, u)$$

3. **Flow Conservation:** For all $u \in V - \{s, t\}$,
$$\sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why skew symmetry matters:** It allows us to *implicitly* represent $f(v, u)$ even when the edge $(v, u)$ does **not** physically exist. We define $f(v, u) = -f(u, v)$ when $(u, v) \in E$, and $f(u, v) = 0$ when neither $(u, v)$ nor $(v, u)$ is in $E$. This bookkeeping is what powers the **residual network**.

## 2.2 The Value of a Flow

The total flow leaving the source is given by:

$$\vert f \vert = \sum_{v \in V} f(s, v)$$

Equivalently, since flow is conserved, this also equals the total flow entering the sink:

$$\vert f \vert = \sum_{v \in V} f(v, t)$$

## 2.3 Residual Networks — The Engine of Augmentation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Given a flow $f$, the **residual capacity** $c_f(u, v)$ of an edge $(u, v)$ is the amount of *additional* flow that can be pushed along that edge.

$$c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$$

- If $f(u, v) > 0$, then $(v, u)$ has a **residual edge** with capacity $f(u, v)$ (i.e., we can *cancel* previously sent flow).
- If $f(u, v) < c(u, v)$, then $(u, v)$ has a **residual edge** with capacity $c(u, v) - f(u, v)$ (i.e., we can *push more* flow).

The **residual network** is $G_f = (V, E_f)$ where

$$E_f = \{(u, v) \in V \times V : c_f(u, v) > 0\}$$

## 2.4 Augmenting Paths

An **augmenting path** $p$ is a simple path from $s$ to $t$ in the residual network $G_f$. The **residual capacity** of the path is:

$$c_f(p) = \min \{c_f(u, v) : (u, v) \in p\}$$

We can increase the flow by $c_f(p)$ along every edge of $p$:

$$f_p(u, v) = \begin{cases} c_f(p) & \text{if } (u, v) \in p \\ -c_f(p) & \text{if } (v, u) \in p \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The updated flow is $f' = f + f_p$, with $\vert f' \vert = \vert f \vert + c_f(p)$.

## 2.5 Cuts of a Flow Network

An **$s$-$t$ cut** $(S, T)$ is a partition of $V$ with $s \in S$ and $t \in T$. The **cut capacity** is:

$$c(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S, \, v \in T} c(u, v)$$

The **net flow across the cut** is:

$$f(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S, \, v \in T} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S, \, v \in T} f(v, u)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Invariant (often tested):** For *any* flow $f$ and *any* $s$-$t$ cut $(S, T)$:
$$f(S, T) = \vert f \vert$$
This says the net flow across **any** cut equals the value of the flow — a beautiful and exam-favorite identity.

## 2.6 The Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem (FOUNDATIONAL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem (Ford & Fulkerson, 1956):**
> The following three statements are equivalent:
> 1. $f$ is a **maximum flow** in $G$.
> 2. The residual network $G_f$ contains **no augmenting path** from $s$ to $t$.
> 3. There exists an $s$-$t$ cut $(S, T)$ such that $\vert f \vert = c(S, T)$ (i.e., the cut capacity equals the flow value, meaning the cut is a **minimum cut**).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vert f \vert = \sum_{v} f(s, v)$ | Computing total flow value |
| 2 | $f(u, v) \leq c(u, v)$ | Capacity constraint check |
| 3 | $\sum_{v} f(u, v) = 0, \, u \neq s, t$ | Flow conservation check |
| 4 | $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$ | Residual capacity |
| 5 | $c_f(p) = \min_{(u,v)\in p} c_f(u, v)$ | Bottleneck of augmenting path |
| 6 | $f' = f + f_p, \,\, \vert f' \vert = \vert f \vert + c_f(p)$ | Flow update after augmentation |
| 7 | $f(S, T) = \vert f \vert$ for all cuts | Cross-cut flow invariant |
| 8 | $\vert f^* \vert = \min c(S, T)$ | Max-Flow = Min-Cut (theorem) |
| 9 | $\sum f_{in}(u) - \sum f_{out}(u) = 0$ | Vertex balance equation |
| 10 | $f(u, v) = -f(v, u)$ | Skew symmetry |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Telecommunications:** Maximum bandwidth routing between two nodes in a packet-switched network.
- **Bipartite Matching:** Reducing a job-assignment problem to a max-flow problem (add source and sink, set all edge capacities to 1).
- **Image Segmentation:** Computer vision algorithms like GrabCut model foreground/background separation as a min-cut problem.
- **Transportation & Logistics:** Maximizing goods shipped from a factory hub to a distribution center given road capacities.
- **Airline Scheduling:** Optimizing aircraft/crew routing under resource constraints.
- **VLSI Design:** Routing wires on a chip while respecting channel capacities.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Ford–Fulkerson Method (General Schema)

### Generic Pseudocode (Algorithm-Only — CS Subject)

```
FORD-FULKERSON(G, s, t):
  1. for each edge (u,v) ∈ E(G):
  2.     f(u,v) ← 0, f(v,u) ← 0
  3. while there exists a path p from s to t in G_f:
  4.     c_f(p) ← min { c_f(u,v) : (u,v) ∈ p }
  5.     for each edge (u,v) ∈ p:
  6.         if (u,v) ∈ E:
  7.             f(u,v) ← f(u,v) + c_f(p)
  8.         else:
  9.             f(v,u) ← f(v,u) - c_f(p)
  10. return f
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Termination & Correctness:** Ford–Fulkerson terminates only if edge capacities are **integers** (or rationals with bounded denominators). When it terminates, by the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem, the resulting $f$ is a **maximum flow**. The runtime bound is $O(E \cdot \vert f^* \vert)$ where $\vert f^* \vert$ is the value of the maximum flow.

## 3.2 The Edmonds–Karp Algorithm (BFS-based Augmentation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Improvement:** Edmonds–Karp specifies that the augmenting path in step 3 must be the **shortest** such path, found via **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** on the residual graph. This single change makes the algorithm **strongly polynomial** in $O(V E^2)$, independent of the actual flow values.

### Why BFS Guarantees Polynomial Time

Every BFS augmentation increases the **shortest-path distance from $s$ to $t$** monotonically. The total number of augmentations is bounded by $O(VE)$ because each augmentation "saturates" or "empties" at least one edge, and any edge can be saturated/emptied at most $O(V)$ times. Hence:

$$T(n) = O(V E^2)$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Ford–Fulkerson Execution

Consider the following flow network with source $s = A$ and sink $t = D$.

**Capacities:**
- $c(A, B) = 16$
- $c(A, C) = 13$
- $c(B, C) = 10$
- $c(B, D) = 12$
- $c(C, B) = 4$ (residual of $B \to C$)
- $c(C, D) = 14$

> [!NOTE]
> Step 0: Initialize $f(e) = 0$ for all edges.

### Iteration 1: Find Augmenting Path

Choose path $p_1 = A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D$.

- Bottleneck: $\min(c(A, B), c(B, D)) = \min(16, 12) = 12$.
- Residual capacity $c_f(p_1) = 12$.

Update flows:
- $f(A, B) = 0 + 12 = 12$
- $f(B, D) = 0 + 12 = 12$

Total flow so far: $\vert f \vert = 12$.

### Iteration 2: Find Next Augmenting Path

Choose path $p_2 = A \rightarrow C \rightarrow D$.

- Bottleneck: $\min(c(A, C), c(C, D)) = \min(13, 14) = 13$.
- Residual capacity $c_f(p_2) = 13$.

Update flows:
- $f(A, C) = 0 + 13 = 13$
- $f(C, D) = 0 + 13 = 13$

Total flow so far: $\vert f \vert = 12 + 13 = 25$.

### Iteration 3: Check Residual Network

Build the residual network after iterations 1 and 2:

**Forward residual capacities (where $c - f > 0$):**
- $c_f(A, B) = 16 - 12 = 4$
- $c_f(A, C) = 13 - 13 = 0$ (saturated)
- $c_f(B, C) = 10 - 0 = 10$
- $c_f(C, D) = 14 - 13 = 1$
- $c_f(B, D) = 12 - 12 = 0$ (saturated)

**Backward residual capacities (where $f > 0$):**
- $c_f(B, A) = f(A, B) = 12$
- $c_f(D, B) = f(B, D) = 12$
- $c_f(C, A) = f(A, C) = 13$
- $c_f(D, C) = f(C, D) = 13$

Now look for a path from $A$ to $D$ in this residual graph. Available forward edges: $A \to B$ (cap 4), $B \to C$ (cap 10), $C \to D$ (cap 1).

Path $p_3 = A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D$:
- Bottleneck: $\min(4, 10, 1) = 1$.
- $c_f(p_3) = 1$.

Update flows:
- $f(A, B) = 12 + 1 = 13$
- $f(B, C) = 0 + 1 = 1$
- $f(C, D) = 13 + 1 = 14$

Total flow so far: $\vert f \vert = 25 + 1 = 26$.

### Iteration 4: Verify Max-Flow via Min-Cut

Residual edges remaining with positive capacity:
- $A \to B$ (4), $B \to A$ (13), $B \to C$ (9), $C \to B$ (1), $C \to A$ (13), $D \to B$ (12), $D \to C$ (14).

No path from $A$ to $D$ exists in the residual graph (both forward edges $A \to B \to C$ and $A \to C$ lead to dead-ends; $D$ is unreachable from $A$). Therefore, by the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem, $f$ is **maximum**.

The **minimum cut** is found by BFS in the residual graph: $S = \{A, B, C\}$ and $T = \{D\}$.

$$\min c(S, T) = c(B, D) + c(C, D) = 12 + 14 = 26$$

Confirmed: $\vert f^* \vert = 26 = c(S, T)$. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Augmenting Path Strategy | Time Complexity | Requires Integer Capacities? | Strongly Polynomial? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ford–Fulkerson** | Any path (e.g., DFS) | $O(E \cdot \vert f^* \vert)$ | Yes (for termination guarantee) | No |
| **Edmonds–Karp** | Shortest path (BFS) | $O(V E^2)$ | No | Yes |
| **Dinic's Algorithm** | BFS levels + blocking flows | $O(V^2 E)$ | No | Yes |
| **Push-Relabel (Goldberg-Tarjan)** | Local relabeling + pushes | $O(V^2 E)$ or $O(V^3)$ | No | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Edmonds–Karp is the **default** answer when the question asks for a polynomial-time max-flow algorithm. The maximum number of augmentations in Edmonds–Karp is at most $\frac{VE}{2}$ (with the actual bound being $O(VE)$), giving the $O(VE^2)$ total complexity.

## 3.5 Derivation — Why $\vert f \vert = f(S, T)$ for All Cuts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Claim:** For any $s$-$t$ cut $(S, T)$ and any flow $f$, $f(S, T) = \vert f \vert$.

**Proof:**

$$f(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S, \, v \in T} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S, \, v \in T} f(v, u)$$

Expand both sums over **all** $u \in S, v \in V$:

$$f(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} f(u, v) - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} f(v, u)$$

By skew symmetry, the last two sums cancel:

$$\sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} f(v, u) = - \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} f(u, v)$$

Therefore:

$$f(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) - 0 = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v)$$

Split into $v \in S$ and $v \in T$. The $v \in S$ part vanishes by flow conservation at every internal vertex:

$$\sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in S} f(u, v) = 0 \quad \text{(by flow conservation on each } u \in S - \{s\})$$

Hence:

$$f(S, T) = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in T} f(u, v) + \sum_{u \in S, v \in S} f(u, v) = \sum_{u \in S} \sum_{v \in V} f(u, v) = \sum_{v \in V} f(s, v) = \vert f \vert$$

$\blacksquare$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Original Flow Network (Worked Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA((A - source))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeC((C))
    nodeD((D - sink))
    nodeA -- "16" --> nodeB
    nodeA -- "13" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "10" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "12" --> nodeD
    nodeC -- "14" --> nodeD
```

> **Diagram Reading:** The numeric labels on each edge are the **edge capacities** $c(u, v)$. Source is $A$ (left), sink is $D$ (right). The graph has 4 vertices and 5 directed edges (no antiparallel pair here).

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Ford–Fulkerson Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputBlock["INPUT: Flow Network G, source s, sink t"]
    initBlock["INIT: Set all flows f(u,v) = 0"]
    residualBuild["BUILD: Residual Network G_f"]
    bfsPath["SEARCH: Find augmenting path p in G_f via BFS or DFS"]
    decisionCheck{"Path p exists?"}
    bottleneck["COMPUTE: Bottleneck c_f(p) = min c_f(u,v) along p"]
    augment["AUGMENT: Update f = f + f_p along path p"]
    outputBlock["OUTPUT: Maximum flow f*"]
    stopNoPath["STOP: G_f has no s-t path; f is max by Min-Cut theorem"]

    inputBlock --> initBlock
    initBlock --> residualBuild
    residualBuild --> bfsPath
    bfsPath --> decisionCheck
    decisionCheck -- "Yes" --> bottleneck
    bottleneck --> augment
    augment --> residualBuild
    decisionCheck -- "No" --> stopNoPath
    stopNoPath --> outputBlock
```

> **Architectural Reading:** The loop `residualBuild → bfsPath → augment → residualBuild` is the iterative heart. The exit condition is precisely the *absence* of an $s$-$t$ path in the current residual network, which the Max-Flow Min-Cut theorem equates to optimality.

## 4.3 Residual Network After Two Augmentations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA((A))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeC((C))
    nodeD((D))
    nodeA -- "4" --> nodeB
    nodeA -- "0 saturated" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "10" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "0 saturated" --> nodeD
    nodeC -- "1" --> nodeD
    nodeB -- "12 back" --> nodeA
    nodeC -- "13 back" --> nodeA
    nodeD -- "12 back" --> nodeB
    nodeD -- "13 back" --> nodeC
```

> **Reading:** Solid lines are forward residual edges, dashed-labeled lines are backward residual edges. Edge $A \to C$ is now saturated (zero residual forward capacity), and the only path from $A$ to $D$ requires going through the bottleneck edge $C \to D$ with residual capacity 1.

## 4.4 Maximum-Flow Solution (Final State)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA((A))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeC((C))
    nodeD((D))
    nodeA -- "f=13 / 16" --> nodeB
    nodeA -- "f=13 / 13 saturated" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "f=1 / 10" --> nodeC
    nodeB -- "f=12 / 12 saturated" --> nodeD
    nodeC -- "f=14 / 14 saturated" --> nodeD
```

> **Final Verification:** $\vert f \vert = f(A, B) + f(A, C) = 13 + 13 = 26$. Also, $\vert f \vert = f(B, D) + f(C, D) = 12 + 14 = 26$. The minimum cut $\{A, B, C\}$ vs $\{D\}$ has capacity 26. All three quantities match, confirming optimality. ✓

---
