<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** 2-mark conceptual + 1-mark example/diagram. Keep answers **30–50 words**.

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define the **Transportation Problem (TP)**. What conditions must be satisfied for a TP to be called *balanced*?

> **Model Answer:**
> The Transportation Problem is a special class of Linear Programming that determines the **minimum-cost shipping schedule** for a homogeneous product from $m$ origins (with supply $a_i$) to $n$ destinations (with demand $b_j$). It is *balanced* when $\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} b_j$ so that the supply exactly equals the demand. (3 marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the **MODI method's optimality condition** for a transportation problem.

> **Model Answer:**
> In the MODI ($u_i$–$v_j$) method, after computing the multipliers from the basic cells using $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$, the opportunity cost of every non-basic cell is $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$. The current BFS is **optimal** for a minimisation problem **if and only if** $\Delta_{ij} \ge 0$ for all non-basic cells. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two sub-parts of 7 marks each, OR one full 14-mark problem. An *internal choice* means you may answer **either Question A or Question B**.

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Likely Model]**

> A company has **3 factories** $F_1, F_2, F_3$ and **4 warehouses** $W_1, W_2, W_3, W_4$ with the following data:

| | $W_1$ | $W_2$ | $W_3$ | $W_4$ | **Supply** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $F_1$ | 3 | 1 | 7 | 4 | 300 |
| $F_2$ | 2 | 6 | 5 | 9 | 400 |
| $F_3$ | 8 | 3 | 3 | 2 | 100 |
| **Demand** | 250 | 350 | 150 | 50 | **800** |

**(a)** Formulate the problem as a **Linear Program**. State the objective, supply, and demand constraints explicitly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the **initial basic feasible solution** using **Vogel's Approximation Method** and compute the corresponding total cost. Show all penalty calculations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Decision Variable:** Let $x_{ij}$ be the number of units shipped from factory $F_i$ to warehouse $W_j$, $i \in \{1,2,3\}$, $j \in \{1,2,3,4\}$.

**Objective Function:** Minimise the total transportation cost.

$$Z_{\min} \;=\; 3x_{11} + 1x_{12} + 7x_{13} + 4x_{14} + 2x_{21} + 6x_{22} + 5x_{23} + 9x_{24} + 8x_{31} + 3x_{32} + 3x_{33} + 2x_{34}$$

**Supply Constraints:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{11} + x_{12} + x_{13} + x_{14} &= 300 \quad (F_1)\\
x_{21} + x_{22} + x_{23} + x_{24} &= 400 \quad (F_2)\\
x_{31} + x_{32} + x_{33} + x_{34} &= 100 \quad (F_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Demand Constraints:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{11} + x_{21} + x_{31} &= 250 \quad (W_1)\\
x_{12} + x_{22} + x_{32} &= 350 \quad (W_2)\\
x_{13} + x_{23} + x_{33} &= 150 \quad (W_3)\\
x_{14} + x_{24} + x_{34} &= 50 \quad (W_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Non-negativity:** $x_{ij} \ge 0$ for all $i, j$.

**Balance check:** $300 + 400 + 100 = 800 = 250 + 350 + 150 + 50$ ✓ — Balanced TP.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — VAM

**Step 1:** Compute row and column penalties.

- Row $F_1$: sorted costs $\{1, 3, 4, 7\}$, penalty $= 3 - 1 = 2$.
- Row $F_2$: sorted costs $\{2, 5, 6, 9\}$, penalty $= 5 - 2 = 3$.
- Row $F_3$: sorted costs $\{2, 3, 3, 8\}$, penalty $= 3 - 2 = 1$.
- Col $W_1$: $\{2, 3, 8\}$, penalty $= 3 - 2 = 1$.
- Col $W_2$: $\{1, 3, 6\}$, penalty $= 3 - 1 = 2$.
- Col $W_3$: $\{3, 5, 7\}$, penalty $= 5 - 3 = 2$.
- Col $W_4$: $\{2, 4, 9\}$, penalty $= 4 - 2 = 2$.

> Maximum penalty $= 3$ at row $F_2$. Minimum cost in $F_2$ is $2$ (cell $F_2 W_1$).
> Allocate $\min(400, 250) = 250$ to $(F_2, W_1)$. Row $F_2$ reduces to $400 - 250 = 150$, column $W_1$ is satisfied (demand $= 0$). **Strike out column $W_1$**.

**Step 2:** New table (with $W_1$ removed):

| | $W_2$ | $W_3$ | $W_4$ | **Supply** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $F_1$ | 1 | 7 | 4 | 300 |
| $F_2$ | 6 | 5 | 9 | 150 |
| $F_3$ | 3 | 3 | 2 | 100 |
| **Demand** | 350 | 150 | 50 | |

- Row $F_1$: $\{1, 4, 7\}$, penalty $= 4 - 1 = 3$.
- Row $F_2$: $\{5, 6, 9\}$, penalty $= 6 - 5 = 1$.
- Row $F_3$: $\{2, 3, 3\}$, penalty $= 3 - 2 = 1$.
- Col $W_2$: $\{1, 3, 6\}$, penalty $= 3 - 1 = 2$.
- Col $W_3$: $\{3, 5, 7\}$, penalty $= 5 - 3 = 2$.
- Col $W_4$: $\{2, 4, 9\}$, penalty $= 4 - 2 = 2$.

> Maximum penalty $= 3$ at row $F_1$. Minimum cost in $F_1$ is $1$ (cell $F_1 W_2$).
> Allocate $\min(300, 350) = 300$ to $(F_1, W_2)$. Row $F_1$ supply becomes $0$ → **strike out row $F_1$**.

**Step 3:** Reduced table:

| | $W_2$ | $W_3$ | $W_4$ | **Supply** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $F_2$ | 6 | 5 | 9 | 150 |
| $F_3$ | 3 | 3 | 2 | 100 |
| **Demand** | 50 | 150 | 50 | |

- Row $F_2$: $\{5, 6, 9\}$, penalty $= 1$.
- Row $F_3$: $\{2, 3, 3\}$, penalty $= 1$.
- Col $W_2$: $\{3, 6\}$, penalty $= 3$.
- Col $W_3$: $\{3, 5\}$, penalty $= 2$.
- Col $W_4$: $\{2, 9\}$, penalty $= 7$.

> Maximum penalty $= 7$ at column $W_4$. Minimum cost in $W_4$ is $2$ (cell $F_3 W_4$).
> Allocate $\min(100, 50) = 50$ to $(F_3, W_4)$. Column $W_4$ is satisfied.

**Step 4:** Reduced table:

| | $W_2$ | $W_3$ | **Supply** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_2$ | 6 | 5 | 150 |
| $F_3$ | 3 | 3 | 50 |
| **Demand** | 50 | 150 | |

- Row $F_2$: penalty $= 1$.
- Row $F_3$: penalty $= 0$ (both costs equal).
- Col $W_2$: $\{3, 6\}$, penalty $= 3$.
- Col $W_3$: $\{3, 5\}$, penalty $= 2$.

> Maximum penalty $= 3$ at column $W_2$. Minimum cost in $W_2$ is $3$ (cell $F_3 W_2$).
> Allocate $\min(50, 50) = 50$ to $(F_3, W_2)$. Both row $F_3$ and column $W_2$ are satisfied.

**Step 5:** Last cell: $(F_2, W_3) = 150$.

**Step 6 — Final VAM Solution Table:**

| Cell | $x_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ | $c_{ij} x_{ij}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(F_2, W_1)$ | 250 | 2 | 500 |
| $(F_1, W_2)$ | 300 | 1 | 300 |
| $(F_3, W_4)$ | 50 | 2 | 100 |
| $(F_3, W_2)$ | 50 | 3 | 150 |
| $(F_2, W_3)$ | 150 | 5 | 750 |
| **Total** | 800 | | **1800** |

$$
\boxed{\;Z_{\text{VAM}} = \text{₹}\,1800, \quad \text{Number of basic cells} = 5 = m + n - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1\;\checkmark\;}
$$

> (For full marks, examiners expect a follow-up **MODI optimality test** and possible loop-improvement iterations. The VAM solution $Z = 1800$ is *not* necessarily optimal — students should run MODI to verify. For brevity, we present only the VAM stage here.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **Dijkstra's algorithm** for finding the single-source shortest path in a weighted graph. Mention its time complexity and the condition under which it fails. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the directed graph with vertices $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and edges with weights as listed below. Find the **shortest path from $S = A$ to $T = F$** using Dijkstra's algorithm. Show every iteration clearly. **(7 marks)**

| Edge | $w$ | Edge | $w$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A \to B$ | 7 | $C \to F$ | 2 |
| $A \to C$ | 3 | $D \to E$ | 4 |
| $B \to D$ | 2 | $D \to F$ | 5 |
| $C \to B$ | 2 | $E \to F$ | 1 |
| $C \to D$ | 4 | $B \to E$ | 5 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (Concise Examiner-Friendly Answer)

**Dijkstra's Algorithm (Greedy SSSP):**

1. Initialise $d(s) = 0$ and $d(v) = \infty$ for all $v \ne s$. Mark all vertices *unvisited*. Set predecessor $\pi(v) = \text{null}$.
2. Create a min-priority queue $Q$ keyed on $d(\cdot)$ and insert all vertices.
3. While $Q$ is not empty:
   - Extract vertex $u$ with minimum $d(u)$ from $Q$.
   - Mark $u$ as *visited* (finalised).
   - For every outgoing edge $(u, v)$ with weight $w(u, v)$:
     - **Relaxation:** If $d(u) + w(u, v) < d(v)$, set $d(v) = d(u) + w(u, v)$ and $\pi(v) = u$; update $Q$.
4. Stop. The array $d[\cdot]$ gives the shortest distance from $s$ to every vertex.

**Time Complexity:** $O\big((V + E) \log V\big)$ using a binary min-heap; $O(V^2)$ with an array.

**Failure Condition:** Dijkstra **fails (gives incorrect answer)** when the graph contains **negative edge weights** (or negative-weight cycles reachable from $s$). For such cases, the **Bellman–Ford algorithm** must be used.

**Schematic Diagram** (re-use the Mermaid block from Section 4.2 to illustrate the relaxation process).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Dijkstra Trace

| Iter | Visited | $d(A)$ | $d(B)$ | $d(C)$ | $d(D)$ | $d(E)$ | $d(F)$ | Min-Heap Extract |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\emptyset$ | **0** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $A$ |
| 1 | $\{A\}$ | 0 | $0+7=7$ | $0+3=3$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $C$ |
| 2 | $\{A, C\}$ | 0 | $\min(7, 3+2)=5$ | **3** | $3+4=7$ | $\infty$ | $3+2=5$ | $B$ (ties — pick B first) |
| 3 | $\{A, C, B\}$ | 0 | **5** | 3 | $\min(7, 5+2)=7$ | $5+5=10$ | $\min(5, 5+5)=5$ | $F$ (or $D$; both $d=5$) |
| 4 | $\{A, C, B, F\}$ | 0 | 5 | 3 | 7 | 10 | **5** | $D$ |
| 5 | $\{A, C, B, F, D\}$ | 0 | 5 | 3 | **7** | $\min(10, 7+4)=10$ | 5 | $E$ |
| 6 | $\{A, C, B, F, D, E\}$ | 0 | 5 | 3 | 7 | **10** | 5 | — |

**Result:** $d(F) = 5$. Back-tracing: $F \leftarrow C \leftarrow A$.

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Shortest path} = A \to C \to F, \quad \text{Cost} = 3 + 2 = 5\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to balance the TP** — If $\sum a_i \ne \sum b_j$, you must add a **dummy row or column** with zero cost before applying VAM/MODI. Students routinely lose 2 marks here.
> 2. **Wrong penalty calculation in VAM** — The penalty is the difference between the **two smallest costs in a row/column**, not the largest and smallest. A common mistake costs 3 marks.
> 3. **Degeneracy in TP** — If VAM yields fewer than $m + n - 1$ positive allocations, place a tiny symbol $\varepsilon$ in the lowest-cost empty cell to avoid zero-pivots in MODI.
> 4. **Dijkstra applied to negative weights** — Examiners specifically test this: state *explicitly* that Dijkstra assumes $w(e) \ge 0$ and fails for negative weights.
> 5. **Wrong initial $d(\cdot)$** — Some students forget to set $d(\text{source}) = 0$, causing the entire trace to collapse. Always start with $d(s) = 0$ and the rest as $\infty$.
> 6. **Not back-tracing the path** — Writing only the distance, not the actual path, loses 2 marks in KTU evaluation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this up before your exam!)**

- [x] **TP = minimum-cost shipping** on a bipartite network, solved as an LP.
- [x] **Balanced TP** condition: $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$. If not balanced → add dummy row/column with $c = 0$.
- [x] **Number of basic cells** in any BFS of a TP = $m + n - 1$. Less than this → *degenerate*.
- [x] **Three heuristics for initial BFS**: NWCM (fast, poor), LCM (greedy), VAM (best quality, uses penalties).
- [x] **VAM Penalty** for row/column = (second-smallest cost) − (smallest cost). Pick the row/column with the **largest** penalty; allocate to the smallest-cost cell within it.
- [x] **MODI method**: solve $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ for basic cells; compute $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - u_i - v_j$ for non-basic. **Optimal iff every** $\Delta_{ij} \ge 0$.
- [x] **Stepping Stone method** is the alternative to MODI; uses closed loops to compute the same opportunity cost.
- [x] **Dijkstra's algorithm** solves SSSP in $O((V + E)\log V)$ with a min-heap. Fails on negative weights.
- [x] **Bellman–Ford** handles negative weights (no negative cycles) in $O(VE)$.
- [x] **RCSP / Constrained Shortest Path** is **NP-hard**. Use Lagrangian relaxation with modified edge weights $\tilde{w} = c + \lambda r$.
- [x] **Minimum Cost Flow (MCF)** generalises the TP to general networks. The TP is the MCF restricted to a bipartite graph.
- [x] **Engineering applications** to remember: supply chains, telecom routing, VLSI, smart grids, economic dispatch, KSRTC route planning.
- [x] **Drawings expected in exam**: transportation network, Dijkstra trace table, VAM penalty table, MODI loop diagram.
- [x] **Final answer must always include** the optimal allocations $x_{ij}^{*}$, the minimum cost $Z^{*}$, and a one-line *practical interpretation* (e.g., "Factory 2 should ship 150 units to Warehouse 1 and 50 units to Warehouse 2, incurring a minimum cost of ₹550").

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assignment: Network Bandwidth Allocation using Dinic's Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Assignment: Network Bandwidth Allocation using Dinic's Algorithm # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Network Bandwidth Allocation? **Network Bandwidth Allocation** is a real-world application of the **Maximum Flow Problem** in weighted directed graphs. Given a communication network modeled as a flow network (a directed graph where each edge carries a non-negative *capacity*), the objective is to determine the **maximum rate (bandwidth)** at which data can be transmitted from a **source node** (e.g., server) to a **sink node** (e.g., client) without violating any edge's capacity constraint. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** A *flow network* is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with a source $s \in V$, a sink $t \in V$, and a capacity function $c: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$. A valid flow $f: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ must satisfy: > 1. **Capacity Constraint:** $f(u,v) \leq c(u,v)$ for every edge $(u,v) \in E$. > 2. **Flow Conservation:** $\sum_{w} f(v,w) - \sum_{u} f(u,v) = 0$ for all $v \neq s, t$. ## 1.2 What is Dinic's Algorithm? **Dinic's Algorithm** (1970, Yefim Dinitz) is a **polynomial-time algorithm** for computing the maximum flow in a flow network. It improves upon the Ford-Fulkerson method by using the concept of **Level Graphs** and **Blocking Flows**, achieving a time complexity of $O(V^2 \cdot E)$, which is significantly faster than the basic Ford-Fulkerson approach for dense graphs. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (PECST595 Module 1):** Dinic's algorithm is the prescribed advanced technique for max-flow problems. It is especially preferred in **computer networks, data center traffic engineering, and VLSI routing** because it terminates in $O(\sqrt{E})$ iterations for unit-capacity networks. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Water Pipeline System 💧 Imagine a **water treatment plant (source S)** sending water to a **city reservoir (sink T)** through a network of pipes (edges) of varying diameters (capacities). | Network Element | Real-World Analogy | |---|---| | **Server (S)** | Water treatment plant (pumping station) | | **Client (T)** | City reservoir (collection point) | | **Routers** | Junction valves/distribution nodes | | **Edge Capacity** | Pipe diameter (max water flow rate) | | **Edge Flow** | Current water throughput in that pipe | **Maximum bandwidth** = the maximum total water delivery rate from plant to reservoir, *simultaneously respecting every pipe's diameter limit*. Dinic's algorithm is like a smart supervisor who builds a "**level map**" (height of water at each junction) and then systematically saturates all reachable paths from low to high, only rebuilding the level map when no more water can be pushed — making the allocation provably optimal. ## 1.4 Why Dinic's Algorithm for Bandwidth Allocation? - **Guaranteed Termination:** Unlike naive Ford-Fulkerson (which can loop forever on irrational capacities), Dinic's runs in strict polynomial time. - **BFS-Based Level Construction:** BFS over a network of routers is *naturally* implementable in real communication protocols (e.g., OSPF-style shortest path trees). - **Blocking Flow Efficiency:** It pushes as much flow as possible per phase, reducing the number of iterations dramatically. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Algorithmic Architecture of Dinic's Algorithm Dinic's algorithm operates in **phases**. Each phase consists of two principal procedures: ### Phase A — BFS for Level Graph Construction 1. **Initialize:** Set $\text{level}[s] = 0$ and $\text{level}[v] = -1$ for all $v \neq s$. 2. **Traverse:** Perform a **Breadth-First Search** from $s$ in the **residual graph** $G_f$ (the current graph with remaining capacities). 3. **Assign:** For every node $v$ discovered, set $\text{level}[v] = \text{level}[u] + 1$ where $u$ is the parent in BFS. 4. **Termination Check:** If $t$ is unreachable (i.e., $\text{level}[t] = -1$), the **algorithm terminates** — max flow is achieved. > The **Level Graph** $G_L$ is the subgraph of $G_f$ containing only edges $(u, v)$ such that $\text{level}[v] = \text{level}[u] + 1$. ### Phase B — DFS for Blocking Flow 1. Repeatedly find **augmenting paths** from $s$ to $t$ using **iterative DFS**, *restricted* to edges in the level graph $G_L$. 2. Compute the **bottleneck** $\Delta = \min c_f(u,v)$ along the path. 3. **Augment** the flow by $\Delta$ and update the residual capacities. 4. Stop when no $s \to t$ path exists in $G_L$ (a **blocking flow** has been found). 5. **Repeat** Phase A. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step | Step | Engineering Rationale | |---|---| | **BFS Level Construction** | Creates a **shortest-path layering**, preventing infinite loops. A BFS layer ensures every augmenting path is a shortest residual path. | | **Level Graph Restriction** | Eliminates "cross edges" and "back edges" at the same level, ensuring DFS only moves *forward* in level — guaranteeing progress. | | **Blocking Flow** | Maximizes the flow pushed per phase. After one phase, no $s$–$t$ path of strictly increasing levels remains in the residual graph. | | **Phase Repetition** | Each phase either (a) finds blocking flow or (b) proves $t$ is unreachable. Total phases $\leq V - 1$. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is a **must-memorize** reference for KTU 2024 board examinations on max-flow topics. | **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Engineering Unit / Interpretation** | |---|---|---| | **Residual Capacity** | $c_f(u,v) = c(u,v) - f(u,v)$ | Remaining bandwidth on a link | | **Net Flow Out of $s$** | $\vert f \vert = \sum_{v} f(s,v) - \sum_{u} f(u,s)$ | Total bandwidth injected at source | | **Net Flow Into $t$** | $\vert f \vert = \sum_{u} f(u,t) - \sum_{w} f(t,w)$ | Total bandwidth received at sink | | **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem** | $\max \vert f \vert = \min_{S \subset V, s \in S, t \notin S} c(S, \bar{S})$ | Max bandwidth = min cut capacity (Gbps) | | **Dinic Time Complexity** | $O(V^2 \cdot E)$ | General dense graph | | **Dinic Time (Unit Cap.)** | $O(\min(V^{2/3}, \sqrt{E}) \cdot E)$ | All edges capacity = 1 | | **Bottleneck of Path** | $\Delta = \min_{(u,v) \in P} c_f(u,v)$ | Rate-limiting edge of augmenting path | | **BFS Level Assignment** | $\text{level}[v] = \text{level}[u] + 1$ | $u$ is BFS parent of $v$ | | **Augment Flow Update** | $f(u,v) \mathrel{+}= \Delta, \; f(v,u) \mathrel{-}= \Delta$ | Forward push + reverse cancel | | **Capacity Constraint** | $0 \leq f(u,v) \leq c(u,v)$ | Hard physical link limit | | **Flow Conservation** | $\sum_{(u,v) \in E} f(u,v) = \sum_{(v,w) \in E} f(v,w)$ | No leakage at intermediate routers | | **Termination Condition** | $\text{level}[t] = -1$ in BFS | Sink unreachable → OPTIMAL | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Dinic's algorithm and max-flow theory underpin mission-critical systems in: - **🌐 Telecom Backbone Engineering:** MPLS-TE tunnel capacity planning (Cisco, Juniper routers). - **☁️ Data Center Fabric:** AWS, Google Cloud use max-flow variants for *ECMP load balancing* and *east-west traffic shaping*. - **🛡️ Network Security:** Maximum flow models detect *botnet command exfiltration bandwidth* anomalies. - **🚚 Supply Chain & Logistics:** Cold-chain capacity allocation for time-sensitive goods (modeled identically). - **🖼️ Image Processing:** Graph-cut segmentation (Boykov-Kolmogorov, a max-flow variant) for medical image analysis. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Worked Assignment > [!IMPORTANT] > **Assignment Problem Statement (Network Bandwidth Allocation):** > A media server **S** must stream data to a client terminal **T** through two intermediate routers **A** and **B**. The available bandwidth (in Gbps) of each communication link is given below. Using **Dinic's algorithm**, determine the **maximum possible sustained bandwidth** from **S** to **T**, and identify the **bottleneck (minimum cut)**. **Network Topology (Edge Capacities in Gbps):** $$ \begin{aligned} c(S, A) &= 10 \\ c(S, B) &= 8 \\ c(A, B) &= 5 \\ c(A, T) &= 7 \\ c(B, T) &= 12 \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.1 Iteration 1 — Phase A: BFS Level Graph Construction **Residual Capacities (initially equal to $c$, no flow yet):** | Edge | Residual $c_f$ | |---|---| | $S \to A$ | 10 | | $S \to B$ | 8 | | $A \to B$ | 5 | | $A \to T$ | 7 | | $B \to T$ | 12 | **BFS Traversal from $S$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Queue} &= [S] \\ \text{Visit } S &: \text{level}[S] = 0; \text{enqueue neighbors} \\ & S \to A \text{ (residual 10 > 0)} \Rightarrow \text{level}[A] = 1 \\ & S \to B \text{ (residual 8 > 0)} \Rightarrow \text{level}[B] = 1 \\ \text{Queue} &= [A, B] \\ \text{Visit } A &: A \to B \text{ already at level 1, skip (no level increase)} \\ & A \to T \text{ (residual 7 > 0)} \Rightarrow \text{level}[T] = 2 \\ \text{Visit } B &: B \to T \text{ already at level 2, skip} \\ \text{BFS ends: } T \text{ reachable at level 2.} & \end{aligned} $$ **Level Graph $G_L$ (only edges advancing exactly one level):** $$ \begin{aligned} G_L = \{\, & (S, A): 10, \; (S, B): 8, \; (A, T): 7, \; (B, T): 12 \,\} \end{aligned} $$ > **Note:** The edge $(A, B)$ is **excluded** because both $A$ and $B$ are at level 1 — no level progression. --- ## 3.2 Iteration 1 — Phase B: DFS Blocking Flow We now find augmenting paths via DFS, always restricted to the level graph. **Augmenting Path 1:** $S \to A \to T$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta_1 &= \min\{\, c_f(S, A),\; c_f(A, T) \,\} \\ &= \min\{10,\; 7\} = 7 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ Update residual capacities: $$ \begin{aligned} c_f(S, A) &\leftarrow 10 - 7 = 3 \\ c_f(A, T) &\leftarrow 7 - 7 = 0 \\ c_f(A, S) &\leftarrow 0 + 7 = 7 \quad \text{(reverse edge)} \\ c_f(T, A) &\leftarrow 0 + 7 = 7 \end{aligned} $$ **Running Total Flow:** $f = 7$ Gbps. **Augmenting Path 2:** $S \to B \to T$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta_2 &= \min\{\, c_f(S, B),\; c_f(B, T) \,\} \\ &= \min\{8,\; 12\} = 8 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ Update residual capacities: $$ \begin{aligned} c_f(S, B) &\leftarrow 8 - 8 = 0 \\ c_f(B, T) &\leftarrow 12 - 8 = 4 \\ c_f(B, S) &\leftarrow 0 + 8 = 8 \\ c_f(T, B) &\leftarrow 0 + 8 = 8 \end{aligned} $$ **Running Total Flow:** $f = 7 + 8 = 15$ Gbps. **DFS terminates** — no more $S \to T$ path in $G_L$ (since $A \to T$ and $S \to B$ are both saturated at 0). **Blocking flow achieved for Iteration 1.** --- ## 3.3 Iteration 2 — Phase A: BFS Rebuild Level Graph **Updated Residual Capacities (relevant forward edges):** | Edge | Residual $c_f$ | |---|---| | $S \to A$ | 3 | | $S \to B$ | 0 | | $A \to B$ | 5 | | $A \to T$ | 0 | | $B \to T$ | 4 | **BFS Traversal from $S$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Visit } S &: \text{level}[S] = 0 \\ & S \to A \text{ (residual 3 > 0)} \Rightarrow \text{level}[A] = 1 \\ & S \to B \text{ (residual 0)} \Rightarrow \text{ skip} \\ \text{Visit } A &: \\ & A \to B \text{ (residual 5 > 0)} \Rightarrow \text{level}[B] = 2 \\ & A \to T \text{ (residual 0)} \Rightarrow \text{ skip} \\ \text{Visit } B &: \\ & B \to T \text{ (residual 4 > 0)} \Rightarrow \text{level}[T] = 3 \\ \text{BFS ends: } T \text{ reachable at level 3.} & \end{aligned} $$ **New Level Graph $G_L$:** $$ \begin{aligned} G_L = \{\, & (S, A): 3, \; (A, B): 5, \; (B, T): 4 \,\} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Iteration 2 — Phase B: DFS Blocking Flow **Augmenting Path:** $S \to A \to B \to T$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta_3 &= \min\{\, c_f(S, A),\; c_f(A, B),\; c_f(B, T) \,\} \\ &= \min\{3,\; 5,\; 4\} = 3 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ Update residual capacities: $$ \begin{aligned} c_f(S, A) &\leftarrow 3 - 3 = 0 \\ c_f(A, B) &\leftarrow 5 - 3 = 2 \\ c_f(B, T) &\leftarrow 4 - 3 = 1 \\ \text{Reverse edges updated accordingly.} & \end{aligned} $$ **Running Total Flow:** $f = 15 + 3 = 18$ Gbps. **DFS terminates** — no more $S \to T$ path in current $G_L$. --- ## 3.5 Iteration 3 — Phase A: BFS Final Check **Residual Capacities (forward edges only):** | Edge | Residual $c_f$ | |---|---| | $S \to A$ | 0 | | $S \to B$ | 0 | | $A \to B$ | 2 | | $A \to T$ | 0 | | $B \to T$ | 1 | **BFS from $S$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Visit } S &: S \to A \text{ (0)}, \; S \to B \text{ (0)} \Rightarrow \text{ no forward progress} \\ \text{level}[T] &\text{ remains } -1 \end{aligned} $$ **Sink $T$ is UNREACHABLE → Algorithm terminates.** --- ## 3.6 Final Result & Min-Cut Verification > [!IMPORTANT] > **MAXIMUM SUSTAINABLE BANDWIDTH: $f_{\max} = 18$ Gbps** **Verification via Min-Cut:** The set $S = \{S\}$ (source side) gives the cut crossing edges $(S, A)$ and $(S, B)$: $$ \begin{aligned} c(\{S\}, \{A, B, T\}) &= c(S, A) + c(S, B) \\ &= 10 + 8 = 18 \text{ Gbps} \end{aligned} $$ This **matches the max flow** $f_{\max} = 18$ Gbps, confirming the **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem**: $$ \max \vert f \vert = \min c(S, \bar{S}) = 18 \text{ Gbps} \quad \checkmark $$ **Final Flow Allocation Table:** | Edge | Flow (Gbps) | Capacity (Gbps) | Utilization | |---|---|---|---| | $S \to A$ | 10 | 10 | **100% (Saturated)** | | $S \to B$ | 8 | 8 | **100% (Saturated)** | | $A \to B$ | 3 | 5 | 60% | | $A \to T$ | 7 | 7 | **100% (Saturated)** | | $B \to T$ | 11 | 12 | 91.67% | > **Engineering Insight:** The **bottleneck cut** is the source node's outgoing links. To increase bandwidth, the network engineer must upgrade the physical fiber from **S** to either **A** or **B**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Original Network Flow Graph > The following Mermaid block renders the input network with all five directed edges and their bandwidth capacities. ```mermaid graph LR nodeS(("S
SERVER")) nodeA(("A
ROUTER")) nodeB(("B
ROUTER")) nodeT(("T
CLIENT")) nodeS -- "10 Gbps" --> nodeA nodeS -- "8 Gbps" --> nodeB nodeA -- "5 Gbps" --> nodeB nodeA -- "7 Gbps" --> nodeT nodeB -- "12 Gbps" --> nodeT style nodeS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style nodeT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style nodeA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style nodeB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Legend:** - 🟡 **Source S** (Server) — data origin - 🟢 **Sink T** (Client) — data destination - 🔵 **A, B** (Routers) — intermediate switching nodes ## 4.2 Algorithmic Flowchart of Dinic's Procedure > The block diagram below maps the **complete control flow** of Dinic's algorithm as applied to bandwidth allocation. ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["START: Read Network G and capacities c"] initA["Initialize flow f = 0
Build residual graph Gf"] bfsA["BFS from s in Gf
Assign level v for each node"] checkT{"Is sink t
reachable?
level t greater than -1"} stopA["STOP
Return max flow f
and min cut S"] buildLG["Construct Level Graph GL
Keep only edges level v minus level u equals 1"] dfsA["DFS in GL
Find augmenting path s to t"] pathExist{"Path
found?"} bottleneck["Compute bottleneck
Delta = min cf u v on path"] augment["Augment flow f by Delta
Update residual cf"] loopA["Repeat DFS
in same GL"] rebuild["Rebuild level graph
via BFS next iteration"] startA --> initA --> bfsA --> checkT checkT -- "NO t unreachable" --> stopA checkT -- "YES t reached" --> buildLG --> dfsA --> pathExist pathExist -- "NO blocking flow done" --> rebuild --> bfsA pathExist -- "YES" --> bottleneck --> augment --> loopA --> dfsA style startA fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style stopA fill:#90EE90,color:#000 style checkT fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style pathExist fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style bottleneck fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000 style augment fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Level Graph Evolution Across Iterations ```mermaid graph LR subgraph ITER1["ITERATION 1: BFS Levels - S=0, A=1, B=1, T=2"] L1S(("S=0")) L1A(("A=1")) L1B(("B=1")) L1T(("T=2")) L1S -- "10" --> L1A L1S -- "8" --> L1B L1A -- "7" --> L1T L1B -- "12" --> L1T end subgraph ITER2["ITERATION 2: BFS Levels - S=0, A=1, B=2, T=3"] L2S(("S=0")) L2A(("A=1")) L2B(("B=2")) L2T(("T=3")) L2S -- "3" --> L2A L2A -- "5" --> L2B L2B -- "4" --> L2T end ITER1 --> ITER2 style ITER1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333 style ITER2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333 style L1S fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style L1T fill:#90EE90,color:#000 style L2S fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style L2T fill:#90EE90,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** The level graph **restructures dynamically** as residual capacities change. Iteration 1 reaches $T$ in 2 hops; Iteration 2 requires 3 hops because direct $S \to B$ and $A \to T$ are saturated — the algorithm **adapts the routing topology in real time**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]** ### Q1. [3 Marks] **State and briefly explain the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem as applicable to network bandwidth allocation.** **Model Answer:** The Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem (Ford & Fulkerson, 1956) states that in any flow network, the **maximum possible flow** from source $s$ to sink $t$ equals the **minimum cut capacity** separating $s$ from $t$. Formally: $$ \max_{f} \vert f \vert = \min_{(S, \bar{S})} c(S, \bar{S}) $$ where the cut $(S, \bar{S})$ is a partition of vertices such that $s \in S$ and $t \in \bar{S}$, and the cut capacity is the sum of capacities of edges going from $S$ to $\bar{S}$. **Application to Bandwidth:** It guarantees that the maximum sustainable data rate is limited by the **smallest aggregate bandwidth bottleneck** in the network. To increase throughput, the network engineer must upgrade the link(s) in the minimum cut. > *(Examiner's Note: A diagram with source, sink, and a labeled cut is highly appreciated for full 3 marks.)* --- ### Q2. [3 Marks] **Differentiate between Ford-Fulkerson and Dinic's algorithm. Mention their time complexities.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Ford-Fulkerson | Dinic's Algorithm | |---|---|---| | **Path Selection** | Any augmenting path (often DFS) | Augmenting paths restricted to level graph | | **Iteration Strategy** | Edge-by-edge augmentation | Phase-wise blocking flow | | **Termination Guarantee** | Polynomial for integer capacities | Strictly polynomial for all capacities | | **Time Complexity** | $O(E \cdot f_{\max})$ | $O(V^2 \cdot E)$ | | **Worst Case** | Can be exponential | Polynomial | | **Data Structure** | Residual graph + augmenting paths | Residual graph + BFS level graph + DFS | Dinic's algorithm is **strictly superior** in worst-case performance due to the BFS-based level graph and blocking flow phases that minimize redundant work. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Choice-Based) > **[KTU University Exam – End Semester, Model Paper Style, Module 1]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a suitable network diagram, explain the **concept of a level graph** and **blocking flow** in Dinic's algorithm. Show that Dinic's algorithm terminates in at most $O(V)$ phases. **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the following flow network where the source is **S** and the sink is **T**. Edge capacities are: $c(S, A) = 6$, $c(S, B) = 8$, $c(A, B) = 3$, $c(A, T) = 6$, $c(B, T) = 10$. Apply **Dinic's algorithm** to find the **maximum flow** from $S$ to $T$ and identify the **minimum cut**. --- #### **Model Solution for Question A (a):** **1. Level Graph Definition:** A **Level Graph** $G_L = (V, E_L)$ is constructed from the residual graph $G_f$ using a BFS from the source $s$. It contains only those edges $(u, v) \in E_f$ for which: $$ \text{level}[v] = \text{level}[u] + 1 $$ This ensures every edge in $G_L$ represents a **single BFS-layer step forward**, eliminating cross-edges and back-edges within the same level. **2. Blocking Flow Definition:** A **blocking flow** is a flow in the level graph such that **every** path from $s$ to $t$ contains at least one **saturated** edge (i.e., an edge whose flow equals its residual capacity). In other words, the maximum number of disjoint augmenting paths from $s$ to $t$ in the level graph has been exploited. **3. Termination in $O(V)$ Phases:** > **Claim:** Dinic's algorithm requires at most $(V - 1)$ BFS-leveling phases. **Proof Sketch:** After each phase, the shortest path distance (in edges) from $s$ to $t$ in the residual graph **strictly increases by at least 1**. Since the longest possible simple path in a graph with $V$ vertices has length $V - 1$, the number of phases is bounded above by $V - 1$. Each phase runs in $O(E)$ using BFS + iterative DFS. Hence total time is $O(V \cdot E)$ with the augmented level-graph DFS variant yielding $O(V^2 \cdot E)$. $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Model Solution for Question A (b):** **Network:** $S \to A$: 6, $S \to B$: 8, $A \to B$: 3, $A \to T$: 6, $B \to T$: 10. ##### **Iteration 1:** **BFS Levels:** $S=0$, $A=1$, $B=1$, $T=2$ (both $A \to T$ and $B \to T$ advance to level 2). **Level Graph $G_L$:** $\{(S,A):6, (S,B):8, (A,T):6, (B,T):10\}$. **DFS Augmenting Paths:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Path 1: } & S \to A \to T, \quad \Delta_1 = \min(6, 6) = 6 \\ & c_f(S,A) = 0, \; c_f(A,T) = 0 \\ \text{Path 2: } & S \to B \to T, \quad \Delta_2 = \min(8, 10) = 8 \\ & c_f(S,B) = 0, \; c_f(B,T) = 2 \end{aligned} $$ **Flow after Iteration 1:** $f = 6 + 8 = 14$. ##### **Iteration 2:** **Updated Residuals:** $S \to A = 0$, $S \to B = 0$, $A \to B = 3$, $A \to T = 0$, $B \to T = 2$. **BFS from $S$:** Only $S$ explored; $A, B$ unreachable. **$T$ unreachable.** **Algorithm Terminates.** > **Maximum Flow: $f_{\max} = 14$** **Min-Cut Verification:** $$ \begin{aligned} S = \{S\}, \quad \bar{S} = \{A, B, T\} \\ c(S, \bar{S}) = c(S, A) + c(S, B) = 6 + 8 = 14 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Bottleneck:** The source's outgoing links form the minimum cut. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **BFS-based level construction phase** of Dinic's algorithm. Write the pseudo-code for building the level graph and prove that it correctly computes shortest augmenting path distances. **(b) [7 Marks]** For a network with vertices $\{S, A, B, C, T\}$ and edges: $S \to A$ (8), $S \to B$ (6), $A \to C$ (5), $A \to T$ (4), $B \to C$ (4), $C \to T$ (9), apply **Dinic's algorithm** to find the maximum flow from $S$ to $T$. --- #### **Model Solution for Question B (a):** **Pseudo-code for BFS Level Construction:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Function BuildLevelGraph}(G_f, s, t): \\ &\quad \text{level}[s] \leftarrow 0 \\ &\quad \text{For all } v \neq s: \text{level}[v] \leftarrow -1 \\ &\quad \text{Initialize empty queue } Q \leftarrow [s] \\ &\quad \text{While } Q \text{ is not empty:} \\ &\quad\quad u \leftarrow Q.\text{dequeue}() \\ &\quad\quad \text{For each edge } (u, v) \in E_f \text{ with } c_f(u, v) > 0: \\ &\quad\quad\quad \text{If } \text{level}[v] = -1: \\ &\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{level}[v] \leftarrow \text{level}[u] + 1 \\ &\quad\quad\quad\quad Q.\text{enqueue}(v) \\ &\quad \text{Return level array} \end{aligned} $$ **Correctness Proof (Shortest Distance):** BFS explores nodes in **non-decreasing order of distance** from $s$. When a node $v$ is first discovered via edge $(u, v)$, the path $s \leadsto u \to v$ has length $\text{level}[u] + 1$. Since BFS guarantees $u$ was discovered at the shortest possible distance, the path to $v$ is also shortest. The `level[v] = -1` check ensures each node is processed only once, preventing longer paths from overriding shorter ones. $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Model Solution for Question B (b):** **Network:** $S \to A$ (8), $S \to B$ (6), $A \to C$ (5), $A \to T$ (4), $B \to C$ (4), $C \to T$ (9). ##### **Iteration 1:** **BFS Levels:** $S=0$, $A=1$, $B=1$, $C=2$ (from $A$), $T=2$ (from $A$). Wait — recheck: $A \to C$ gives level[C]=2, $A \to T$ gives level[T]=2, $B \to C$ would attempt to set level[C] but already set. So **levels:** $S=0, A=1, B=1, C=2, T=2$. **Level Graph $G_L$:** $\{(S,A):8, (S,B):6, (A,C):5, (A,T):4, (B,C):4\}$. Note $(C,T)$ is excluded as both at level 2. **DFS Paths:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Path 1: } & S \to A \to T, \quad \Delta_1 = \min(8, 4) = 4 \\ & c_f(A,T) = 0, \; c_f(S,A) = 4 \\ \text{Path 2: } & S \to A \to C \to T, \quad \Delta_2 = \min(4, 5, 9) = 4 \\ & c_f(S,A) = 0, \; c_f(A,C) = 1, \; c_f(C,T) = 5 \\ \text{Path 3: } & S \to B \to C \to T, \quad \Delta_3 = \min(6, 4, 5) = 4 \\ & c_f(S,B) = 2, \; c_f(B,C) = 0, \; c_f(C,T) = 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Flow after Iteration 1:** $f = 4 + 4 + 4 = 12$. ##### **Iteration 2:** **Residuals:** $S \to A = 0$, $S \to B = 2$, $A \to C = 1$, $A \to T = 0$, $B \to C = 0$, $C \to T = 1$. **BFS from $S$:** $S \to A = 0$ (skip), $S \to B = 2$ → level[B]=1. From $B$: $B \to C = 0$ (skip). **$T$ unreachable.** > **Algorithm Terminates. Maximum Flow: $f_{\max} = 12$.** **Min-Cut Check:** $$ \begin{aligned} S = \{S, A\}, \quad \bar{S} = \{B, C, T\} \\ c(S, \bar{S}) = c(S, B) + c(A, C) + c(A, T) = 6 + 5 + 4 = 15 \end{aligned} $$ That doesn't match. Let me recompute the min cut correctly: Try $S = \{S, B\}$: cut edges = $c(S, A) + c(B, C) + c(B, T)$ — but $B \to T$ is not an edge. So cut = $8 + 4 = 12$. ✓ $$ \min \text{ cut} = c(\{S, B\}, \{A, C, T\}) = c(S, A) + c(B, C) = 8 + 4 = 12 \text{ Gbps} \quad \checkmark $$ > **Maximum Flow = Minimum Cut = 12 Gbps.** Bottleneck: edges $(S, A)$ and $(B, C)$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting reverse edges:** When augmenting flow, students often forget to update the *reverse* residual capacities. This breaks subsequent BFS traversals and yields incorrect final flow. **Penalty: −2 marks per occurrence.** > 2. **Including same-level edges in the level graph:** A frequent error is retaining $(A, B)$ when both are at level 1. The level graph must include **only** edges where level advances by **exactly** +1. > 3. **Premature termination:** Stopping after one BFS phase. Remember: you must **rebuild** the level graph and repeat BFS until $T$ becomes unreachable. > 4. **Not verifying the min-cut:** KTU examiners award bonus marks for explicit min-cut verification via the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem. Always end with a cut check. > 5. **Confusing capacity with flow:** Bottleneck is calculated using **residual capacities**, not original capacities. > 6. **Unit omission:** Always state the unit (Gbps, Mbps) — losing 0.5 marks is common. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize Before KTU Exam** - 🔹 **Dinic's Algorithm = BFS (level graph) + DFS (blocking flow)** — two-phase iteration. - 🔹 **Time Complexity:** $O(V^2 \cdot E)$ general, $O(\sqrt{E} \cdot E)$ for unit-capacity graphs. - 🔹 **Phases ≤ $V - 1$**, since shortest path length strictly increases each phase. - 🔹 **Termination Condition:** $\text{level}[t] = -1$ in BFS phase. - 🔹 **Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem:** $\max |f| = \min c(S, \bar{S})$ — the two values are **equal** at optimality. - 🔹 **Residual Capacity Formula:** $c_f(u, v) = c(u, v) - f(u, v)$; reverse edge always exists with $c_f(v, u) = f(u, v)$. - 🔹 **Bottleneck of augmenting path:** $\Delta = \min c_f(u, v)$ over path edges — this is the **rate-limiting factor**. - 🔹 **Flow Conservation:** At every intermediate router, *inflow = outflow* (Kirchhoff's current law analogy). - 🔹 **Augmentation Rule:** When pushing $\Delta$ along path $P$, increment $f(u,v)$ by $\Delta$ and decrement $f(v,u)$ by $\Delta$ for every $(u, v) \in P$. - 🔹 **Real-world use cases:** Data center fabric routing, MPLS-TE, VLSI layout, image segmentation, supply chain logistics. - 🔹 **Algorithmic advantage over Ford-Fulkerson:** Faster, deterministic polynomial bound, no infinite-loop pathologies. - 🔹 **Edge case alert:** In networks with parallel edges or self-loops, **always** reduce to a simple directed graph first. - 🔹 **Examiner tip:** Draw the **residual graph** explicitly after each augmentation — it earns 1–2 easy marks.

topics.title: Assignment: Logistics Optimization using Capacity Scaling Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Assignment: Logistics Optimization using Capacity Scaling Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Capacity Scaling Algorithm** is a maximum-flow algorithm introduced by Ahuja & Orlin (1981) that augments flow along paths whose bottleneck residual capacity is guaranteed to be at least a current scaling parameter **Δ**. Formally, given a flow network $G = (V, E)$ with source $s$, sink $t$ and integer edge capacities $c(u,v) \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$, the algorithm maintains a residual graph $G_f$ and restricts each augmentation to the **Δ-residual subgraph** $G_f(\Delta)$ — the subgraph induced by edges whose residual capacity satisfies $c_f(u,v) \geq \Delta$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Δ-Residual Graph (KTU Syllabus Definition):**  
> $G_f(\Delta) = \{(u,v) \in E : c_f(u,v) \geq \Delta\}$  
> where $c_f(u,v)$ is the residual capacity of edge $(u,v)$ under the current flow $f$.

The algorithm begins with $\Delta = 2^{\lfloor \log_2 C_{\max} \rfloor}$ (largest power of two not exceeding the maximum edge capacity $C_{\max}$) and repeatedly halves $\Delta$ until $\Delta = 0$, performing BFS/DFS-based augmentations within each scaling phase.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Logistics Dispatcher's View

Imagine you are a **logistics dispatcher** running a national freight network. Trucks (capacity) move boxes from a central factory ($s$) to retail destinations ($t$) through warehouses and hubs (intermediate vertices). Each road has a maximum truck-throughput per day (the edge capacity).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Naïve dispatchers send one truck at a time along any available route. Smart dispatchers prioritise **high-volume highways first** — if a road can carry 100 trucks, fill it up before bothering with small lanes. The Capacity Scaling Algorithm does exactly this: it ignores edges whose remaining capacity is "too small" (less than $\Delta$), focusing all effort on **high-capacity augmentations** before zooming into finer detail.

This focus on large augmentations is the key to the algorithm's polynomial-time guarantee: each scaling phase forces a *meaningful* amount of flow onto the network, eliminating the exponential blow-up that afflicts Ford–Fulkerson on adversarial integer networks.

## 1.3 Engineering Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range (Logistics Context) |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{\max}$ | Maximum edge capacity in the network | **500–50,000 units/day** |
| $U$ | Upper bound on edge capacities | $\lceil \log_2 C_{\max} \rceil$ |
| $\Delta$ | Current scaling threshold | $2^k$ for $k = \lfloor \log_2 C_{\max} \rfloor, \ldots, 0$ |
| $|E|, \vert V \vert$ | Edge / vertex count | **10–10,000** for mid-scale networks |

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Mechanism — Phase-by-Phase Logic

The capacity scaling algorithm executes a strict iterative recipe. Each "scaling phase" is bounded by the current $\Delta$, and the algorithm drops $\Delta$ by a factor of 2 once no augmenting path of bottleneck $\geq \Delta$ remains.

**Phase 0 — Initialization**
1. Identify $C_{\max} = \max_{(u,v)\in E} c(u,v)$.
2. Set $\Delta \leftarrow 2^{\lfloor \log_2 C_{\max} \rfloor}$ and flow $f \leftarrow 0$.

**Phase Loop — For each scaling threshold $\Delta$**

1. **Construct $\Delta$-residual graph** $G_f(\Delta)$ — include only edges whose residual capacity $\geq \Delta$.
2. **Search** for any augmenting path $P$ from $s$ to $t$ within $G_f(\Delta)$ using BFS/DFS.
3. **Compute bottleneck** $b(P) = \min_{(u,v) \in P} c_f(u,v)$.
4. **Augment** flow along $P$ by $b(P)$.
5. Update residual capacities: $c_f(u,v) \leftarrow c_f(u,v) - b(P)$ and $c_f(v,u) \leftarrow c_f(v,u) + b(P)$.
6. Repeat steps 2–5 until no $s$–$t$ path exists in $G_f(\Delta)$.
7. **Halve** $\Delta \leftarrow \Delta / 2$ and proceed to the next phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Termination Condition:** The loop terminates when $\Delta = 0$. Since all capacities are integers, $\Delta$ is always a non-negative power of 2, so the loop runs in $O(\log C_{\max})$ outer iterations.

## 2.2 The "Why" — Engineering Rationale Behind Each Step

- **Why start with the largest power of 2?** It guarantees the *first* augmentation moves a "geometrically large" quantity, ensuring each edge enters saturation or near-saturation in $O(1)$ phases.
- **Why halve $\Delta$ by 2 (not 3 or 10)?** Base-2 halving yields $\lceil \log_2 C_{\max} \rceil$ outer iterations while never requiring division or floating-point arithmetic on capacities. It is the simplest integer-friendly geometric decay.
- **Why restrict augmentations to $G_f(\Delta)$?** Augmenting along a path whose bottleneck is $\geq \Delta$ guarantees that at least one edge along the path reaches residual capacity $< \Delta$ (specifically the bottleneck edge). This bounds the *number of augmentations per phase* by $O(E)$, giving a strong overall complexity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Statement | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{\max} = \max_{(u,v) \in E} c(u,v)$ | Max edge capacity; determines initial $\Delta$. |
| 2 | $\Delta_0 = 2^{\lfloor \log_2 C_{\max} \rfloor}$ | Initial scaling threshold. |
| 3 | $G_f(\Delta) = \{(u,v) : c_f(u,v) \geq \Delta\}$ | $\Delta$-residual subgraph (active edges only). |
| 4 | $b(P) = \min_{(u,v) \in P} c_f(u,v)$ | Bottleneck capacity of augmenting path $P$. |
| 5 | $f'(u,v) = f(u,v) + b(P)$, $f'(v,u) = f(u,v) - b(P)$ | Flow update on forward / reverse edges. |
| 6 | $\lvert f^* \rvert = \min_{S \subset V, s \in S, t \notin S} c(S, \bar{S})$ | Max-flow min-cut theorem (correctness certificate). |
| 7 | Number of scaling phases | $\lfloor \log_2 C_{\max} \rfloor + 1 \in O(\log U)$ |
| 8 | Augmentations per phase | $\leq \lvert E \rvert$ (one edge saturates per augmentation). |
| 9 | Worst-case time complexity | $O(E^2 \log U)$ |
| 10 | With BFS-based path search | $O(E)$ per augmentation → $O(E^2 \log U)$ total |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Board Exam:** The complexity "E-squared log U" is a signature feature — always quote it when asked about Capacity Scaling. Comparing: Ford–Fulkerson = $O(E \cdot \lvert f^* \rvert)$ (pseudo-polynomial), Edmonds–Karp = $O(V E^2)$, Dinic's = $O(V^{2/3} E)$, Capacity Scaling = $O(E^2 \log U)$ (strongly polynomial in $U$ and $\lvert V \rvert$).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The capacity scaling paradigm powers:
- **Supply-chain logistics** (Amazon, DHL, FedEx route planners) — warehouses as nodes, truck capacity as edge weight.
- **Air-traffic flow management** — airspace sectors as nodes, hourly throughput as capacity.
- **Telecommunications bandwidth allocation** — switches as nodes, link capacity as edge weight.
- **Power-grid load dispatch** — substations as nodes, transformer MVA as capacity.
- **Pipeline gas-flow optimization** — pumping stations as nodes, cubic-meter-per-hour as capacity.

In every case, the **bottleneck-driven** nature of capacity scaling mimics how engineers think about high-throughput assets: clear the big arteries first, then refine the capillaries.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — A Logistics Network

Consider a parcel-delivery network modelled as a flow graph. The central **Sorting Hub** ($s$) ships parcels through two regional depots (**A**, **B**) to the city **Distribution Centre** ($t$). Capacities (parcels/hour) are:

$$
c(s,A) = 10, \quad c(s,B) = 8, \quad c(A,B) = 5, \quad c(A,t) = 7, \quad c(B,t) = 12
$$

We are to find the **maximum parcel throughput** using the Capacity Scaling Algorithm.

### 3.2 Initialization

$$
C_{\max} = \max\{10, 8, 5, 7, 12\} = 12
$$

$$
\Delta_0 = 2^{\lfloor \log_2 12 \rfloor} = 2^3 = 8
$$

Initial flow $f \equiv 0$, so initial residual capacity equals original capacity on every edge.

### 3.3 Phase 1 — $\Delta = 8$

Construct $G_f(8)$: retain only edges with $c_f \geq 8$.

$$
\text{Active edges: } (s,A)[10],\ (s,B)[8],\ (B,t)[12]
$$

Edges $(A,B)[5]$ and $(A,t)[7]$ are **excluded** (residual < 8).

Search for an $s$–$t$ path:
- $s \to A$: edge exists.
- From $A$, no outgoing edge $\geq 8$ (both excluded). Dead end.
- $s \to B \to t$: min(8, 12) = **8**. ✓ Path found: $P_1 = s \to B \to t$.

**Augment by bottleneck $b(P_1) = 8$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(s,B) &\leftarrow 0 + 8 = 8 \\
f(B,t) &\leftarrow 0 + 8 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

Update residual capacities after Phase 1 augmentation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_f(s,B) &= 8 - 8 = 0 \quad (\text{saturated}) \\
c_f(B,s) &= 0 + 8 = 8 \quad (\text{reverse edge opened}) \\
c_f(B,t) &= 12 - 8 = 4 \\
c_f(t,B) &= 0 + 8 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

Reconstruct $G_f(8)$: no edge out of $s$ has $c_f \geq 8$. **No more augmenting paths.** Halve: $\Delta \leftarrow 4$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cumulative flow after Phase 1:** $\lvert f \rvert = 8$ parcels/hour.

### 3.4 Phase 2 — $\Delta = 4$

Construct $G_f(4)$:

$$
\text{Active edges: } (s,A)[10],\ (B,t)[4],\ (A,t)[7],\ (A,B)[5]
$$

Plus reverse edges from previous augmentation: $(B,s)[8]$, $(t,B)[8]$ — these are also $\geq 4$, but we only traverse them as part of cycle-cancelling paths.

**Augmentation 1:** $P_2 = s \to A \to t$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b(P_2) &= \min(c_f(s,A),\, c_f(A,t)) = \min(10, 7) = 7 \\
f(s,A) &\leftarrow 0 + 7 = 7 \\
f(A,t) &\leftarrow 0 + 7 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

Updated residuals:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_f(s,A) &= 10 - 7 = 3 \\
c_f(A,s) &= 0 + 7 = 7 \\
c_f(A,t) &= 7 - 7 = 0 \quad (\text{saturated}) \\
c_f(t,A) &= 0 + 7 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Augmentation 2:** $P_3 = s \to A \to B \to t$

$$
\begin{aligned}
b(P_3) &= \min(c_f(s,A),\, c_f(A,B),\, c_f(B,t)) = \min(3, 5, 4) = 3 \\
f(s,A) &\leftarrow 7 + 3 = 10 \quad (\text{now saturated}) \\
f(A,B) &\leftarrow 0 + 3 = 3 \\
f(B,t) &\leftarrow 8 + 3 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

Updated residuals after Phase 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_f(s,A) &= 3 - 3 = 0 \quad (\text{saturated}) \\
c_f(A,B) &= 5 - 3 = 2 \\
c_f(B,t) &= 4 - 3 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Reconstruct $G_f(4)$: $s$ has no outgoing edge $\geq 4$. **Halve:** $\Delta \leftarrow 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cumulative flow after Phase 2:** $\lvert f \rvert = 8 + 7 + 3 = 18$ parcels/hour.

### 3.5 Phase 3 — $\Delta = 2$

Construct $G_f(2)$:

$$
\text{Active edges: } (A,B)[2],\ (A,s)[7],\ (B,s)[8],\ (B,A)[5],\ (t,A)[7],\ (t,B)[8]
$$

All edges from $s$ (forward direction) are saturated; only reverse edges return flow to $s$. **No forward $s$–$t$ path exists.** Halve: $\Delta \leftarrow 1$.

### 3.6 Phase 4 — $\Delta = 1$

Active edges with $c_f \geq 1$:

$$
(A,B)[2],\ (B,t)[1],\ \text{and all reverse edges from prior phases}
$$

From $s$: $c_f(s,A) = 0$, $c_f(s,B) = 0$. **No outgoing edge from $s$.** No augmenting path. Algorithm terminates.

### 3.7 Final Result & Verification via Min-Cut

**Maximum parcel throughput:** $\lvert f^* \rvert = 18$ parcels/hour.

**Min-cut verification:** Cut $S = \{s\}$, $\bar{S} = \{A, B, t\}$:

$$
c(S, \bar{S}) = c(s,A) + c(s,B) = 10 + 8 = 18 \ \checkmark
$$

By the Max-Flow Min-Cut Theorem, the maximum flow equals the minimum cut, confirming optimality.

### 3.8 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Phase | $\Delta$ | Augmenting Path | Bottleneck | Cumulative $\lvert f \rvert$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 8 | $s \to B \to t$ | 8 | 8 |
| 2 | 4 | $s \to A \to t$ | 7 | 15 |
| 2 | 4 | $s \to A \to B \to t$ | 3 | **18** |
| 3 | 2 | None | — | 18 |
| 4 | 1 | None | — | 18 |

### 3.9 Derivation of the $O(E^2 \log U)$ Bound

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \underbrace{\lceil \log_2 U \rceil}_{\text{scaling phases}} \times \underbrace{O(E)}_{\text{augmentations per phase}} \times \underbrace{O(E)}_{\text{BFS/DFS per aug.}} \\
&= O(E^2 \log U)
\end{aligned}
$$

The $O(E)$ augmentations per phase hold because each augmentation saturates at least one edge (the bottleneck), and an edge can only saturate once per phase. With $E$ edges, at most $E$ augmentations occur.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Input flow network G]) --> initCmax["Compute C_max = max edge capacity"]
    initCmax --> initDelta["Set Delta = 2^floor(log2 C_max)"]
    initDelta --> initFlow["Set flow f = 0 on all edges"]
    initFlow --> checkDelta{"Delta >= 1?"}
    checkDelta -- No --> done([Output: Maximum flow f*])
    checkDelta -- Yes --> buildResid["Build Delta-residual graph G_f(Delta)"]
    buildResid --> bfsStart["BFS/DFS from source s"]
    bfsStart --> pathFound{"Augmenting path s-t found?"}
    pathFound -- No --> halveDelta["Delta = Delta / 2"]
    halveDelta --> checkDelta
    pathFound -- Yes --> computeBottle["Compute bottleneck b(P) = min residual capacity on P"]
    computeBottle --> augment["Augment flow along P by b(P)"]
    augment --> updateResid["Update residual capacities: forward -= b, reverse += b"]
    updateResid --> bfsStart
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["Phase 1: Delta = 8"] --> a1["Augment s-B-t by 8"]
    a1 --> p2["Phase 2: Delta = 4"]

    a2 --> a3["Augment s-A-B-t by 3"]
    a3 --> p3["Phase 3: Delta = 2"]
    p3 --> na1["No augmenting path; halve Delta"]
    na1 --> p4["Phase 4: Delta = 1"]
    p4 --> na2["No augmenting path; terminate"]
    na2 --> final["Maximum flow = 18 parcels/hr"]
```
