# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q:** Define **dynamic graph connectivity**. List the three fundamental operations supported by a dynamic connectivity data structure.

**Model Answer:**
Dynamic graph connectivity is the problem of maintaining a graph that changes over time via edge insertions and deletions, while answering path-existence queries between pairs of vertices efficiently. The three fundamental operations are:

1. `insert(u, v)` — Add an edge between $u$ and $v$.
2. `delete(u, v)` — Remove the edge between $u$ and $v$.
3. `connected(u, v)` — Determine whether a path exists between $u$ and $v$ in the current graph.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**Q:** State the **spanning forest invariant** and explain why it is the foundation of dynamic connectivity algorithms.

**Model Answer:**
The spanning forest invariant states that two vertices $u$ and $v$ are connected in $G$ if and only if they are connected in any spanning forest $F$ of $G$. This invariant is foundational because maintaining a dynamic spanning forest is sufficient to answer all connectivity queries. Since trees are easier to maintain dynamically (via Link-Cut Trees or Euler Tour Trees), reducing the problem to forest maintenance yields efficient algorithms with $O(\log n)$ per operation.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model, Module 2]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Link-Cut Tree (LCT)** data structure. Describe the `access`, `link`, and `cut` operations with their time complexities.

**Model Solution:**

A Link-Cut Tree is a data structure that maintains a dynamic forest of rooted trees. It represents the forest using a decomposition into **preferred paths**, each of which is stored as a balanced binary search tree (splay tree) called an **auxiliary tree**. Every node in the forest belongs to exactly one preferred path.

**`access(x)` operation:**

The goal is to expose the path from $x$ to the root of its tree. Steps:

1. Splay $x$ to the root of its auxiliary tree.
2. Detach its right child (which represents the part of the tree below the preferred path).
3. Move upward through the preferred paths, splaying each ancestor and reassigning right children, until $x$ becomes the root of the entire represented tree.

**`link(u, v)` operation (joins two trees via edge $(u, v)$):**

1. Call `evert(u)` to make $u$ the root of its tree.
2. Call `access(v)` to expose the path from $v$ to its root.
3. Set the parent pointer of $v$'s root to $u$.

**`cut(u, v)` operation (removes edge $(u, v)$):**

1. Call `access(u)` followed by `access(v)`.
2. If $u$'s right child after `access(u)` is $v$, and $v$ has no left child, then the edge is a tree edge and can be cut by removing the parent-child link.

**Time Complexities:**
- `access(x)`: $O(\log n)$ amortized
- `link(u, v)`: $O(\log n)$ amortized
- `cut(u, v)`: $O(\log n)$ amortized

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Using LCT, design an algorithm to answer `connected(u, v)` in $O(\log n)$ time. Prove its correctness.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm:**

```
function connected(u, v):
    access(u)
    access(v)
    return (find_root(u) == v)
```

**Correctness Proof:**

*Step 1:* After `access(u)`, the preferred path from $u$ to the root of its tree is exposed, and $u$ becomes the root of its auxiliary tree.

*Step 2:* `access(v)` then re-roots the tree of $v$ at $v$ and exposes the path from $v$ to the new root.

*Step 3:* `find_root(u)` returns the deepest node on the path from $u$ to the root — which is the root of the represented tree after `access(v)`.

*Case 1 — $u$ and $v$ are connected:* Then $u$ and $v$ share a root. After `access(v)`, this root is $v$ (since $v$ is re-rooted). The path from $u$ to the root passes through $v$, so `find_root(u) = v`.

*Case 2 — $u$ and $v$ are not connected:* They lie in different trees. After `access(v)`, the root of $u$'s tree is unchanged. Since $v$ is not in $u$'s tree, `find_root(u) ≠ v`.

Hence `connected(u, v)` returns **TRUE** iff a path exists. Time complexity: two `access` calls + one `find_root`, each $O(\log n)$ amortized. Total: $O(\log n)$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Model, Module 2]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Holm-de Lichtenberg-Thorup (HDLT)** fully dynamic connectivity algorithm for general graphs. State its time complexity.

**Model Solution:**

The HDLT algorithm maintains a hierarchy of $\log_2 n$ spanning forests $F_0, F_1, \ldots, F_{\log n}$ where:

- $F_0$ is a spanning forest of the entire graph $G$.
- $F_i$ (for $i > 0$) is a spanning forest of a random subgraph sampled from $F_{i-1}$.

**Sampling:** Each edge in $F_{i-1}$ is independently included in $F_i$ with probability $p_i = n^{-a^i/\log n}$ for some constant $0 < a < 1$.

**Vertex Levels:** Every vertex $v$ has a level $\ell(v)$. An edge $(u, v)$ inherits level $\max(\ell(u), \ell(v))$.

**Operations:**

1. **Insertion:** Add the new edge to $F_0$. If it connects two previously separate trees, it is promoted upward through the levels, potentially being added to $F_1, F_2, \ldots$ until two levels already keep it in the same component.

2. **Deletion:** Mark the edge as deleted in $F_0$. If it is a non-tree edge, no further work. If it is a tree edge at level $i$, search for a replacement edge at level $i+1$ (a non-tree edge connecting the two split components). If found, replace. Otherwise, demote all edges incident to the affected vertices by one level and recurse.

**Time Complexity:**
- **Expected amortized time per operation:** $O(\log^2 n)$ (Monte Carlo).
- This is the breakthrough result for **fully dynamic general graph connectivity**.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare Link-Cut Trees, Euler Tour Trees, and HDLT in terms of scope, complexity, and use cases.

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Link-Cut Trees | Euler Tour Trees | HDLT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Graph Class** | Forests only | Forests only | General graphs |
| **Link/Cut Time** | $O(\log n)$ amortized | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log^2 n)$ expected |
| **Connectivity Query** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n / \log \log n)$ |
| **Implementation** | Complex (splay-based) | Simpler (Treap-based) | Highly complex (multi-level) |
| **Deterministic** | Yes | Yes | No (randomized) |
| **Key Use Case** | Network routing, dynamic trees | Representing forests as balanced BSTs | Social networks, dynamic graphs |
| **Space** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ expected |

**Summary:** Link-Cut Trees and Euler Tour Trees are efficient for **acyclic** dynamic graphs but cannot handle general graphs with cycles. HDLT extends dynamic connectivity to general graphs at the cost of higher time complexity and randomization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing *incremental* connectivity (insertions only, easier, $O(\alpha(n))$) with *fully dynamic* connectivity (both ops, harder). Read the question stem carefully.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing "Link-Cut Trees work for general graphs." They **only** work for **forests**. For general graphs, you must use HDLT or similar.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting that `access(x)` exposes the path but does **not** change the rooted structure permanently — only the preferred path decomposition changes.
> - **Mark Loss Alert:** In 14-mark answers, students often describe LCT operations *without* the amortized analysis (potential function). Examiners **deduct 2–3 marks** if amortized bounds are missing.
> - **Mark Loss Alert:** For HDLT, students frequently forget to mention the **sampling probability formula** and the **demotion procedure** — both are mandatory for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Dynamic Graph Connectivity:**

- **DGC** = Maintain graph under edge insertions/deletions + answer connectivity queries.
- Three variants: **Incremental**, **Decremental**, **Fully Dynamic**.
- The **spanning forest invariant** is the cornerstone of all DGC algorithms.
- **Link-Cut Trees (Sleator-Tarjan):** $O(\log n)$ amortized per operation; forests only; uses splay trees and preferred paths.
- **Euler Tour Trees:** $O(\log n)$ per operation; forests only; represent tree via Euler tour in a balanced BST.
- **HDLT Algorithm:** $O(\log^2 n)$ expected per operation for general graphs; randomized; uses multi-level sampling.
- **Key LCT operations to memorize:** `access`, `evert`, `link`, `cut`, `connected`, `find_root`.
- **Amortized analysis** uses a potential function based on splay tree sizes.
- **DGC applications:** Network routing, social networks, compiler optimization, dynamic VLSI.
- **Examiner hot topics:** LCT operation walk-through (must show amortized bound), HDLT algorithm overview (must include sampling + demotion).
- Always state whether the algorithm is **deterministic** vs **randomized** and whether it handles **forests** vs **general graphs**.
- **Time complexity hierarchy:** $O(\log n)$ < $O(\log^2 n)$ < $O(\sqrt{n}\log n)$ < $O(\sqrt{n})$ (best to worst for general graphs).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Connectivity Algorithms, Eulerian and Hamiltonian Paths
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Dynamic Connectivity Algorithms, Eulerian and Hamiltonian Paths # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Dynamic Connectivity ### Formal Definition **Dynamic Connectivity** is the problem of maintaining the connected components of a graph that is changing over time through the insertion and deletion of edges. It supports three fundamental operations on an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$: - `INSERT(u, v)`: Add edge $(u, v)$ to the graph. - `DELETE(u, v)`: Remove edge $(u, v)$ from the graph. - `CONNECTED(u, v)`: Determine whether vertices $u$ and $v$ currently lie in the same connected component. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECST595 M2):** > The *incremental* variant (insertions only) is solvable in **amortized $O(\alpha(n))$** per operation using **Union-Find**. The *fully dynamic* variant (insertions + deletions) requires sophisticated structures such as **Holm–de Lichtenberg–Thorup (HDLT)** with $O(\log^2 n)$ amortized per operation. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a social network where friendships form and break every second. Each person is a vertex; each friendship is an edge. When Alice and Bob become friends, the network merges their social circles. When they unfriend, their circles may split. **Dynamic connectivity** is the engine that answers the question *"Are Alice and Carol still in the same friend group?"* in real time. --- ## 1.2 Eulerian Paths and Circuits ### Formal Definition An **Eulerian Path** (or **Eulerian Trail**) in a connected graph is a trail that visits **every edge exactly once**. An **Eulerian Circuit** (or **Eulerian Cycle**) is an Eulerian Path that **starts and ends on the same vertex**. ### Conceptual Analogy Picture a postman who must deliver mail along every street of a city without retracing any street. If he can return to the post office, the city has an **Eulerian Circuit**; if he must end at a different depot, it has an **Eulerian Path**. Königsberg's seven bridges (1736) — where Leonhard Euler proved no such walk exists — is the historical origin of graph theory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Eulerian Theorems (KTU High-Yield):** > - A connected graph has an **Eulerian Circuit** iff every vertex has **even degree**. > - A connected graph has an **Eulerian Path (but no circuit)** iff it has **exactly 0 or exactly 2 vertices of odd degree**. > - The two odd-degree vertices (if any) must be the **endpoints** of the path. --- ## 1.3 Hamiltonian Paths ### Formal Definition A **Hamiltonian Path** is a path that visits **every vertex of the graph exactly once**. A **Hamiltonian Cycle** is a Hamiltonian Path that returns to the starting vertex, forming a cycle through all $n$ vertices. ### Conceptual Analogy Unlike the postman who retraces streets, think of a *traveling salesman* who wants to visit each of $n$ cities **exactly once**. The existence of such a route is the **Hamiltonian Path problem** — and unlike Eulerian problems, it is famously **NP-complete** to decide in general. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** > While Euler's theorem gives a **polynomial-time checkable** necessary and sufficient condition, no such clean characterization exists for Hamiltonicity. The best-known sufficient conditions are **Dirac's Theorem (1952)** and **Ore's Theorem (1960)**. --- ## 1.4 Summary Snapshot | Concept | Decision Complexity | Typical Algorithm | |---|---|---| | Eulerian Path/Circuit | $O(V + E)$ (Euler, 1736) | Hierholzer's Algorithm | | Hamiltonian Path | **NP-Complete** | Backtracking, Branch & Bound | | Dynamic Connectivity (incremental) | $O(\alpha(n))$ | Union-Find (DSU) | | Dynamic Connectivity (fully) | $O(\log^2 n)$ amortized | HDLT / Euler Tour Trees | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Dynamic Connectivity via Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) The **Disjoint Set Union (DSU)** data structure maintains a partition of vertices into disjoint sets under two operations: - `FIND(x)`: returns the representative (root) of the set containing $x$. - `UNION(x, y)`: merges the sets containing $x$ and $y$. ### Operational Steps 1. Each vertex begins as its own singleton set. 2. To check `CONNECTED(u, v)`, compare `FIND(u)` with `FIND(v)`. 3. To process `INSERT(u, v)`, perform `UNION(u, v)` — merges components lazily. 4. **Path Compression** flattens the tree during `FIND`, and **Union by Rank/Size** keeps trees shallow. ### Real-World Utility DSU powers: **Kruskal's MST**, **network percolation**, **image segmentation (union-find flood fill)**, **Kruskal-based maze generation**, **compiler equivalence classes**, and **online friend-suggestion systems**. --- ## 2.2 Eulerian Path — Theoretical Conditions Let $G = (V, E)$ be a connected undirected graph. Define the set of **odd-degree** vertices: $$O(G) = \{ v \in V \;\vert\; \deg(v) \text{ is odd} \}$$ The **Eulerian Theorem** (Euler, 1736) states: $$\text{Eulerian Circuit exists} \iff |O(G)| = 0$$ $$\text{Eulerian Path exists (non-circuit)} \iff |O(G)| = 2$$ ### For Directed Graphs - **Eulerian Circuit**: $\forall v \in V,\; \deg_{\text{in}}(v) = \deg_{\text{out}}(v)$, and the underlying undirected graph is connected. - **Eulerian Path**: Exactly one vertex $s$ with $\deg_{\text{out}}(s) = \deg_{\text{in}}(s) + 1$ (start), one vertex $t$ with $\deg_{\text{in}}(t) = \deg_{\text{out}}(t) + 1$ (end), and all others balanced. ### Real-World Utility Chinese Postman Problem, DNA fragment assembly (de Bruijn/Eulerian), circuit board drilling, road-snow plowing optimization, network routing with link-cost minimization. --- ## 2.3 Hamiltonian Path — Sufficient Conditions Although Hamiltonicity lacks a clean characterization, two classical **sufficient conditions** guarantee a Hamiltonian cycle: ### Dirac's Theorem (1952) For a simple graph $G$ with $n \geq 3$ vertices: $$\delta(G) \geq \frac{n}{2} \;\Rightarrow\; G \text{ has a Hamiltonian cycle}$$ where $\delta(G)$ is the **minimum degree** of $G$. ### Ore's Theorem (1960) For a simple graph $G$ with $n \geq 3$ vertices, if for every **pair of non-adjacent** vertices $u, v$: $$\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq n$$ then $G$ has a Hamiltonian cycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pitfall to Avoid:** Dirac's and Ore's conditions are **sufficient but NOT necessary**. Many Hamiltonian graphs violate them. ### Real-World Utility Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP), VLSI circuit design (wire routing), genome assembly, project scheduling, tournament scheduling, orbit calculations in astronomy. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Condition / Formula | Complexity | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Eulerian Circuit (undirected) | $\forall v \in V,\; \deg(v) \equiv 0 \pmod 2$ | Verified in $O(V + E)$ | | 2 | Eulerian Path (undirected) | $\vert O(G) \vert = 2$ | Verified in $O(V + E)$ | | 3 | Eulerian Circuit (directed) | $\forall v,\; \deg_{\text{in}}(v) = \deg_{\text{out}}(v)$ | Verified in $O(V + E)$ | | 4 | Handshaking Lemma | $\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2 \cdot \vert E \vert$ | $O(V)$ | | 5 | Hierholzer's Algorithm | Builds Eulerian circuit using DFS + stack | $O(V + E)$ | | 6 | Fleury's Algorithm | Greedy; never crosses a bridge unless forced | $O(E^2)$ worst case | | 7 | Dirac's Condition | $\delta(G) \geq n/2 \Rightarrow$ Hamiltonian | $O(V + E)$ to check | | 8 | Ore's Condition | $\forall (u,v) \notin E,\; \deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq n \Rightarrow$ Hamiltonian | $O(V^2)$ to check | | 9 | Union-Find w/ Compression + Rank | `UNION`, `FIND` amortized | $O(\alpha(n))$ | | 10 | HDLT Fully Dynamic | Insertion + deletion of edges | $O(\log^2 n)$ amortized | | 11 | Hamiltonian Path Decision | General case | **NP-Complete** | | 12 | Hamiltonian Cycle in DAG | Always solvable in $O(V + E)$ via topological order | Polynomial | > [!NOTE] > $\alpha(n)$ is the inverse Ackermann function — for all practical $n$ ($\leq$ universe size), $\alpha(n) \leq 5$, making DSU effectively **constant-time** per operation. --- ## 2.5 Why These Theorems Matter in Algorithm Design 1. **Eulerian problems** admit efficient constructive algorithms because the *necessary conditions* (degree parity) are also *sufficient*. 2. **Hamiltonian problems** remain intractable in general because no simple characterization is known — only sufficient conditions exist. 3. **Dynamic connectivity** bridges static graph theory with real-world streaming applications — a cornerstone of modern data structure design. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Proof of Euler's Theorem (Necessary Direction) ### Statement If a connected graph $G$ has an Eulerian Circuit, then every vertex has even degree. ### Proof Let $C$ be an Eulerian Circuit: $v_0 \to v_1 \to v_2 \to \cdots \to v_m = v_0$, visiting each edge exactly once. Pick any vertex $v_k$ appearing $k_{\text{visits}}$ times as an internal vertex of $C$ (excluding the start/end appearance). Each visit to $v_k$ consumes **exactly 2 incident edges** — one entering, one leaving. The final return to $v_0$ also consumes 2 edges. Therefore the total number of edges incident to $v_k$ is: $$\deg(v_k) = 2 \cdot k_{\text{visits}} \in 2\mathbb{Z}$$ This proves $\deg(v_k)$ is even for every $k$. $\blacksquare$ ### Sufficient Direction (Sketch) By **induction on the number of edges**. Base case: empty graph has an Eulerian Circuit (trivial). Inductive step: in a graph where all degrees are even and $\vert E \vert \geq 1$, start a maximal trail from any vertex. Because all degrees are even, the trail can never get "stuck" at a non-start vertex, so it must return to the start. If all edges are covered, done; otherwise, the unused edges form subgraphs where each vertex (in the subgraph + the boundary vertex on the cycle) still has even degree — apply induction and splice. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — Eulerian Path Construction (Hierholzer's Algorithm) **Graph $G$:** $$V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}, \quad E = \{AB, BC, CD, DA, AC, CE\}$$ ### Step 1: Degree Audit $$\deg(A) = 3,\; \deg(B) = 2,\; \deg(C) = 4,\; \deg(D) = 2,\; \deg(E) = 2$$ $$\text{Odd vertices: } O(G) = \{A\} \;\text{only — but we need 0 or 2, so NO Eulerian Path/Circuit.}$$ > Let us **add edge $AE$** to make $\deg(A) = 4$, $\deg(E) = 3$. Now $O(G) = \{E\}$, still odd. Add another edge — say $BD$. Final degrees: $A{=}4, B{=}3, C{=}4, D{=}3, E{=}2$. Now $O(G) = \{B, D\}$. **Eulerian Path from $B$ to $D$ exists.** ### Step 2: Apply Hierholzer Starting at $B$ Trail so far: $B \to A \to C \to B \to D \to C \to E \to D$. $$|E| = 8 \quad \text{(all 8 edges used)} \implies \text{Eulerian Path found.}$$ ### Step 3: Complexity $$\text{Hierholzer: } T(n) = O(V + E)$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Dirac's Theorem Application **Graph $G$** with $n = 6$ vertices: complete graph $K_6$ **minus** a perfect matching $\{(1,2), (3,4), (5,6)\}$. ### Step 1: Compute Minimum Degree Every vertex is missing exactly one neighbor, so: $$\deg(v) = (n - 1) - 1 = 4 \quad \forall v \in V$$ ### Step 2: Apply Dirac's Bound $$\delta(G) = 4 \geq \frac{n}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \quad \checkmark$$ ### Step 3: Conclusion $$G \text{ has a Hamiltonian cycle.}$$ **Explicit Hamiltonian cycle:** $$1 \to 3 \to 5 \to 2 \to 4 \to 6 \to 1$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Ore's Theorem Application **Graph $G$** with $n = 5$: cycle $C_5$ on vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. ### Step 1: List Non-Adjacent Pairs In $C_5$, vertex $i$ is adjacent to $(i-1) \bmod 5$ and $(i+1) \bmod 5$. Non-adjacent pairs: $$(1,3),\; (1,4),\; (2,4),\; (2,5),\; (3,5)$$ ### Step 2: Degree Sum Check In $C_5$, every vertex has degree 2. So: $$\deg(u) + \deg(v) = 2 + 2 = 4 < n = 5 \quad \text{Ore's condition FAILS.}$$ But $C_5$ itself is Hamiltonian (cycle: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 1$). This confirms **Ore's condition is sufficient, not necessary**. --- ## 3.5 Detailed Derivation — Handshaking Lemma The number of odd-degree vertices in any undirected graph is **always even**. ### Derivation $$\begin{aligned} \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) &= 2 \cdot \vert E \vert \\ \underbrace{\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v)}_{\text{even}} &= \underbrace{\sum_{v \in O(G)} \deg(v)}_{\text{odd-degree sum}} + \underbrace{\sum_{v \notin O(G)} \deg(v)}_{\text{even-degree sum}} \end{aligned}$$ Therefore the odd-degree sum must be even, forcing $|O(G)|$ to be even. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This lemma guarantees that if Eulerian Paths exist, they have either 0 or 2 odd-degree vertices — never 1, never 3. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Eulerian Path | Hamiltonian Path | |---|---|---| | Visits every **edge** | ✓ | (not required) | | Visits every **vertex** | (not required) | ✓ | | Closed-form criterion | Yes (parity) | No known criterion | | Decision complexity | Polynomial | NP-Complete | | Best algorithm | Hierholzer: $O(V+E)$ | Backtracking: $O(2^n \cdot n)$ | | Foundational year | 1736 (Euler) | 1859 (Hamilton) | | Famous historical problem | Königsberg Bridges | Icosian Game | | Sufficient condition | Degree parity check | Dirac / Ore theorems | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Dynamic Connectivity — Union-Find Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: n vertices isolated]) opInit["Make-Set for each vertex
parent x = x, rank x = 0"] stateDSU[Disjoint Set Union Forest] opInsert["INSERT edge u,v"] callFind1["FIND u returns rootU"] callFind2["FIND v returns rootV"] decisionRoot{"rootU = rootV ?"} opUnion["UNION by rank:
attach smaller tree under larger"] opConnected["CONNECTED u,v ?"] outputYes[Return TRUE] outputNo[Return FALSE] loopBack([Loop: next operation]) endA([End]) startA --> opInit --> stateDSU stateDSU --> opInsert opInsert --> callFind1 --> callFind2 --> decisionRoot decisionRoot -- Yes --> opConnected decisionRoot -- No --> opUnion --> stateDSU opConnected --> outputYes --> loopBack opConnected --> outputNo --> loopBack loopBack --> stateDSU loopBack --> endA ``` ## 4.2 Eulerian Circuit Verification Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD startB([Input: connected undirected graph G]) check1{"All vertices
degree >= 1 ?"} countOdd["Count odd-degree vertices
O G = v : deg v is odd"] decisionOdd{"vert O G vert = 0 ?"} circuitPath["Eulerian CIRCUIT exists"] decisionTwo{"vert O G vert = 2 ?"} pathPath["Eulerian PATH exists
Endpoints = the two odd vertices"] reject["NO Eulerian Path or Circuit"] hierAlg["Apply Hierholzer Algorithm
Build tour in O V plus E"] doneB([End]) startB --> check1 check1 -- No --> reject --> doneB check1 -- Yes --> countOdd --> decisionOdd decisionOdd -- Yes --> circuitPath --> hierAlg --> doneB decisionOdd -- No --> decisionTwo decisionTwo -- Yes --> pathPath --> hierAlg --> doneB decisionTwo -- No --> reject --> doneB ``` ## 4.3 Hamiltonian Path — Backtracking Search Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD startC([Input: graph G, n vertices]) init["pathList = empty
visited array = all false"] callBT["BACKTRACK vertex 0"] nodeCheck{"All vertices visited ?"} nodeTrue["Hamiltonian Path FOUND
Print pathList"] nodeFalse["No path in this branch
BACKTRACK unmark last vertex"] pickLoop["FOR each neighbor v of current vertex"] availCheck{"v not visited
AND edge exists ?"} markVisit["Mark v visited
Append v to pathList"] recurse["Recurse BACKTRACK v"] nodeDone([Done]) startC --> init --> callBT callBT --> nodeCheck nodeCheck -- Yes --> nodeTrue --> nodeDone nodeCheck -- No --> pickLoop --> availCheck availCheck -- Yes --> markVisit --> recurse availCheck -- No --> pickLoop recurse --> nodeCheck ``` ## 4.4 Eulerian vs. Hamiltonian — Conceptual Contrast Block ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BLK_EULER["EULERIAN DOMAIN"] eDef[Visit EVERY EDGE exactly once] eCrit[Degree Parity Criterion] eAlg[Hierholzer: O V plus E] eHard[Polynomial Time] end subgraph BLK_HAMIL["HAMILTONIAN DOMAIN"] hDef[Visit EVERY VERTEX exactly once] hCrit[Dirac or Ore Sufficient Only] hAlg[Backtracking: O 2 power n] hHard[NP Complete] end eDef --- hDef eCrit -.NO EQUIVALENT.-> hCrit eAlg -.NO EQUIVALENT.-> hAlg eHard -.NO EQUIVALENT.-> hHard ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** State and explain the necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of an Eulerian Circuit in a connected undirected graph. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Theorem (Euler, 1736):** A connected undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ contains an Eulerian Circuit if and only if every vertex of $G$ has an **even degree**. **Necessity (1 mark):** Suppose $C$ is an Eulerian Circuit. As the circuit passes through any vertex $v$ (other than start/end), it uses exactly 2 edges at $v$ — one entering, one leaving. Hence $\deg(v)$ is even. **Sufficiency (2 marks):** Proved by induction on $|E|$. Start a maximal trail from any vertex $v$. Since all degrees are even, the trail cannot get stuck elsewhere, so it must return to $v$. If all edges are covered, we have an Eulerian Circuit. Otherwise, an unvisited-edge subgraph still has all vertices of even degree (relative to it); apply induction and splice cycles into the main tour. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between an **Eulerian Path** and a **Hamiltonian Path** with one illustrative example each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Eulerian Path | Hamiltonian Path | |---|---|---| | Visits every **edge** exactly once | ✓ | ✗ | | Visits every **vertex** exactly once | ✗ | ✓ | | Existence criterion | Degree parity (polynomial check) | NP-Complete decision | | Example | In $K_5$: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1 \to 4 \to 5 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 5 \to 1$ traverses all 10 edges | In $K_4$: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4$ visits all 4 vertices | --- ## 📗 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper] **(a)** State Dirac's Theorem and Ore's Theorem for Hamiltonian cycles. Prove Dirac's Theorem. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the graph $G$ with vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and edges $\{(1,2), (2,3), (3,4), (4,5), (5,1), (1,3)\}$, determine whether $G$ contains: - (i) An Eulerian Circuit - (ii) An Eulerian Path (non-circuit) - (iii) A Hamiltonian Cycle Justify each answer using the appropriate theorem. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(a): Dirac's & Ore's Theorems + Proof of Dirac** **Statement of Dirac's Theorem (2 marks):** Let $G$ be a simple graph with $n \geq 3$ vertices. If $\delta(G) \geq n/2$, then $G$ contains a Hamiltonian cycle. **Statement of Ore's Theorem (2 marks):** Let $G$ be a simple graph with $n \geq 3$ vertices. If for every pair of non-adjacent vertices $u, v \in V$: $$\deg(u) + \deg(v) \geq n$$ then $G$ contains a Hamiltonian cycle. **Proof of Dirac's Theorem (3 marks):** We prove the contrapositive: assume $G$ has **no** Hamiltonian cycle and show $\delta(G) < n/2$. Since $G$ is not Hamiltonian, there exists a longest path $P = v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k$ in $G$ where $k \leq n$. By maximality, **all neighbors of $v_1$ and $v_k$ lie on $P$**. Suppose $v_1$ is adjacent to $v_i$ for some $i \geq 2$. Then to avoid a Hamiltonian cycle, $v_{i-1}$ **cannot** be adjacent to $v_k$ (otherwise we could form the cycle). Thus: $$\begin{aligned} \deg(v_1) + \deg(v_k) &\leq k - 1 + k - 2 \\ &= 2k - 3 \\ &\leq 2n - 3 \end{aligned}$$ By Handshaking-adjacency bound, if $\deg(v_1) + \deg(v_k) \geq n$ then we get a contradiction. For Dirac's bound, note: $$\deg(v_1) + \deg(v_k) \leq (k-1) + (k-1) = 2k - 2 \leq 2n - 2$$ Hence both endpoints have degree at most $k - 1 \leq n - 1$. More carefully, since each endpoint sees only vertices on $P$: $$\deg(v_1) \leq k - 1, \quad \deg(v_k) \leq k - 1$$ So: $$\delta(G) \leq \deg(v_1) \leq k - 1 < \frac{n}{2}$$ which gives the contrapositive. $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Model Solution to Q.A(b): Graph Analysis** **Step 1 — Degree Computation (2 marks):** $$\deg(1) = 3, \quad \deg(2) = 2, \quad \deg(3) = 3, \quad \deg(4) = 2, \quad \deg(5) = 2$$ **Step 2 — Eulerian Circuit Check (2 marks):** $\deg(1)$ and $\deg(3)$ are odd. Number of odd-degree vertices = 2 ≠ 0. $$\boxed{\text{No Eulerian Circuit}}$$ **Step 3 — Eulerian Path Check (1 mark):** $|O(G)| = 2$ (vertices 1 and 3 are odd). The graph is connected. $$\boxed{\text{Eulerian Path exists: } 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1 \to 5 \to 4 \to 3}$$ **Step 4 — Hamiltonian Cycle Check (2 marks):** Try $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 1$. Check each edge: - $(1,2)$ ✓, $(2,3)$ ✓, $(3,4)$ ✓, $(4,5)$ ✓, $(5,1)$ ✓ $$\boxed{\text{Hamiltonian Cycle exists: } 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \to 1}$$ --- ### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] (Alternative Choice) **(a)** Explain the **Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)** data structure with the optimizations **Path Compression** and **Union by Rank**. Prove that with both optimizations, any sequence of $m$ operations on $n$ elements takes $O(m \cdot \alpha(n))$ time. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the incremental dynamic connectivity problem on the sequence of edge insertions below. Use **Union-Find** to process each insertion and answer the queries. Initial graph: vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, no edges. Insertions (in order): 1. `INSERT(1, 2)` 2. `INSERT(2, 3)` 3. `INSERT(4, 5)` 4. `QUERY CONNECTED(1, 3)` → ? 5. `INSERT(3, 4)` 6. `QUERY CONNECTED(1, 5)` → ? 7. `INSERT(1, 5)` (redundant?) Show the parent and rank arrays after each operation. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(a): Union-Find with Optimizations** **Data Structure (2 marks):** Maintain two arrays of size $n$: - `parent[v]`: parent of $v$ in the DSU forest; root if `parent[v] = v`. - `rank[v]`: an upper bound on the height of the tree rooted at $v$. **FIND with Path Compression (2 marks):** $$\text{FIND}(v) = \begin{cases} v & \text{if } \text{parent}[v] = v \\ \text{FIND}(\text{parent}[v]) & \text{otherwise, with parent[v] reassigned to root} \end{cases}$$ **UNION by Rank (1 mark):** Let $r_x = \text{FIND}(x)$, $r_y = \text{FIND}(y)$. - If $r_x = r_y$: already in same set. - If $\text{rank}[r_x] < \text{rank}[r_y]$: set $\text{parent}[r_x] = r_y$. - Else: set $\text{parent}[r_y] = r_x$; if ranks were equal, increment $\text{rank}[r_x]$. **Complexity Proof (2 marks — outline):** Define $\alpha(n)$ as the inverse Ackermann function. Tarjan (1975) showed via potential-function analysis that: - Each `FIND` with path compression flattens the tree, reducing future costs. - Union by rank guarantees tree height $\leq \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ without compression. - The amortized cost per operation is $\Theta(\alpha(n))$, which is $\leq 4$ for any $n < 2^{2^{2^{2^{2^{2}}}}}$ — effectively constant. $$\boxed{T(m, n) = O(m \cdot \alpha(n))}$$ --- #### **Model Solution to Q.B(b): Trace of DSU Operations** Initial: `parent = [0,1,2,3,4,5]`, `rank = [0,1,1,1,1,1]` (using 1-indexed). | Step | Operation | parent after | rank after | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `INSERT(1,2)` | `[0,1,1,3,4,5]` | `[0,1,1,1,1,1]` | rank 1 > rank 1 → 2 attaches to 1; tie, rank[1]↑ | | 2 | `INSERT(2,3)` | `[0,1,1,1,4,5]` | `[0,2,1,1,1,1]` | FIND(2)=1, FIND(3)=3; rank 2>1 → 3 under 1 | | 3 | `INSERT(4,5)` | `[0,1,1,1,4,4]` | `[0,2,1,1,2,1]` | FIND(4)=4, FIND(5)=5; rank 1<1 → tie, 5 under 4, rank[4]↑ | | 4 | `QUERY CONNECTED(1,3)` | (unchanged) | (unchanged) | FIND(1)=1, FIND(3)=1 → **TRUE** | | 5 | `INSERT(3,4)` | `[0,1,1,1,1,4]` | `[0,2,1,1,2,1]` | FIND(3)=1, FIND(4)=4; rank 2>2 → 4 attaches to 1 | | 6 | `QUERY CONNECTED(1,5)` | (unchanged) | (unchanged) | FIND(1)=1, FIND(5)=1 → **TRUE** | | 7 | `INSERT(1,5)` | `[0,1,1,1,1,4]` | `[0,2,1,1,2,1]` | FIND(1)=1, FIND(5)=1 → same set, **NO-OP** | **Final DSU Forest (representative 1):** $$\boxed{\text{All vertices } \{1,2,3,4,5\} \text{ belong to a single connected component.}}$$ --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Eulerian vs. Hamiltonian:** Many students write "Hamiltonian = visits every vertex" but forget to specify "exactly once." (−0.5 mark) > 2. **Forgetting connectivity:** Eulerian conditions require the graph to be **connected**. Applying them to disconnected components loses marks. (−1 mark) > 3. **Handshaking Lemma omission:** When asked to *prove* Euler's condition, students often skip the Handshaking Lemma role. Always mention it. (−0.5 mark) > 4. **Ore's vs. Dirac's:** These are NOT equivalent. Dirac uses *minimum degree*; Ore uses *sum of non-adjacent pair degrees*. Mixing them up is a frequent error. (−1 mark) > 5. **DSU Trace Errors:** Failing to update `rank` correctly during ties, or forgetting path compression recursion depth. (−0.5 mark per wrong step) > 6. **In Part B trace problems:** Skipping intermediate state snapshots. KTU examiners award partial marks only if intermediate parent/rank arrays are clearly shown. (−1 to −2 marks) --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Eulerian Circuit** ⇔ every vertex has **even degree** AND graph is connected. - ✅ **Eulerian Path** ⇔ exactly **0 or 2** vertices of odd degree AND graph is connected. - ✅ **Handshaking Lemma**: number of odd-degree vertices is **always even**. - ✅ **Hierholzer's Algorithm** constructs Eulerian circuits in $O(V + E)$. - ✅ **Fleury's Algorithm** is simpler but $O(E^2)$. - ✅ **Hamiltonian Cycle** decision is **NP-Complete** — no simple polynomial criterion. - ✅ **Dirac's Theorem**: $\delta(G) \geq n/2 \Rightarrow$ Hamiltonian cycle (sufficient). - ✅ **Ore's Theorem**: $\forall (u,v) \notin E,\; \deg(u)+\deg(v) \geq n \Rightarrow$ Hamiltonian cycle (sufficient). - ✅ **Union-Find** with path compression + union by rank gives $O(\alpha(n))$ amortized per operation. - ✅ **Fully dynamic connectivity** requires HDLT or Euler Tour Trees ($O(\log^2 n)$ amortized). - ✅ Always check **connectivity** before applying Eulerian conditions. - ✅ Always mention **Handshaking Lemma** in degree-parity proofs. - ✅ Distinguish **necessary & sufficient** (Euler) from **sufficient-only** (Dirac/Ore). - ✅ For trace problems, **show intermediate states** explicitly. - ✅ **DAG + Hamiltonian cycle** is polynomial ($O(V+E)$ via topological sort) — but general Hamiltonian cycle is NP-Complete. - ✅ Eulerian problems trace roots to **1736 Königsberg**; Hamiltonian to **1859 Icosian Game**.

topics.title: Applications: Real-Time Network Monitoring, Dynamic Route Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications: Real-Time Network Monitoring, Dynamic Route Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Graph (KTU Formal Definition)**
> A **Dynamic Graph** is a graph $G_t = (V_t, E_t, w_t)$ whose vertex set, edge set, and/or edge weight function evolve discretely or continuously with time $t$. The state at any moment is the result of a sequence of operations: $\texttt{Insert}$, $\texttt{Delete}$, and $\texttt{Update}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Time Network Monitoring (RTNM)**
> The continuous acquisition, aggregation, and algorithmic analysis of telemetry from a communication network modeled as a dynamic graph, with the explicit goal of detecting **link failures**, **congestion**, **routing loops**, and **topology anomalies** within bounded latency budgets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Route Planning (DRP)**
> The repeated computation of optimal (shortest, fastest, or least-cost) paths in a **time-dependent network** $G_t$, where edge traversal costs are functions of the departure time $\tau(u)$ at the tail node $u$. The planner must respond to live traffic, accidents, and road closures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time-Dependent Network**
> A directed graph $G = (V, E)$ coupled with a weight function $w: E \times \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{> 0}$ such that traversing edge $e = (u,v)$ departing at time $t$ costs $w(e, t)$ time units and arrival occurs at $t' = t + w(e, t)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

**Real-Time Network Monitoring** is analogous to a **city's air traffic control room**. The city is the **graph** (airports = vertices, flight routes = edges), and the radar feeds are the **telemetry stream** (edge weights, plane positions). The control room must *continuously* update a model of reality and re-plan routes when conditions change. Just as a controller cannot rerun the entire planning algorithm from scratch every second, RTNM systems use **incremental** and **dynamic graph algorithms** to update only the affected substructures.

**Dynamic Route Planning** is the experience of using **Google Maps during a thunderstorm**. The graph is the road network; the edge weights are *travel times* that change as accidents, signals, and traffic evolve. When the algorithm discovers that a previously optimal edge now has a 90-minute delay, it does *not* recompute the entire route — it triggers an **incremental update** that surgically repairs only the affected subpath.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

The following metrics govern the design of every RTNM and DRP system and are **bold-flagged** as they are board-favorite terminology:

- **Latency** $L$ — Time between an event (e.g., link failure) and the system's awareness. Target: **sub-second** for tier-1 ISPs.
- **Jitter** $J$ — Variance in consecutive update times. Critical for control-plane stability.
- **Throughput** $\Theta$ — Updates processed per second. Modern SDN controllers sustain $\Theta \geq 10^6$ updates/sec.
- **Query Time** $Q$ — Time to answer a shortest-path query after an update. State-of-the-art: $Q = O(\log n)$ via **Hub Labeling**.
- **Stretch** $\sigma$ — Ratio of query answer to true optimum. Approximate methods (e.g., Contraction Hierarchies) achieve $\sigma = 1$ exactly.
- **Convergence Time** $T_c$ — Time for routing protocol to stabilize post-failure. OSPF target: $T_c < 1\,\text{s}$.

## 1.4 Applications Taxonomy

| Domain | Graph Element | Edge Weight | Update Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| ISP Backbone | Routers / Optical links | Latency, Loss | 1–10 Hz |
| SDN Fabric | Switches / Virtual links | Bandwidth, Delay | 100–10 000 Hz |
| Urban Road Network | Intersections / Road segments | Travel time | 1 Hz |
| Airline Routing | Airports / Flight legs | Wind-adjusted time | Static + disruptions |
| Data Center | TOR / Leaf-Spine | Queueing delay | 10 000+ Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight** — A common board question maps a real-world system onto a graph. The correct mapping is: *vertices = entities* (routers, intersections), *edges = relationships* (links, roads), *weights = properties* (latency, travel time).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Dynamic Graph Model

A dynamic graph is manipulated by five primitive operations. Every dynamic algorithm must specify the **amortized** or **worst-case** cost per operation.

$$\texttt{Insert}(u, v, w), \quad \texttt{Delete}(u, v), \quad \texttt{Update}(u, v, w), \quad \texttt{Insert}(v), \quad \texttt{Delete}(v)$$

### 2.1.1 Algorithmic Regimes

- **Static (Batch)** — Re-run from scratch. Cost: $O(m + n \log n)$ per update using Dijkstra.
- **Incremental** — Only insertions and weight increases. Often simpler; e.g., **Dijkstra with lazy deletion** works.
- **Decremental** — Only deletions and weight decreases. Hardest case; weight decreases propagate **forward** through the graph.
- **Fully Dynamic** — Both directions. The gold standard for production systems.

### 2.1.2 The Lower-Bound Reality

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall** — A common misconception is that dynamic shortest paths can be maintained in $O(1)$ per update. The known worst-case lower bound is $\Omega(1)$ per operation, but **no known algorithm** achieves $O(1)$ worst-case for SSSP in general graphs. Fully dynamic SSSP remains one of the most challenging open problems in graph algorithms.

## 2.2 Real-Time Network Monitoring — Algorithmic Stack

The monitoring pipeline uses four layers of graph algorithms, each with distinct trade-offs:

1. **Topology Discovery** — Builds the graph from LLDP, BGP, or SDN flow tables. Uses **graph isomorphism** and **subgraph matching**.
2. **Connectivity Verification** — Maintains a **dynamic spanning forest** to answer "is $u$ still connected to $v$?" in $O(1)$ via **Link-Cut Trees (Sleator & Tarjan, 1983)**.
3. **Latency Tracking** — Maintains dynamic SSSP from each "monitoring vantage point" to detect **route flaps** and **micro-loops**.
4. **Anomaly Detection** — Runs **graph-stream algorithms** (e.g., Count-Min Sketch on edge degrees, spectral analysis on adjacency matrix) to flag DDoS patterns.

## 2.3 Dynamic Route Planning — Algorithmic Stack

Three algorithmic families dominate the DRP landscape:

- **Labeling Methods** — Pre-compute distance labels (Hub Labeling, Pruned Landmark Labeling). Query time $O(k)$ where $k$ is label size.
- **Hierarchical Methods** — Pre-compute a multi-level graph (Contraction Hierarchies, Customizable CH). Query time $O(\log n)$ or $O(1)$ with bucket queues.
- **Goal-Directed Methods** — Use heuristics to bias the search (A*, D* Lite, ALT). Excellent for robotics and online replanning.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Property | Notation & Units | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time-Dependent Weight | $w_e : E \times \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{> 0}$ | Time units (sec, min) | Edge cost varies with departure |
| Arrival-Time Recurrence | $\tau(v) = \min_{u \in \text{Pred}(v)} \bigl[\tau(u) + w_{(u,v)}(\tau(u))\bigr]$ | Time at vertex $v$ | Dynamic Dijkstra |
| FIFO (Non-Overtaking) | $t_1 \leq t_2 \;\Rightarrow\; t_1 + w_e(t_1) \leq t_2 + w_e(t_2)$ | Boolean condition | Guarantees Dijkstra correctness |
| D* Lite rhs-Value | $rhs(s) = 0, \quad rhs(v) = \min_{u \in \text{Pred}(v)} \bigl[g(u) + c(u,v)\bigr]$ | Reachable heuristic | Incremental replanning |
| D* Lite Priority Key | $k(v) = \bigl[\min(g(v), rhs(v)) + h(v, s_{\text{goal}}),\; \min(g(v), rhs(v))\bigr]$ | Heap key pair | Determines expansion order |
| MST Cut Property | $\forall S \subset V: \; e^* = \arg\min_{e \in \delta(S)} w(e) \in \text{MST}(G)$ | Cut $\delta(S)$ edges | Dynamic MST update |
| Hub Labeling Query | $d(u,v) = \min_{(h_u, h_v) \in L(u) \times L(v),\; h_u = h_v} \bigl[w(u,h_u) + w(h_v,v)\bigr]$ | Labels $L(u), L(v)$ | $O(k)$ shortest-path query |
| CH Query Bound | $Q_{\text{CH}} = O(\log n)$ worst-case | Hop count bounded | Hierarchical search |
| LCT Link Operation | $O(\log n)$ amortized per $\texttt{link}$, $\texttt{cut}$, $\texttt{find-root}$ | Splay tree cost | Dynamic connectivity |
| Convergence Target | $T_c < 1\,\text{s}$ for OSPF/IS-IS | Seconds | Post-failure reroute |

> [!NOTE]
> The notation $\vert x \vert$ in any future context will be rendered as $\vert x \vert$ using the **vert** macro; within tables we use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid markdown table breaking.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google Maps / Waze** — Pre-process a road network into a Contraction Hierarchy; respond to live traffic via **Customizable Contraction Hierarchies** (CCH) where traffic weights are applied in $O(n \log n)$ then queries run in $O(1)$ amortized.
- **Cisco / Juniper Routers** — Run **OSPF** with dynamic SSSP from each router; on link failure, recompute only affected sub-DAGs using **Partial SPF**.
- **SDN Controllers (ONOS, OpenDaylight)** — Maintain an in-memory dynamic graph of the entire fabric and stream **incremental graph updates** to north-bound applications.
- **Self-Driving Cars** — Use **D* Lite** or **Field D*** to replan in dynamic environments (moving obstacles) at 10 Hz with sub-10 ms latency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate** — Every algebraic step is expanded. No shortcut phrases are used. Multi-line math is wrapped in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks with single blank lines surrounding each display equation.

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-Dependent Shortest Path via FIFO

We start from a static Dijkstra-style recurrence and extend it to time-dependent edge weights. Let $G = (V, E)$ be a directed graph with time-dependent weight function $w_e(t)$. Define the earliest arrival time function $\tau : V \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0} \cup \{\infty\}$.

**Step 1.** Establish the boundary condition at the source $s$.

$$\tau(s) = 0$$

The text below explains: the planner is at the source at time zero; arrival is trivially zero.

**Step 2.** Express the earliest arrival at any other vertex $v$ as a minimization over its predecessors.

$$\tau(v) = \min_{u \in \text{Pred}(v)} \Bigl[\tau(u) + w_{(u,v)}\bigl(\tau(u)\bigr)\Bigr]$$

The text below explains: to reach $v$ as early as possible, choose the predecessor $u$ that, when departed from at time $\tau(u)$, yields the smallest total elapsed time. The weight is evaluated at the *departure* time, not at zero, because travel time depends on when we leave.

**Step 3.** Show that the standard "label-setting" property holds under FIFO.

Assume $\tau(u) \leq \tau(u')$ for two predecessors $u, u'$ of $v$. We want to prove that $u$ being settled first implies optimality. Under FIFO, departing earlier cannot result in later arrival.

$$\tau(u) \leq \tau(u') \;\Rightarrow\; \tau(u) + w_{(u,v)}\bigl(\tau(u)\bigr) \leq \tau(u') + w_{(u,v)}\bigl(\tau(u')\bigr)$$

**Step 4.** Combine the inequalities. If $u$ is the predecessor that minimizes Step 2, then no future relaxation can improve $\tau(v)$:

$$\tau(v) = \tau(u) + w_{(u,v)}\bigl(\tau(u)\bigr) \leq \tau(u') + w_{(u,v)}\bigl(\tau(u')\bigr)$$

**Step 5.** Therefore, the time-dependent Dijkstra procedure is **label-setting** (settled vertices are never re-relaxed) **if and only if** the FIFO property holds for all edges. This is the central correctness theorem.

$$\boxed{\;\text{TD-Dijkstra is correct} \;\iff\; \forall e, \forall t_1 \leq t_2:\; t_1 + w_e(t_1) \leq t_2 + w_e(t_2)\;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: D* Lite's rhs-Value Update Rule

D* Lite maintains, for each vertex $v$, two scalars: $g(v)$ (current best-known distance from start $s$) and $rhs(v)$ (one-step lookahead estimate). We derive how these evolve under an edge-weight change.

**Step 1.** Initial state at the goal $s_{\text{goal}}$.

$$g(s_{\text{goal}}) = \infty, \quad rhs(s_{\text{goal}}) = 0$$

The text below explains: the goal is "free" in a backward search; we run the algorithm in reverse from goal to start.

**Step 2.** For any other vertex $v$, $rhs(v)$ is the minimum over all in-edges.

$$rhs(v) = \min_{u \in \text{Pred}(v)} \bigl[g(u) + c(u,v)\bigr]$$

**Step 3.** A vertex $v$ is **consistent** when $g(v) = rhs(v)$; otherwise **inconsistent** and must be in the priority queue.

**Step 4.** Suppose an edge $(u, v)$ has its weight decreased from $c_{\text{old}}$ to $c_{\text{new}}$ with $c_{\text{new}} < c_{\text{old}}$. Compute the new $rhs(v)$.

$$rhs_{\text{new}}(v) = \min \Bigl[\, rhs_{\text{old}}(v),\; g(u) + c_{\text{new}}(u,v) \Bigr]$$

**Step 5.** Show when $v$ becomes inconsistent. Two cases:

- If $g(u) + c_{\text{new}}(u,v) < g(v)$, then $rhs_{\text{new}}(v) < g(v)$, hence $v$ becomes **overconsistent** ($rhs < g$).
- Otherwise $v$ remains consistent with possibly lower $rhs$.

**Step 6.** The priority key for the heap is recomputed.

$$k(v) = \Bigl[\min\bigl(g(v), rhs(v)\bigr) + h(v, s_{\text{start}}),\; \min\bigl(g(v), rhs(v)\bigr)\Bigr]$$

**Step 7.** Algorithm complexity: each edge weight change triggers at most one rhs-update, and each rhs-update costs $O(\log n)$ heap work. The **amortized cost per edge change** in a graph with $m$ edges and $n$ vertices is:

$$\boxed{\;T_{\text{D* Lite}} = O\bigl(m \log n\bigr)\;\text{ total},\; \text{amortized } O(\log n)\;\text{ per update}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Dynamic MST Edge Insertion via Cut Property

**Step 1.** Let $T$ be the current MST of $G = (V, E)$ with $n - 1$ edges. Insert a new edge $e = (u, v)$ with weight $w_e$.

**Step 2.** Adding $e$ to $T$ creates exactly one cycle $C$. Identify $C$ by running $\texttt{find-path}(u, v)$ in $T$ via DFS — cost $O(n)$ in the worst case, or $O(\log n)$ with LCT.

**Step 3.** To maintain the MST, replace the **maximum-weight edge** on $C$ if and only if it is strictly heavier than $e$.

$$e_{\text{replace}} = \arg\max_{e' \in C} w(e')$$

**Step 4.** Apply the cut property: consider the cut $\delta(S)$ where $S$ is the set of vertices reachable from $u$ in $T - e'$.

$$\text{If } w_e < w_{e_{\text{replace}}}: \quad T \leftarrow (T \setminus \{e_{\text{replace}}\}) \cup \{e\}$$

**Step 5.** Otherwise, $T$ is unchanged. The **Cut Property Theorem** guarantees this is correct because $e$ is the unique minimum-weight edge crossing the cut $\delta(S)$ iff it belongs to *some* MST, but we keep the existing one if $e$ is not lighter.

**Step 6.** Complexity with Link-Cut Trees: $O(\log n)$ amortized for both path query and edge replacement. The classical Holm–de Lichtenberg–Thorup algorithm achieves $O(\log^4 n)$ amortized for fully dynamic MST, beating the static $O(m \log \beta(m,n))$ of Chazelle.

$$\boxed{\;T_{\text{Dyn-MST}}(n) = O(\log^4 n)\;\text{ amortized per update (Holm et al., 2001)}\;}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection for KTU

| Application | Required Property | Best Algorithm | Query Time | Update Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Post-failure connectivity check | Connectivity under edge delete | Link-Cut Tree / ETT | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Internet routing (OSPF) | Single-source paths from one node | Dynamic SSSP (Dijkstra + decremental) | $O(m)$ rebuild | $O(m)$ |
| GPS navigation, vehicle | Goal-directed, replanning | D* Lite | $O(k \log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| City-scale map service | Many queries, moderate updates | Contraction Hierarchies | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Ride-hailing surge routing | Frequent weight updates | Customizable Contraction Hierarchies | $O(1)$ amortized | $O(n \log n)$ |
| ISP traffic engineering | All-pairs, link criticality | Hub Labeling (HL / PLL) | $O(k)$ | $O(n \log D)$ |
| Robot path planning | Continuous replanning, moving goal | Lifelong Planning A* (LPA*) | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| DDoS detection | Edge degree stream | Count-Min Sketch + Spanners | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a dynamic graph. List the three fundamental operations and illustrate with one real-time network monitoring example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A dynamic graph $G_t = (V_t, E_t, w_t)$ is a graph whose vertex set, edge set, or edge weights change over discrete time $t$.
- **Operations (1 mark):** $\texttt{Insert}(u,v,w)$, $\texttt{Delete}(u,v)$, $\texttt{Update}(u,v,w_{\text{new}})$.
- **Example (1 mark):** In an ISP backbone, when a fiber link between routers $R_1$ and $R_2$ fails, the operation $\texttt{Delete}(R_1, R_2)$ is issued; the monitoring system must immediately update its dynamic spanning forest and recompute affected shortest paths to re-route traffic.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the FIFO property in time-dependent networks. Why is it essential for dynamic Dijkstra?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** For every edge $e$ and times $t_1 \leq t_2$, $t_1 + w_e(t_1) \leq t_2 + w_e(t_2)$.
- **Explanation (1 mark):** A vehicle (or packet) that departs earlier cannot arrive later at the same destination — overtaking is impossible.
- **Significance (1 mark):** FIFO guarantees the **label-setting** property of dynamic Dijkstra: once a vertex is settled (extracted from the priority queue), its earliest-arrival label is final and never improved. Without FIFO, a "settled" vertex may need re-relaxation, and the algorithm becomes label-correcting with no termination guarantee in polynomial time.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mark-losing mistakes on this topic: (1) **Forgetting the FIFO assumption** when discussing dynamic shortest paths — examiners deduct 2 marks instantly. (2) **Confusing incremental vs decremental** with the *direction of weight change*. (3) Stating D* Lite complexity as $O(n \log n)$ per query — it is $O(\log n)$ amortized per update. (4) Failing to **map real-world entities to graph primitives** (vertex/edge/weight) — always begin with a clear mapping.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks] With a worked example, demonstrate how the D* Lite algorithm handles dynamic edge-weight changes during route replanning.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
Consider a navigation graph with five vertices representing intersections $A, B, C, D, E$ (goal). The robot starts at $A$ and must reach $E$. Initial edge costs:
- $c(A, B) = 4, \; c(A, C) = 2, \; c(B, D) = 3, \; c(C, D) = 1, \; c(D, E) = 5$.

**Step 2 — Backward Search Initialization (1 mark):**
D* Lite runs *backwards* from the goal $E$. Set $g(E) = \infty, \; rhs(E) = 0$. Insert $E$ into OPEN with key $k(E) = [0 + h(E, A), 0] = [7, 0]$ (using Manhattan heuristic $h$).

**Step 3 — First Expansion (1 mark):**
Pop $E$. Update $D$ and successors: $rhs(D) = g(E) + c(D, E) = 0 + 5 = 5$, so $D$ is inserted with key $k(D) = [5 + h(D, A), 5] = [7, 5]$.

**Step 4 — Continue to $A$ (1 mark):**
After full expansion, the path $A \to C \to D \to E$ is chosen with total cost $2 + 1 + 5 = 8$.

**Step 5 — Event: Edge Weight Change (1 mark):**
The edge $(C, D)$ becomes congested; its cost increases from $1$ to $10$. The robot detects this at time $t_1$.

**Step 6 — Update rhs-Values (1 mark):**
$rhs(D)$ is recomputed:
- $rhs_{\text{new}}(D) = \min\bigl[g(E) + c(D, E),\; g(C) + c_{\text{new}}(C, D)\bigr] = \min[5,\; 4 + 10] = 5$
- $rhs_{\text{new}}(C) = \min\bigl[g(A) + c(A, C),\; g(B) + c(B, C)\bigr] = 2$ (unchanged).

**Step 7 — Replanning (1 mark):**
Since $rhs(D)$ is unchanged, $D$ is still consistent. However, the path through $C$ is now suboptimal. D* Lite identifies that the path $A \to B \to D \to E$ with cost $4 + 3 + 5 = 12$ becomes the new optimum. The algorithm updates keys for $C$ and $B$ and pops the OPEN set only as far as needed — typically $O(k \log n)$ where $k$ is the number of affected vertices, **not the full graph**.

**Result:** The robot reroutes around the congested edge with **amortized $O(\log n)$** work per changed edge, not a full $O(n \log n)$ recomputation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of a Real-Time Network Monitoring system based on dynamic graphs. Include a discussion of the role of Link-Cut Trees and dynamic SSSP.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Physical Layer (1 mark):**
Network devices (routers, switches, optical cross-connects) emit telemetry (SNMP traps, sFlow, NetFlow, gNMI streams). These form the input edge events of the dynamic graph.

**Step 2 — Graph Abstraction Layer (1 mark):**
A **Dynamic Graph State Engine** maintains the current graph $G_t$ in memory. It exposes a transactional API: $\texttt{Insert}, \texttt{Delete}, \texttt{Update}$.

**Step 3 — Connectivity Layer using Link-Cut Trees (1 mark):**
For each pair of vantage points, a **Link-Cut Tree (LCT)** of Sleator and Tarjan maintains a dynamic spanning forest. The operations $\texttt{link}(u,v)$, $\texttt{cut}(u,v)$, and $\texttt{connected}(u,v)$ all run in $O(\log n)$ amortized time. This answers "is router $R_i$ still reachable from $R_j$?" in logarithmic time.

**Step 4 — Dynamic SSSP Layer (1 mark):**
For latency monitoring, a dynamic SSSP from each vantage point is maintained. When an edge weight changes, only the affected sub-DAG is relaxed. Modern implementations use **Dijkstra with potential functions** (the Thorup–Zwick approach) to limit work.

**Step 5 — Anomaly Detection (1 mark):**
Edge-degree streams feed a **Count-Min Sketch** to detect DDoS-flood signatures in $O(1)$ per packet. Spectral analysis on the adjacency matrix detects micro-loops.

**Step 6 — Decision and Actuation (1 mark):**
The Decision Layer correlates events (e.g., a link delete plus a simultaneous BGP route flap) and triggers either a human alert or an automated reroute via the SDN controller, which then issues flow-mod messages that themselves become $\texttt{Update}$ events on the graph.

**Step 7 — Feedback Loop (1 mark):**
The system is **closed-loop**: actuator commands (reroutes) are reflected back as graph updates, ensuring consistency between the monitored state and the controlled state. This is the essence of an **SDN control plane** in graph-theoretic terms.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast Incremental, Decremental, and Fully Dynamic shortest-path algorithms. Provide one real-time application for each.**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Incremental | Decremental | Fully Dynamic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Edge change allowed | Insert, weight increase | Delete, weight decrease | Both directions |
| Difficulty | Easier (lazy) | Harder (propagation) | Hardest |
| Classical algorithm | Dijkstra + lazy deletion | Even–Shilon $O(m \log n)$ total | Bernstein fully dynamic |
| Per-update cost | $O(\log n)$ amortized | $O(\log^2 n)$ amortized | $O(\log^4 n)$ amortized |
| Real-time application | Map with new road openings (1) | Network with link failure (1) | Live traffic-aware navigation (1) |

**Step 1 — Incremental (2 marks):**
When a new road is built and added to the city map, all pairwise distances can only *decrease* (or stay equal). The algorithm needs to relax only edges reachable from the new road. Application: **map services integrating newly constructed highways**.

**Step 2 — Decremental (2 marks):**
When a fiber-optic link fails in an ISP, the operator must rapidly detect all routing-table updates. The "weight" of the failed link is effectively $\infty$, but this is the *increase* direction in cost space; the symmetric case (link weight decrease) is the harder propagation. Application: **post-failure rerouting in OSPF**.

**Step 3 — Fully Dynamic (2 marks):**
GPS navigation in rush-hour traffic sees both increases (jam) and decreases (clearing). The algorithm must handle both seamlessly. The current state-of-the-art by Bernstein and others achieves $O(\log^2 n / \log\log n)$ amortized per operation in planar graphs and $O(m^{0.5+\varepsilon})$ worst-case in general graphs. Application: **ride-hailing dynamic dispatch**.

**Step 4 — Summary Comparison (1 mark):**
The three regimes form a strict difficulty ladder. The choice of algorithm in production depends on the **operational asymmetry** of the system: if 95% of changes are deletions, optimize for decremental; if both are common, invest in fully dynamic despite the higher constant factors.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain Contraction Hierarchies (CH) and Customizable Contraction Hierarchies (CCH) for real-time route planning. Why are they preferred for ride-hailing dispatch?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Contraction Hierarchies Concept (1 mark):**
A Contraction Hierarchy is a preprocessing technique that *orders* vertices by importance (e.g., road hierarchy) and *contracts* less-important vertices, shortcutting their incident edges. The result is a multi-level graph with **only upward and downward edges** in the hierarchy.

**Step 2 — Preprocessing (1 mark):**
We compute a **node ordering** $\pi$ (e.g., by edge-difference heuristic). Vertices are contracted in this order; each contraction either removes a vertex and adds shortcuts, or keeps it. Preprocessing time: $O(n \log n)$ with careful implementation.

**Step 3 — Query (1 mark):**
A bidirectional Dijkstra is run from source $s$ and target $t$, but only traversing **upward edges** from $s$ and **downward edges** to $t$. The two searches meet at a common "highest" vertex; the result is the shortest path. Query time: $O(\log n)$ average, often near-constant in practice.

**Step 4 — Customizable Contraction Hierarchies (1 mark):**
CCH (Dibbelt, Strasser, Wagner, 2016) **decouples** the topology preprocessing from the metric (weight) customization. The hierarchy $\pi$ is fixed; **only the metric** is updated when traffic changes. Customization runs in $O(n \log n)$ even for full metric updates.

**Step 5 — Why Preferred for Ride-Hailing (1 mark):**
Ride-hailing dispatch must answer **thousands of shortest-path queries per second** with **weights that change every few seconds** as traffic flows. CH requires $O(n \log n)$ re-customization on every weight change — too slow. CCH's separation allows topology to be amortized over many weight changes, yielding $O(1)$ amortized query time in production.

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (1 mark):**
For a 10-million-node city graph (e.g., Greater Bengaluru), CH preprocessing takes ~5 minutes; CCH allows weight customization in ~2 seconds; queries then run in ~50 microseconds. This throughput is what makes Uber/Ola-grade dispatch feasible.

**Step 7 — Trade-offs and Limitations (1 mark):**
CCH is not optimal for *adversarial* metric changes where the node ordering becomes invalid. In such cases, the system may need a partial re-ordering or fall back to hub-labeling-based methods. Additionally, CCH memory footprint is $O(n \log n + m)$ — manageable for city scale but expensive for planet-scale routing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Applications**

- [x] A **dynamic graph** $G_t = (V_t, E_t, w_t)$ supports five operations: insert-vertex, delete-vertex, insert-edge, delete-edge, update-weight.
- [x] **Real-Time Network Monitoring** uses dynamic graphs to track connectivity, latency, and anomalies in sub-second latency.
- [x] **Dynamic Route Planning** computes shortest paths in time-dependent networks where edge weights are functions of departure time $\tau(u)$.
- [x] The **FIFO property** $t_1 + w_e(t_1) \leq t_2 + w_e(t_2)$ for $t_1 \leq t_2$ is the correctness condition for time-dependent Dijkstra.
- [x] **D* Lite** maintains $g(v)$ and $rhs(v)$; an inconsistent vertex (where $g \neq rhs$) is in the priority queue. Amortized cost: $O(\log n)$ per edge change.
- [x] **Link-Cut Trees** (Sleator & Tarjan) provide $O(\log n)$ amortized $\texttt{link}, \texttt{cut}, \texttt{find-root}$ for dynamic connectivity.
- [x] **Dynamic MST** (Holm–de Lichtenberg–Thorup) achieves $O(\log^4 n)$ amortized per update.
- [x] **Contraction Hierarchies (CH)** preprocess in $O(n \log n)$, query in $O(\log n)$ average, but require $O(n \log n)$ re-customization on weight change.
- [x] **Customizable Contraction Hierarchies (CCH)** decouple topology from metric; customization in $O(n \log n)$, query in $O(1)$ amortized.
- [x] **Hub Labeling (HL)** precomputes distance labels; query in $O(k)$ where $k$ is the label size — best for APSP-heavy workloads.
- [x] Production systems: Google Maps uses CCH; OSPF uses partial SPF; SDN controllers use incremental SSSP.
- [x] **Examiner's three favorite traps**: forgetting FIFO assumption, misstating D* Lite complexity, confusing incremental vs decremental direction.
- [x] The lower bound for fully dynamic SSSP in general graphs remains open — the known best amortized bound is $O(\log^2 n / \log\log n)$ in restricted graph classes.
- [x] The arrival-time recurrence $\tau(v) = \min_{u} [\tau(u) + w_{(u,v)}(\tau(u))]$ is the **single most important equation** for this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
