topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Graph Coloring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Graph Coloring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **proper vertex coloring** of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is an assignment of colors to the vertices of $G$ such that no two **adjacent** vertices (i.e., vertices connected by an edge) share the same color.

Formally, a proper $k$-coloring is a function
$$f : V(G) \rightarrow \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, k\}$$
such that for every edge $(u, v) \in E(G)$, the condition $f(u) \neq f(v)$ holds strictly.

- A graph is called **$k$-colorable** if it admits at least one proper $k$-coloring.
- The **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$ is the **smallest integer $k$** for which $G$ admits a proper $k$-coloring.
- A graph with $\chi(G) = k$ is said to be **$k$-chromatic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Chromatic Number**
> - $\chi(G) = 1 \iff G$ is an **empty graph** (edgeless graph).
> - $\chi(G) = 2 \iff G$ is **bipartite** and non-empty.
> - $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$, where $\omega(G)$ is the **clique number** (size of the largest complete subgraph).
> - $\chi(G) \geq 3$ if $G$ contains any **odd cycle**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Insight)

Imagine you are the **registrar of a university** preparing an examination timetable. Each course is a vertex, and an edge between two courses means *at least one student is common to both* (a clash). You must assign exam **time slots** (colors) such that clashing courses never share a slot. The **minimum number of slots** required is exactly the **chromatic number** of this conflict graph.

The same idea governs:
- **Map coloring**: Countries sharing a border must be shaded differently.
- **Compiler register allocation**: Variables that are "live" simultaneously (interference) cannot share a CPU register.
- **Frequency assignment in wireless networks**: Adjacent cell towers must broadcast on non-overlapping frequencies.
- **Sudoku puzzles**: Each row, column, and 3×3 box is a clique — solving Sudoku is essentially a 9-coloring problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chromatic Polynomial $P(G, k)$

> [!NOTE]
> The **chromatic polynomial** $P(G, k)$ counts the **exact number of distinct proper $k$-colorings** of a graph $G$ (colorings that differ only by relabeling of colors are counted separately, which is why it is a polynomial in $k$).

### 2.1.1 The Deletion–Contraction Theorem (Foundational Tool)

For any edge $e \in E(G)$ that is **neither a loop nor a bridge**:
$$P(G, k) = P(G - e, k) - P(G / e, k)$$

- $G - e$ : delete edge $e$ from $G$.
- $G / e$ : contract edge $e$ (merge its two endpoints into a single vertex).

### 2.1.2 Closed-Form Chromatic Polynomials for Canonical Graphs

Let $n = \vert V(G) \vert$ and $m = \vert E(G) \vert$ (with no multiple edges).

| Graph Type | Notation | Chromatic Polynomial $P(G, k)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Empty graph (no edges) | $\overline{K_n}$ | $k^n$ |
| Complete graph | $K_n$ | $k(k-1)(k-2)\cdots(k-n+1)$ |
| Tree | $T_n$ | $k(k-1)^{n-1}$ |
| Cycle (even length) | $C_{2n}$ | $(k-1)^{2n} + (k-1)$ |
| Cycle (odd length) | $C_{2n+1}$ | $(k-1)^{2n+1} - (k-1)$ |
| Path | $P_n$ | $k(k-1)^{n-1}$ |
| Bipartite complete | $K_{m,n}$ | Explicit (computed via deletion–contraction) |

## 2.2 Fundamental Lower and Upper Bounds

| Bound | Statement | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Lower bound | $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$ | Every clique needs distinct colors |
| Lower bound | $\chi(G) \geq \lceil n / \alpha(G) \rceil$ | $\alpha$ = independence number |
| Upper bound | $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$ | Greedy coloring worst case |
| Upper bound | $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$ | **Brooks' Theorem** (with exceptions) |
| Edge-coloring | $\chi'(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$ | **Vizing's Theorem** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brooks' Theorem (1941)**
> If $G$ is a **connected** graph that is **neither** a complete graph $K_n$ **nor an odd cycle**, then
> $$\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$$
> where $\Delta(G)$ is the maximum vertex degree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vizing's Theorem (1964) — Edge Coloring**
> For any simple graph $G$, the chromatic index $\chi'(G)$ (minimum colors for a proper edge coloring) satisfies
> $$\Delta(G) \leq \chi'(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$$
> Graphs with $\chi'(G) = \Delta(G)$ are called **Class 1**; others are **Class 2**.

## 2.3 The Greedy Coloring Algorithm (Sequential Coloring)

The **Welsh–Powell** algorithm is a refinement of greedy coloring:

1. List all vertices in **non-increasing order of degree**.
2. Assign color **1** to the first vertex, then to every vertex not adjacent to it.
3. Repeat with color **2**, **3**, … until every vertex is colored.

For **DSATUR** (Degree of Saturation), at each step choose the vertex with the **largest number of distinctly colored neighbors** (ties broken arbitrarily), then assign it the **lowest legal color**.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Applications

- **VLSI design and register allocation** (Chaitin's algorithm).
- **Wireless channel assignment** in cellular networks.
- **Sudoku solver engines** (constraint propagation + 9-coloring).
- **Train/platform scheduling** in railway networks.
- **Frequency planning** in radio and satellite communication systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Chromatic Polynomial of a Path $P_n$

We claim that $P(P_n, k) = k(k-1)^{n-1}$.

**Step 1 — Base case.** For $n = 1$, a single vertex with no edges:
$$P(P_1, k) = k \quad \text{(no adjacency constraint)}$$

**Step 2 — Inductive hypothesis.** Assume $P(P_{n-1}, k) = k(k-1)^{n-2}$.

**Step 3 — Inductive step.** Add an $n$-th vertex connected to vertex $n-1$. The new vertex must take a color **different** from vertex $n-1$. Since the prior $n-1$ vertices have been colored, vertex $n-1$ uses exactly one color, leaving $k - 1$ legal choices for the new vertex:
$$P(P_n, k) = P(P_{n-1}, k) \cdot (k-1)$$

**Step 4 — Substitution.**
$$P(P_n, k) = k(k-1)^{n-2} \cdot (k-1) = k(k-1)^{n-1} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Chromatic Polynomial of a Cycle $C_n$ via Deletion–Contraction

**Step 1.** Take any edge $e = (v_1, v_n)$ of the cycle $C_n$. Apply the deletion–contraction identity:
$$P(C_n, k) = P(C_n - e, k) - P(C_n / e, k)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the two subgraphs.**
- $C_n - e$ becomes the path $P_n$, so
$$P(C_n - e, k) = k(k-1)^{n-1}$$

- $C_n / e$ collapses the cycle into a wheel with one merged vertex connected to **all** $n-2$ interior vertices, which is simply a **single vertex connected to a path of $n-2$ vertices** = the path $P_{n-1}$ (because the merged vertex is one of the path's endpoints):
$$P(C_n / e, k) = k(k-1)^{n-2}$$

**Step 3 — Combine.**
$$P(C_n, k) = k(k-1)^{n-1} - k(k-1)^{n-2}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $k(k-1)^{n-2}$.**
$$P(C_n, k) = k(k-1)^{n-2}\bigl[(k-1) - 1\bigr] = k(k-1)^{n-2}(k-2)$$

**Step 5 — Final elegant form.** Using $k-1$ as a substitution,
$$P(C_n, k) = (k-1)^n + (-1)^n (k-1) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Chromatic Polynomial of the Petersen Graph (via Properties)

The Petersen graph has 10 vertices, 15 edges, is 3-regular, and is famously non-Hamiltonian.

- It is **not bipartite** (contains 5-cycles).
- It is **not 2-colorable** ⇒ $\chi(G) \geq 3$.
- Welsh–Powell always succeeds with 3 colors on the Petersen graph ⇒ $\chi(G) = 3$.
- Therefore $P(\text{Petersen}, k)$ is a degree-10 polynomial with $P(1) = 0$, $P(2) = 0$, $P(3) = 12$ (12 distinct 3-colorings up to color permutation counted with multiplicity, divisible by $3!=6$, giving 2 essentially distinct 3-colorings).

> [!NOTE]
> The closed-form chromatic polynomial of the Petersen graph is:
> $$P(G_{\text{Petersen}}, k) = k(k-1)(k-2)(k^7 - 12k^6 + 67k^5 - 230k^4 + 529k^3 - 814k^2 + 775k - 352)$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Welsh–Powell Coloring of a Sample Graph

Consider the graph with vertices $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and edges
$\{AB, AC, AD, BC, CD, DE\}$ giving degrees $\deg(A)=3, \deg(B)=2, \deg(C)=3, \deg(D)=3, \deg(E)=1$.

| Step | Action | Coloring |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sort by degree: A(3), C(3), D(3), B(2), E(1) | — |
| 2 | Color 1 → A; skip neighbors B, C, D | $A=1$ |
| 3 | First uncolored = C. Color 2 → C; skip D | $C=2$ |
| 4 | First uncolored = D. Color 3 → D; skip A, C, E | $D=3$ |
| 5 | First uncolored = B (neighbor of A=1, C=2). Color 3 → B | $B=3$ |
| 6 | First uncolored = E (neighbor of D=3). Color 1 → E | $E=1$ |

Final 3-coloring: $A=1, C=2, D=3, B=3, E=1$. Hence $\chi(G) \leq 3$. Since the graph contains a triangle $\{A, C, D\}$, we have $\chi(G) \geq 3$, giving $\chi(G) = 3$.

---

## 3.5 Proof Sketch: Brooks' Theorem

**Claim:** If $G$ is connected, not $K_n$, and not an odd cycle, then $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$.

**Proof outline (induction on $\Delta$):**

**Step 1 — Base case.** $\Delta = 1$: graph is a matching, bipartite, so $\chi = 2 \leq \Delta+1$, but here $\Delta = 1$ implies edgeless matching ⇒ $\chi = 1 \leq 1$. ✓

**Step 2 — Inductive step.** Assume true for all graphs with max degree $< \Delta$. For a vertex $v$ of degree $\Delta$, by induction the graph $G - v$ satisfies $\chi(G-v) \leq \Delta$ (or $\leq \Delta - 1$ if $G - v$ is $K_\Delta$ or odd cycle — handled separately).

**Step 3 — Reinserting $v$.** The vertex $v$ has at most $\Delta$ neighbors. If $G - v$ uses at most $\Delta$ colors and the neighbors of $v$ do not use all $\Delta$ colors, assign $v$ the missing color. Otherwise, perform a **Kempe chain argument**: recolor the connected components of two color classes in $G - v$ to free up a color for $v$. This recoloring is always possible because $G$ is neither a complete graph nor an odd cycle (those are the exceptional cases). $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Graph Coloring Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Graph G=V,E] --> B[Compute Degree Sequence]
    B --> C{Degree > Threshold?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply Welsh-Powell Ordering]
    C -- No --> E[Use Natural Vertex Order]
    D --> F[Greedy Color Assignment Loop]
    E --> F
    F --> G{All Vertices Colored?}
    G -- No --> H[Pick Next Vertex in Order]
    H --> I[Select Lowest Legal Color]
    I --> J[Check Adjacency Constraint]
    J -- Conflict --> I
    J -- OK --> K[Assign Color]
    K --> F
    G -- Yes --> L[Output Coloring + k]
    L --> M{Validate Chi Lower Bound}
    M -- Holds --> N[Done: chi G equals k]
    M -- Fails --> O[Increase k and Retry]
    O --> F
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Chromatic Polynomial via Deletion–Contraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Graph G with edge e] --> B{Is e a Loop?}
    B -- Yes --> C[P = 0]
    B -- No --> D{Is e a Bridge?}
    D -- Yes --> E[P G,k = P G-e,k times k-1]
    D -- No --> F[Compute G minus e]
    F --> G[Compute G contracted by e]
    G --> H[P G-e,k minus P G/e,k]
    H --> I{Base Graph Recognized?}
    I -- Tree --> J[P = k times k-1 sup n-1]
    I -- Cycle --> K[P = k-1 sup n plus -1 sup n times k-1]
    I -- Complete --> L[P = k times k-1 times ... k-n+1]
    I -- No --> M[Recurse on Subgraphs]
```

## 4.3 Architecture: DSATUR Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize: all vertices UNCOLORED] --> B[Compute initial saturation degrees = 0]
    B --> C[Pick vertex with MAX saturation, tie break by MAX degree]
    C --> D[Assign LOWEST legal color]
    D --> E[Update saturation degrees of neighbors]
    E --> F{Any UNCOLORED vertex left?}
    F -- Yes --> C
    F -- No --> G[Output chi G and coloring]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define the chromatic number of a graph. State Brooks' theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

The **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$ of a graph $G$ is the minimum number of colors required to color the vertices of $G$ such that no two adjacent vertices receive the same color.

> **Brooks' Theorem:** If $G$ is a connected graph that is neither a complete graph $K_n$ nor an odd cycle, then $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$, where $\Delta(G)$ is the maximum degree of any vertex in $G$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**What is a proper edge coloring? Define chromatic index and state Vizing's theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

A **proper edge coloring** assigns colors to edges such that no two edges sharing a common endpoint receive the same color. The **chromatic index** $\chi'(G)$ is the minimum number of colors needed for a proper edge coloring of $G$.

> **Vizing's Theorem:** For any simple graph $G$, $\Delta(G) \leq \chi'(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Define the chromatic polynomial $P(G, k)$ of a graph. Compute $P(G, k)$ for the cycle graph $C_4$ using the deletion–contraction method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the graph $G$ with $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and $E = \{12, 13, 24, 25, 35, 45\}$, find the chromatic number $\chi(G)$ using the Welsh–Powell algorithm. Justify why the obtained value is optimal. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Chromatic Polynomial of $C_4$:**

Pick an edge $e = (v_1, v_4)$ of the 4-cycle. By deletion–contraction:

$$P(C_4, k) = P(C_4 - e, k) - P(C_4 / e, k)$$

- $C_4 - e$ is the path $P_4$:
$$P(P_4, k) = k(k-1)^3$$

- $C_4 / e$ merges $v_1$ and $v_4$ into a single vertex of degree 3 (with self-loops on the merged vertex that do not affect coloring). The resulting graph is the star $K_{1,3}$ (a tree on 4 vertices):
$$P(K_{1,3}, k) = k(k-1)^3$$

Substituting:
$$P(C_4, k) = k(k-1)^3 - k(k-1)^3 = 0$$

Wait — this is **incorrect** because $C_4 - e$ is **not** $P_4$ in a different sense; let us re-examine.

> Actually $C_4$ minus one edge **is** the path $P_4$, and contracting an edge in $C_4$ gives $K_3$ (a triangle), since the 4-cycle shrinks to a 3-cycle. Let us redo:

- $C_4 - e = P_4$, so $P(P_4, k) = k(k-1)^3$.
- $C_4 / e = K_3$ (triangle), so $P(K_3, k) = k(k-1)(k-2)$.

Therefore:
$$P(C_4, k) = k(k-1)^3 - k(k-1)(k-2)$$

Factoring $k(k-1)$:
$$P(C_4, k) = k(k-1)\bigl[(k-1)^2 - (k-2)\bigr]$$

$$= k(k-1)\bigl[k^2 - 2k + 1 - k + 2\bigr] = k(k-1)(k^2 - 3k + 3)$$

**Verification:** The general cycle formula $(k-1)^n + (-1)^n (k-1)$ for $n=4$ gives:
$$(k-1)^4 + (k-1) = k^4 - 4k^3 + 6k^2 - 4k + 1 + k - 1 = k^4 - 4k^3 + 6k^2 - 3k$$

Expanding our answer:
$$k(k-1)(k^2 - 3k + 3) = (k^2 - k)(k^2 - 3k + 3) = k^4 - 3k^3 + 3k^2 - k^3 + 3k^2 - 3k = k^4 - 4k^3 + 6k^2 - 3k \checkmark$$

---

**(b) Welsh–Powell on the given graph:**

Step 1: Compute degrees.

| Vertex | Neighbors | Degree |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2, 3 | 2 |
| 2 | 1, 4, 5 | 3 |
| 3 | 1, 5 | 2 |
| 4 | 2, 5 | 2 |
| 5 | 2, 3, 4 | 3 |

Step 2: Sort vertices by non-increasing degree: 2 (3), 5 (3), 1 (2), 3 (2), 4 (2).

Step 3: Apply color classes.

- **Color 1:** Assign to vertex 2. Skip neighbors {1, 4, 5}. Remaining candidates: 3.
  - Assign color 1 to vertex 3 (not adjacent to 2). Skip neighbor {5}.
- **Color 2:** First uncolored = 5. Assign color 2 to 5. Skip neighbors {2, 3, 4}.
- **Color 3:** First uncolored = 1. Assign color 3 to 1. Skip {2, 3}.
- **Color 3:** Vertex 4 is uncolored. Assign color 3 to 4 (neighbors are 2, 5 — neither has color 3). ✓

Final coloring: $1 \to 3, \ 2 \to 1, \ 3 \to 1, \ 4 \to 3, \ 5 \to 2$.

This uses **3 colors**, so $\chi(G) \leq 3$.

**Optimality check:** The graph contains the triangle $\{2, 4, 5\}$ — verify: edges 24 ✓, 25 ✓, 45 ✓. Any triangle needs 3 distinct colors, so $\chi(G) \geq 3$.

Therefore $\chi(G) = 3$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** State and prove the deletion–contraction theorem for chromatic polynomials. Use it to derive the chromatic polynomial of a tree with $n$ vertices. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the DSATUR algorithm for graph coloring. Apply it step-by-step to the Petersen graph and determine $\chi(G)$. Justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Deletion–Contraction Theorem:**

> **Statement:** For any edge $e$ of a graph $G$ that is neither a loop nor a bridge,
> $$P(G, k) = P(G - e, k) - P(G / e, k)$$

**Proof:** Count proper $k$-colorings of $G - e$. Any such coloring falls into exactly one of two categories:

- **Category A (proper for $G$):** The two endpoints of $e$ have different colors. This set of colorings is in bijection with proper $k$-colorings of $G$ itself. Their count is $P(G, k)$.

- **Category B (improper for $G$):** The two endpoints of $e$ have the same color. Merging them (contraction) yields a proper $k$-coloring of $G / e$. This set has cardinality $P(G / e, k)$.

Since the two categories partition the colorings of $G - e$:
$$P(G - e, k) = P(G, k) + P(G / e, k)$$
$$\Rightarrow P(G, k) = P(G - e, k) - P(G / e, k) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Derivation for a tree $T_n$:**

We use induction on the number of edges $m$. A tree on $n$ vertices has exactly $m = n - 1$ edges. Pick any edge $e$ of $T_n$. Since $T_n$ is a tree:

- $T_n - e$ splits into two smaller trees $T_a$ and $T_b$ with $a + b = n$.
- $T_n / e$ is a tree on $n - 1$ vertices (call it $T_{n-1}$).

By induction:
$$P(T_a, k) = k(k-1)^{a-1}, \quad P(T_b, k) = k(k-1)^{b-1}, \quad P(T_{n-1}, k) = k(k-1)^{n-2}$$

But the colorings of the disjoint union $T_a \cup T_b$ multiply:
$$P(T_n - e, k) = k(k-1)^{a-1} \cdot k(k-1)^{b-1} = k^2 (k-1)^{n-2}$$

Applying deletion–contraction:
$$P(T_n, k) = k^2(k-1)^{n-2} - k(k-1)^{n-2} = k(k-1)^{n-2}(k-1) = k(k-1)^{n-1} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**(b) DSATUR Algorithm on the Petersen graph:**

The Petersen graph $G_P$ is the well-known 3-regular graph on 10 vertices with 15 edges, girth 5.

**DSATUR steps:**

| Step | Vertex Chosen (max saturation) | Color Assigned | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Any vertex, say $v_1$ (sat=0, deg=3) | 1 | First vertex |
| 2 | $v_2$ neighbor of $v_1$ (sat=1) | 2 | Adjacent to color 1 |
| 3 | $v_3$ neighbor of $v_1, v_2$ (sat=2) | 3 | Adjacent to colors 1, 2 |
| 4 | Neighbor of $v_3$ with saturation 1 — pick vertex with max degree (all 3) | 1 | No neighbor has color 1 |
| 5–7 | Continue assigning colors 2, 3 to remaining saturation-2 vertices | — | Standard |

After processing all 10 vertices, DSATUR produces a valid **3-coloring**.

**Lower bound proof:** $G_P$ contains 5-cycles (e.g., the outer pentagon). A 5-cycle is an odd cycle, so $\chi(G_P) \geq 3$. Combined with the 3-coloring above, $\chi(G_P) = 3$. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**
> - In **deletion–contraction**, students often forget to check that the chosen edge is **not a bridge**. If $e$ is a bridge, the correct identity is $P(G, k) = \frac{1}{k} P(G - e, k)$ — marks lost for using the wrong formula.
> - For **Welsh–Powell**, students skip showing the **degree ordering step** explicitly. Examiners award partial marks only when the ordering is written out.
> - In **chromatic polynomial derivations**, do not forget to **substitute $k=1$** to verify $P(G,1) = 0$ for any graph with at least one edge. This is a quick sanity check examiners use.
> - Confusing **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$ (vertex coloring) with **chromatic index** $\chi'(G)$ (edge coloring) costs full marks on the relevant sub-question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- $\chi(G) = 1$ iff $G$ is edgeless; $\chi(G) = 2$ iff $G$ is bipartite and non-empty.
- $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$ — clique number is a lower bound.
- $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$ always; Brooks' theorem tightens this to $\leq \Delta$ for non-complete, non-odd-cycle graphs.
- The **deletion–contraction identity** $P(G, k) = P(G - e, k) - P(G / e, k)$ is the recursive backbone of chromatic polynomial computation.
- Chromatic polynomial **standard results to memorize**: $P(K_n, k) = \prod_{i=0}^{n-1}(k-i)$, $P(T_n, k) = k(k-1)^{n-1}$, $P(C_n, k) = (k-1)^n + (-1)^n (k-1)$.
- **Vizing's theorem**: edge chromatic index $\chi'(G) \in \{\Delta(G), \Delta(G)+1\}$.
- **Welsh–Powell** orders vertices by non-increasing degree; **DSATUR** orders by saturation degree (more refined).
- Graph coloring is **NP-hard** in general (the $k$-colorability decision problem is NP-complete for $k \geq 3$).
- The **Four Color Theorem** (Appel & Haken, 1976) states that every planar graph satisfies $\chi(G) \leq 4$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colorings for Special Classes of Graphs (Planar Graphs, Interval Graphs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Colorings for Special Classes of Graphs (Planar Graphs, Interval Graphs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Graph Coloring: The Foundation

A **proper vertex coloring** of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is an assignment $\varphi : V \rightarrow \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ such that for every edge $uv \in E$, we have $\varphi(u) \neq \varphi(v)$. The minimum number of colors required for a proper coloring is the **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$.

A graph $G$ is called **$k$-colorable** if $\chi(G) \leq k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> For a *special class* of graphs (such as planar or interval graphs), we exploit the structural restrictions of the class to derive tight upper bounds, exact formulas, and polynomial-time algorithms for the otherwise NP-hard general coloring problem.

---

## 1.2 Planar Graphs — Intuitive Overview

A graph is **planar** if it can be drawn in the plane with no two edges crossing. Famous planar graphs include $K_4$, the cube graph $Q_3$, and the dodecahedron.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy (Cartographer's Map):**
> Imagine you are a map maker drawing political boundaries of countries on a flat piece of paper. Your job is to color each country so that no two sharing a border receive the same color. This is *exactly* the Four Color Problem — the chromatic number of any planar map is at most **4**. Cartographers could always color maps with 4 colors, but proving it took until 1976 (Appel & Haken).

**Dual Formulation:** For a connected planar graph $G$ with $V$ vertices, $E$ edges, and $F$ faces, Euler's formula states:

$$V - E + F = 2$$

This combinatorial identity is the gateway to all planar coloring theorems.

---

## 1.3 Interval Graphs — Intuitive Overview

An **interval graph** $G$ is the intersection graph of a set of intervals on the real line. Each interval $I_i = [a_i, b_i]$ becomes a vertex $v_i$, and two vertices are adjacent iff their intervals overlap ($I_i \cap I_j \neq \emptyset$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy (Classroom Scheduling):**
> Suppose a university assigns classrooms to lectures. Each lecture has a *time interval* during which it occupies a room. Two lectures *conflict* (and need different classrooms) precisely when their time intervals overlap. Finding the minimum number of classrooms is *exactly* the chromatic number of the resulting interval graph. This is why interval graphs are central to **job scheduling, register allocation in compilers, and bioinformatics (DNA sequence analysis)**.

---

## 1.4 Why "Special Classes"?

| Class | General Graph Coloring | Special Result |
|---|---|---|
| Arbitrary graph | NP-hard | No polynomial algorithm (unless P = NP) |
| Bipartite graph | Polynomial | $\chi(G) = 2$ iff not empty |
| **Planar graph** | Polynomial (by 4CT) | $\chi(G) \leq 4$ |
| **Interval graph** | Polynomial | $\chi(G) = \omega(G)$ (clique number) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a question mentions "coloring" along with "planar" or "interval", you must invoke the *specific* theorem for that class — never the general greedy bound $\chi(G) \leq \Delta + 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Planar Graphs: Structural Theorems

### 2.1.1 The Four Color Theorem (4CT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Appel & Haken, 1976):** Every planar graph is **4-vertex-colorable**.
> $$\chi(G) \leq 4 \quad \text{for every planar graph } G.$$

The proof is computational (uses a discharging argument over ~1,936 unavoidable configurations) and is the first major theorem to be verified by computer.

### 2.1.2 The Five Color Theorem (Heawood, 1890)

A much simpler — and *exam-favorite* — predecessor of the 4CT:

> **Theorem:** Every planar graph is **5-vertex-colorable**, i.e., $\chi(G) \leq 5$.

**Proof Strategy (logical steps):**
- Every planar graph has a vertex $v$ of degree $\leq 5$ (by the handshaking corollary of Euler's formula: $\sum \deg(v) = 2E \leq 6V - 12 \Rightarrow \exists v : \deg(v) \leq 5$).
- Remove $v$, color the remaining graph with 5 colors (induction on $|V|$).
- Case (a): At most 4 colors appear among the neighbors of $v$ → color $v$ with the missing color.
- Case (b): $v$ has exactly 5 neighbors, all 5 colors used → apply **Kempe chain argument** along a 2-colored subgraph to free up a color for $v$.

### 2.1.3 Planar Edge Coloring

The **edge chromatic number** (or **chromatic index**) $\chi'(G)$ is the minimum number of colors needed to color edges so that no two incident edges share a color.

> **Vizing's Theorem (general):** For any simple graph,
> $$\Delta(G) \leq \chi'(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$$
> where $\Delta(G)$ is the maximum vertex degree.

> **Vizing's Planar Theorem (1965):** If $G$ is a simple planar graph, then
> $$\chi'(G) = \Delta(G) \quad \text{whenever } \Delta(G) \geq 8.$$

For $K_4$: $\Delta = 3$, but $\chi'(K_4) = 3$ (the edges can be paired into 3 perfect matchings).

---

## 2.2 Interval Graphs: Structural Theorems

### 2.2.1 Perfectness of Interval Graphs

> **Definition (Berge, 1960):** A graph $G$ is **perfect** if for every induced subgraph $H \subseteq G$,
> $$\chi(H) = \omega(H)$$
> where $\omega(H)$ is the clique number (size of the largest complete subgraph).

> **Theorem (Interval Graphs are Perfect):** Every interval graph $G$ is perfect. In particular,
> $$\chi(G) = \omega(G).$$

**Why is this true?** *Intuition:* Because intervals are linearly ordered on the real line, the structure is so rigid that "as many colors as needed" = "size of the most overlapping point." Every maximal clique corresponds to a *Helly point* — a real number $x$ contained in all intervals of the clique.

### 2.2.2 The Greedy Algorithm is Optimal on Interval Graphs

The standard **greedy coloring algorithm** (process vertices in some order, assign the smallest available color) runs in $O(n + m)$ but may use up to $\Delta + 1$ colors. For interval graphs:

> **Optimal Strategy:** Sort the intervals by their *left endpoint* $a_i$ (earliest first). Then run greedy. The number of colors used equals the **maximum number of intervals overlapping at any single point**, which is exactly $\omega(G) = \chi(G)$.

This gives a **polynomial-time optimal algorithm** for chromatic number on interval graphs.

### 2.2.3 Maximum Clique in Interval Graphs

The maximum clique can be computed in $O(n \log n)$ by a **sweep line**:
- Sweep a vertical line $x = t$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.
- At each $x = t$, the set of intervals covering $t$ forms a clique.
- The maximum size over all $t$ is $\omega(G)$.

Equivalently, $\omega(G) = \max_t \, \vert \{ i : a_i \leq t \leq b_i \} \vert$.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: The 5-Color Theorem

> **Goal:** Show that every simple planar graph $G$ on $n \geq 1$ vertices satisfies $\chi(G) \leq 5$.

**Step 1 — Establish a low-degree vertex via Euler's formula.**

Assume $G$ is connected (otherwise color each component separately). For a connected simple planar graph with $n \geq 3$:

$$E \leq 3n - 6$$

The handshaking lemma gives:

$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2E \leq 6n - 12$$

Dividing by $n$ (and using the pigeonhole principle, since the average degree is at most $6 - 12/n$):

$$\exists\, v \in V : \deg(v) \leq 5$$

**Step 2 — Inductive setup.**

We proceed by induction on $n$. **Base case:** $n = 1$ trivially needs 1 color. **Inductive step:** Assume the theorem holds for all planar graphs with fewer than $n$ vertices. Let $v$ be the vertex of degree $\leq 5$ found in Step 1.

Remove $v$ and all its incident edges to form $G' = G - v$. $G'$ is still planar, so by the inductive hypothesis, $G'$ has a proper 5-coloring $\varphi : V(G') \to \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$.

**Step 3 — Reinserting $v$ (the easy case).**

Let $N(v) = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_d\}$ with $d \leq 5$. If the set of colors $\varphi(N(v))$ uses at most 4 distinct values, then there exists a color $c \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ not used by any neighbor. Assign $\varphi(v) = c$. Done.

**Step 4 — The hard case: All 5 colors present.**

Suppose $d = 5$ and $\varphi(u_i) = i$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$. (The case $d < 5$ reduces to Step 3.)

**Step 4a — Construct the Kempe chain on colors 1 and 3.**

A **Kempe chain** $K_{1,3}$ is the connected component of the subgraph induced by vertices colored 1 or 3 that contains $u_1$. We examine two sub-cases.

**Step 4b — Sub-case A: $u_3 \notin K_{1,3}$.**

The vertices $u_1$ and $u_3$ lie in different components of the 1-3 subgraph. Swap colors 1 and 3 *within the component containing $u_1$*. After the swap, $u_1$ becomes color 3, and no neighbor of $v$ has color 1. Therefore $v$ can be colored 1. Done.

**Step 4c — Sub-case B: $u_3 \in K_{1,3}$.**

Then there exists a path $P$ from $u_1$ to $u_3$ in $G$ using only colors 1 and 3. The path $P$, together with the edges $vu_1$ and $vu_3$, forms a cycle $C$ in the plane.

Now consider the **Kempe chain on colors 2 and 4** in $G' - v$. We claim $u_2$ and $u_4$ lie in *different* components of the 2-4 subgraph.

Suppose for contradiction they are connected by a path $Q$ using only colors 2 and 4. Then $Q$ from $u_2$ to $u_4$, plus edges $vu_2$ and $vu_4$, forms another cycle $C'$. The cycles $C$ and $C'$ both pass through $v$ and must therefore cross (by the Jordan curve theorem applied in the plane). But $G$ is planar, so $G'$ cannot contain such a crossing. Contradiction.

**Step 4d — Freeing a color in Sub-case B.**

Since $u_2$ and $u_4$ are in different 2-4 Kempe components, swap colors 2 and 4 in the component containing $u_2$. Now $u_2$ is color 4, and no neighbor of $v$ has color 2. Assign $\varphi(v) = 2$. Done.

**Conclusion:** In all cases, $v$ receives a color from $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, completing the induction. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Edge Bound for Planar Graphs

We derive $E \leq 3V - 6$ for a simple connected planar graph with $V \geq 3$.

Start with Euler's formula:

$$V - E + F = 2$$

Every face is bounded by at least 3 edges, and each edge borders at most 2 faces (because $G$ is simple, no bridge is counted twice on the same face). Summing face-sizes:

$$\sum_{f} \text{size}(f) = 2E \geq 3F$$

Hence $F \leq 2E / 3$. Substituting into Euler's:

$$V - E + \frac{2E}{3} \geq 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V - \frac{E}{3} \geq 2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; E \leq 3V - 6$$

Therefore, the average degree $\bar{d} = 2E/V \leq 6 - 12/V < 6$, confirming a vertex of degree $\leq 5$ exists.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Interval Graph Greedy is Optimal

Let $\mathcal{I} = \{I_1, I_2, \dots, I_n\}$ be intervals with $I_i = [a_i, b_i]$. Let $G$ be the interval graph of $\mathcal{I}$.

**Claim:** Sorting by $a_i$ and running greedy yields $\chi(G) = \omega(G)$.

**Proof.**

Let $p^*$ be a real number where the maximum overlap is attained, i.e.,

$$\omega(G) = \max_t \vert \{ i : a_i \leq t \leq b_i \} \vert = k.$$

Then the set $S = \{i : a_i \leq p^* \leq b_i\}$ is a clique of size $k$, so $\omega(G) \geq k$. Conversely, every clique in an interval graph is the set of intervals sharing a common point, so $\omega(G) \leq k$. Hence $\omega(G) = k$.

Now run greedy in order $a_1 \leq a_2 \leq \dots \leq a_n$. Consider the moment interval $I_i$ is processed. The number of colors already in use equals the number of neighbors of $I_i$ that *started before* $I_i$ and have not yet ended — i.e., the number of $I_j$ with $a_j \leq a_i$ and $b_j \geq a_i$. This is exactly the overlap count at the point $t = a_i$, which is at most $k$. So $I_i$ needs at most $k+1$ colors... but the overlap at $a_i$ *cannot strictly exceed* $k$ unless $a_i$ is a new max-overlap point.

A cleaner argument: the algorithm uses the *smallest* available color, so the number of colors used equals

$$\max_i \left(1 + \text{overlap of } I_i \text{ at its left endpoint } a_i \text{ minus open-ended overlaps}\right) = k.$$

Formally, let $C(i) = \vert \{ j : j < i, \, b_j \geq a_i \} \vert$. Then greedy assigns color $\min\{c \notin C(i)\} \leq C(i) + 1 \leq k$ because at $t = a_i$, the overlap is at most $k$, and at least one of those intervals is $I_i$ itself (or hasn't started yet — adjust for the strict left-open condition).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cleaner Final Argument:** The number of colors used by greedy is exactly $\max_{i} C(i)$, which by definition is the max overlap at the left endpoints, which equals the max overlap overall (since overlaps change only at endpoints) $= \omega(G) = \chi(G)$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Coloring an Interval Graph

**Intervals:**
- $I_1 = [1, 4]$
- $I_2 = [2, 6]$
- $I_3 = [3, 5]$
- $I_4 = [7, 9]$
- $I_5 = [5, 8]$

**Step 1 — Sort by left endpoint:** $1, 2, 3, 5, 7$ → order $(I_1, I_2, I_3, I_5, I_4)$.

**Step 2 — Greedy color:**

| Vertex | Interval | Active intervals (overlap at left endpoint) | Smallest free color | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $I_1$ | $[1, 4]$ | $\emptyset$ | 1 | **1** |
| $I_2$ | $[2, 6]$ | $\{I_1\}$ | 2 | **2** |
| $I_3$ | $[3, 5]$ | $\{I_1, I_2\}$ | 3 | **3** |
| $I_5$ | $[5, 8]$ | $\{I_2, I_3\}$ (still alive at 5) | 1 | **1** |
| $I_4$ | $[7, 9]$ | $\{I_5\}$ | 2 | **2** |

**Result:** 3 colors used. Max overlap at $t = 3.5$ is $\{I_1, I_2, I_3\}$, size 3. So $\chi(G) = \omega(G) = 3$. ✓

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: 5-Color Theorem on $K_5$-minor-free Graph

Consider the wheel graph $W_5$ (5-cycle plus a central hub connected to all 5 rim vertices). $W_5$ is planar. $\Delta(W_5) = 5$ (the hub).

- Hub $h$ has degree 5 with neighbors $v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5$ (in cyclic order around the rim).
- Greedy gives the hub 6 colors in the worst case.
- The Kempe chain between $v_1$ and $v_3$ (say) wraps *around* the cycle, so it's a single Kempe component. Then the 2-4 chain between $v_2$ and $v_4$ must be disconnected from it (else cross). We recolor and free a color for $h$.

Hence $\chi(W_5) \leq 5$, which can be shown to be exactly 4 (since $W_5$ contains $K_4$ as a subgraph, and is not $K_5$-minor).
