# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]
**State the Four Color Theorem. Why is it significant for planar graphs?**

**Model Answer:**
The Four Color Theorem (Appel & Haken, 1976) states that **every planar graph is 4-vertex-colorable**, i.e., $\chi(G) \leq 4$ for every planar graph $G$.

**Significance:**
- It solves a long-standing conjecture (1852) about map coloring.
- It is the first major mathematical theorem proved with substantial computer assistance.
- It provides a tight bound for chromatic number on the entire class of planar graphs.
- Practical applications include VLSI routing, frequency assignment, and register allocation where the underlying graph is planar.

*(3 marks: 1 mark statement, 2 marks significance)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]
**Define an interval graph. State the perfectness property of interval graphs.**

**Model Answer:**
An **interval graph** $G$ is the intersection graph of a finite collection of intervals on the real line. Each interval $I_i = [a_i, b_i]$ corresponds to a vertex $v_i$, and $v_i v_j \in E(G)$ iff $I_i \cap I_j \neq \emptyset$.

**Perfectness Property:**
Every interval graph is a **perfect graph**, i.e., for every induced subgraph $H$:

$$\chi(H) = \omega(H)$$

In particular, the chromatic number equals the size of the largest clique, and the clique number can be computed by sweeping a point $t$ along the real line and counting the maximum number of overlapping intervals. This converts the NP-hard coloring problem into a polynomial-time one for this class.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks property with statement)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**a) (7 marks)** State and prove the **Five Color Theorem** for planar graphs. Use Euler's formula to establish the existence of a vertex of degree at most 5, and apply the Kempe chain argument to handle the case where all 5 colors are used by the neighbors.

**b) (7 marks)** Apply the 5-color theorem to the wheel graph $W_6$ (5-cycle with a central hub connected to all 5 rim vertices). Construct a valid 5-coloring of $W_6$ explicitly. State the chromatic number of $W_6$ and justify.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Statement:** Every simple planar graph $G$ is 5-vertex-colorable.

**Proof:**

We proceed by induction on $n = \vert V(G) \vert$.

**Base case:** $n \leq 5$ — trivially colorable with 5 colors.

**Inductive step:** Assume the result for all planar graphs with fewer than $n$ vertices. Let $G$ be planar with $n$ vertices. By Euler's formula and the bound $E \leq 3n - 6$, we have

$$\sum_v \deg(v) = 2E \leq 6n - 12$$

By averaging, $\exists v \in V$ with $\deg(v) \leq 5$.

Remove $v$: the graph $G - v$ is planar, so by induction has a proper 5-coloring $\varphi$. Let $N(v) = \{u_1, \dots, u_d\}$ with $d \leq 5$.

**Case 1:** At most 4 distinct colors appear on $N(v)$. Pick a missing color $c$, set $\varphi(v) = c$. ✓

**Case 2:** $d = 5$ and colors $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ all appear, with $\varphi(u_i) = i$.

Form the **Kempe chain** $K_{1,3}$: the connected component of $G' = G - v$ restricted to vertices colored 1 or 3 that contains $u_1$.

**Sub-case 2a:** $u_3 \notin K_{1,3}$. Swap colors 1 and 3 within $K_{1,3}$. Then $u_1$ becomes color 3, freeing color 1 for $v$. ✓

**Sub-case 2b:** $u_3 \in K_{1,3}$. Then there is a path from $u_1$ to $u_3$ using only colors 1 and 3, forming a cycle with $v$. 

Now consider the **2-4 Kempe chain** containing $u_2$. We claim $u_4 \notin K_{2,4}$. If it were, a 2-4 path from $u_2$ to $u_4$ would form a cycle with $v$ that *crosses* the previous 1-3 cycle (Jordan curve theorem in the plane), contradicting planarity.

So $u_4$ is in a different 2-4 component. Swap 2 and 4 in $K_{2,4}$. Now $u_2$ becomes color 4, freeing color 2 for $v$. ✓

In every case, $v$ is properly colored with a color from $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. $\blacksquare$

*(7 marks: 1 statement, 2 existence of low-degree vertex, 2 case analysis, 2 Kempe chain details)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Wheel Graph $W_6$:** $V = \{h, v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5\}$; edges: rim cycle $v_1 v_2 v_3 v_4 v_5 v_1$ plus spokes $h v_i$ for $i = 1, \dots, 5$.

**Step 1 — Apply 5CT strategy:** Hub $h$ has degree 5. Suppose the 5 rim vertices are colored 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (in cyclic order around the rim).

**Step 2 — Build 1-3 Kempe chain from $v_1$:** In the rim, the path $v_1 - v_2 - v_3$ uses colors 1, 2, 3 — but $v_2$ has color 2, so the 1-3 path from $v_1$ to $v_3$ through the rim is *blocked* at $v_2$. Hence $v_3$ is in a different 1-3 component from $v_1$. (They are in different components because no 1-3 path connects them within $G' = G - h$.)

Wait — we need to check: in the *spoke* subgraph, $v_1$ and $v_3$ are not directly connected (the only path goes through the rim via $v_2$ which is color 2). So $K_{1,3}$ containing $v_1$ is just $\{v_1\}$.

**Step 3 — Swap 1 and 3 in $K_{1,3}$:** Just $v_1$ changes from color 1 to color 3.

**Step 4 — Recolor hub:** Now neighbors of $h$ have colors $\{3, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. Color 1 is missing. Set $\varphi(h) = 1$.

**Final coloring:** $h=1, v_1=3, v_2=2, v_3=3, v_4=4, v_5=5$.

**Chromatic number of $W_6$:** Note that $W_6$ contains $K_4$ as a subgraph (e.g., $\{h, v_1, v_2, v_5\}$ — but check edges: $h v_1, h v_2, h v_5, v_1 v_2, v_2 v_5, v_5 v_1$ — yes, $K_4$). So $\chi(W_6) \geq 4$. Since $W_6$ is planar, $\chi(W_6) \leq 4$. Hence $\chi(W_6) = 4$.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for explicit Kempe application, 2 for $\chi$ determination)*

---

### **Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**a) (7 marks)** Define an **interval graph**. Prove that every interval graph is a **perfect graph**, i.e., $\chi(G) = \omega(G)$ for every interval graph $G$.

**b) (7 marks)** Given the intervals $I_1 = [1, 5], I_2 = [2, 4], I_3 = [6, 9], I_4 = [3, 8], I_5 = [7, 10]$:
   - (i) Construct the interval graph $G$.
   - (ii) Find $\omega(G)$ using the sweep line method.
   - (iii) Apply the greedy algorithm in left-endpoint order to obtain an optimal coloring of $G$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Definition:** An **interval graph** $G = (V, E)$ is the intersection graph of a finite family of closed intervals on the real line. That is, $V = \{v_1, \dots, v_n\}$ with $v_i$ corresponding to $I_i = [a_i, b_i]$, and $v_i v_j \in E$ iff $I_i \cap I_j \neq \emptyset$.

**Theorem:** Every interval graph is perfect: $\chi(G) = \omega(G)$.

**Proof:**

**Part 1 — $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$:** This holds for *any* graph, since any clique of size $k$ requires at least $k$ colors. So $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$ is trivial.

**Part 2 — $\chi(G) \leq \omega(G)$:** We give a constructive proof using a greedy algorithm.

**Step 1:** Sort the intervals as $I_{\pi(1)}, I_{\pi(2)}, \dots, I_{\pi(n)}$ so that $a_{\pi(1)} \leq a_{\pi(2)} \leq \dots \leq a_{\pi(n)}$.

**Step 2:** Color greedily: for $i = 1, \dots, n$, assign to $v_{\pi(i)}$ the smallest color in $\{1, 2, 3, \dots\}$ not used by its already-colored neighbors.

**Step 3 — Analysis:** When $v_{\pi(i)}$ is colored, the active neighbors are those $v_{\pi(j)}$ with $j < i$ such that $I_{\pi(j)} \cap I_{\pi(i)} \neq \emptyset$. This is equivalent to $b_{\pi(j)} \geq a_{\pi(i)}$.

The number of such active neighbors equals the number of intervals covering the point $t = a_{\pi(i)}$ *that have already started*. By definition, the maximum overlap $\omega(G)$ is the maximum number of intervals covering any point, and the number of active neighbors at step $i$ is at most $\omega(G) - 1$ (the $-1$ accounts for the fact that we count only those strictly before $i$ in order, but at the left endpoint of $I_{\pi(i)}$ itself the interval has just started).

Formally, let $k = \omega(G)$. We claim greedy uses at most $k$ colors.

**Claim:** The number of colors used equals $k' = \max_i \, C(i)$, where $C(i) = \vert \{ j : j < i, \, b_{\pi(j)} \geq a_{\pi(i)} \} \vert$.

We show $k' = k$. Clearly $k' \leq k$ because at the point $t = a_{\pi(i)}$, the set of intervals covering $t$ (including $I_{\pi(i)}$) has size at most $k$, so $C(i) \leq k - 1$, meaning greedy uses at most $k$ colors.

For the lower bound: let $t^*$ be a point where the maximum overlap is attained, with intervals $\{I_{i_1}, \dots, I_{i_k}\}$ all covering $t^*$. In the sorted order, the first of these intervals, $I_{\pi(j_1)}$, has the smallest left endpoint among them. At step $j_1$, all the other $k - 1$ intervals in the clique are active (since they cover $t^* \geq a_{\pi(j_1)}$). So $C(j_1) \geq k - 1$, meaning greedy uses at least $k$ colors.

Therefore $\chi(G) = k = \omega(G)$. $\blacksquare$

*(7 marks: 1 definition, 2 lower bound, 2 greedy construction, 2 upper bound analysis)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Part (i) — Construct the interval graph $G$:**

Pairwise intersections:
- $I_1 = [1, 5]$ ∩ $I_2 = [2, 4]$ = $[2, 4]$ → adjacent
- $I_1$ ∩ $I_3 = [6, 9]$ = $\emptyset$ → not adjacent
- $I_1$ ∩ $I_4 = [3, 8]$ = $[3, 5]$ → adjacent
- $I_1$ ∩ $I_5 = [7, 10]$ = $\emptyset$ → not adjacent
- $I_2$ ∩ $I_3$ = $\emptyset$ → not adjacent
- $I_2$ ∩ $I_4 = [3, 4]$ → adjacent
- $I_2$ ∩ $I_5$ = $\emptyset$ → not adjacent
- $I_3 = [6, 9]$ ∩ $I_4 = [3, 8]$ = $[6, 8]$ → adjacent
- $I_3$ ∩ $I_5 = [7, 9]$ → adjacent
- $I_4$ ∩ $I_5 = [7, 8]$ → adjacent

**Edges:** $\{I_1 I_2, I_1 I_4, I_2 I_4, I_3 I_4, I_3 I_5, I_4 I_5\}$.

**Part (ii) — Sweep line for $\omega(G)$:**

Track overlap count as $t$ increases:

| $t$ | Event | Intervals covering $t$ | Overlap |
|---|---|---|---|
| $1$ | $I_1$ starts | $\{I_1\}$ | 1 |
| $2$ | $I_2$ starts | $\{I_1, I_2\}$ | 2 |
| $3$ | $I_4$ starts | $\{I_1, I_2, I_4\}$ | **3** |
| $4$ | $I_2$ ends | $\{I_1, I_4\}$ | 2 |
| $5$ | $I_1$ ends | $\{I_4\}$ | 1 |
| $6$ | $I_3$ starts | $\{I_3, I_4\}$ | 2 |
| $7$ | $I_5$ starts | $\{I_3, I_4, I_5\}$ | **3** |
| $8$ | $I_3, I_4$ end | $\{I_5\}$ | 1 |
| $9$ | $I_3$ end | $\{I_5\}$ | 1 |
| $10$ | $I_5$ ends | $\emptyset$ | 0 |

Maximum overlap = **3** at $t \in [3, 4]$ and $t \in [7, 8]$. So $\omega(G) = 3$.

**Part (iii) — Greedy coloring in left-endpoint order:**

Sort: $I_1(1), I_2(2), I_4(3), I_3(6), I_5(7)$.

| Step | Vertex | Active Neighbors (already colored, still overlapping) | Smallest Free Color | Assigned |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_1$ | none | 1 | **1** |
| 2 | $I_2$ | $\{I_1\}$ | 2 | **2** |
| 3 | $I_4$ | $\{I_1, I_2\}$ | 3 | **3** |
| 4 | $I_3$ | $\{I_4\}$ | 1 | **1** |
| 5 | $I_5$ | $\{I_3, I_4\}$ | 2 | **2** |

**Result:** $\chi(G) = 3$ colors used, matching $\omega(G) = 3$. Optimal. ✓

*(7 marks: 2 construction, 2 sweep-line, 3 greedy coloring with table)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing face-counting and vertex-coloring in planar graphs.** Many students try to "color faces" of a planar graph. While this is valid (map coloring = face coloring in the planar dual), $\chi(G)$ always refers to **vertex** coloring unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 2. **Forgetting the simple-graph assumption.** Vizing's theorem $\Delta \leq \chi' \leq \Delta + 1$ holds for *simple* graphs only. For multigraphs, the bound can be arbitrarily large.
> 3. **Sorting interval graphs by length instead of left endpoint.** The greedy algorithm is optimal *only* when sorted by **left endpoint** (or right endpoint). Sorting by length gives a sub-optimal coloring.
> 4. **Assuming the Four Color Theorem has an elementary proof.** It does not. The 4CT was proved by exhaustive computer verification of ~1,936 configurations. For exam purposes, prove the **5CT** (which has a clean elementary proof) and *state* the 4CT.
> 5. **Mixing up interval graph and chordal graph.** Interval graphs are a *subclass* of chordal graphs. Every interval graph is chordal, but not vice versa. The perfectness property extends to chordal graphs but with a different proof.
> 6. **Off-by-one in Euler's formula derivation.** $E \leq 3V - 6$ requires $V \geq 3$ and *no* loops/multi-edges. State the hypothesis explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3):**

- ✅ **Planar graph definition:** Drawable in plane with no edge crossings. $K_5$ and $K_{3,3}$ are the **minimal non-planar** graphs (Kuratowski).
- ✅ **Euler's formula:** $V - E + F = 2$ for connected planar graphs.
- ✅ **Edge bound:** $E \leq 3V - 6$ for simple planar graphs with $V \geq 3$.
- ✅ **Min-degree existence:** $\exists v$ with $\deg(v) \leq 5$ in any planar graph.
- ✅ **Five Color Theorem:** $\chi(G) \leq 5$ — provable by induction + Kempe chains.
- ✅ **Four Color Theorem:** $\chi(G) \leq 4$ — proved by Appel & Haken (1976) via computer.
- ✅ **Vizing's Theorem:** $\Delta \leq \chi'(G) \leq \Delta + 1$ for simple graphs.
- ✅ **Vizing's Planar Theorem:** $\chi'(G) = \Delta$ for planar graphs with $\Delta \geq 8$.
- ✅ **König's Theorem:** $\chi'(G) = \Delta$ for **bipartite** graphs.
- ✅ **Interval graph:** Intersection graph of intervals on real line.
- ✅ **Interval graph = chordal graph:** Every interval graph is chordal (every cycle of length $\geq 4$ has a chord).
- ✅ **Perfectness of interval graphs:** $\chi(G) = \omega(G)$ for every induced subgraph.
- ✅ **Greedy optimality:** Sort by left endpoint, then greedy coloring uses exactly $\omega(G)$ colors.
- ✅ **Max clique in interval graphs:** $O(n \log n)$ via sweep line; equals max overlap at any point.
- ✅ **Map coloring ↔ Planar face coloring:** Map regions = faces of planar graph; countries sharing border ↔ faces sharing edge.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** VLSI, PCB design, register allocation, scheduling, frequency assignment, bioinformatics.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Answers:** Always state the *class* of the graph first, then invoke the *specific* theorem for that class. Never apply general greedy bounds $\chi \leq \Delta + 1$ when a tighter class-specific bound is available.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications: Frequency Assignment in Wireless Networks, Scheduling Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications: Frequency Assignment in Wireless Networks, Scheduling Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Graph Coloring as an Engineering Modeling Tool:** Many real-world resource allocation problems in engineering can be reduced to the abstract problem of assigning *colors* (labels) to the vertices (or edges) of a graph such that no two adjacent vertices (or edges) receive the same color. The minimum number of colors required is the **chromatic number χ(G)** of the graph.

Two of the most important industrial applications of graph coloring studied under KTU Module 3 are:

**A. Frequency Assignment Problem (FAP) in Wireless Networks**
The Frequency Assignment Problem is the problem of assigning a radio frequency (or channel) to each transmitter (base station, mobile unit, or wireless node) in a wireless communication network such that **electromagnetic interference is minimized** and the **bandwidth (spectrum span) used is as small as possible**. Formally, each transmitter $v_i \in V$ is assigned a frequency $f(v_i) \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$, and for every pair $(u, v) \in E$ representing potential interference, the difference $\vert f(u) - f(v) \vert$ must satisfy a minimum separation constraint $\delta(u, v) \in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$.

**B. Scheduling Problems**
The Scheduling Problem is the problem of assigning **time slots, classrooms, processors, or exam halls** to a set of events (examinations, lectures, jobs) such that no two *conflicting* events are scheduled at the same resource. Conflicts arise when two events share a student, a teacher, a room, or a resource. Formally, given a conflict graph $G = (V, E)$, each vertex $v \in V$ is an event, each edge $(u,v) \in E$ indicates a conflict, and the minimum number of time slots (or rooms) required equals $\chi(G)$, the chromatic number.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Classroom Coloring Analogy:** Imagine you are the head of a university examination cell. You have **8 exams** to schedule in the **fewest possible days** such that no student is asked to appear for two exams on the same day. How would you do it? You would group the exams into *batches* — a batch being the set of exams that can be held simultaneously because no student is enrolled in more than one of them. Each batch is a *color*, and every two exams in the same batch must not share a common student. This is **exactly** graph coloring — except that instead of days, we are assigning frequencies in wireless networks, classroom slots in schools, or registers in CPU scheduling.

| Real Engineering Problem | "Vertex" Object | "Edge" Object | "Color" Resource |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mobile Network Frequency Assignment | Base Station / Transmitter | Interference Pair | Radio Channel |
| University Exam Scheduling | Course / Exam | Common Student Pair | Exam Day / Time Slot |
| Compiler Register Allocation | Program Variable | Simultaneous Live-Range | CPU Register |
| Traffic Light Coordination | Intersection | Conflicting Traffic Lanes | Phase Slot |
| Job Shop Scheduling | Job / Task | Shared Machine Dependency | Time Window |

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following engineering metrics and standard thresholds govern practical Frequency Assignment Problems and are weighted at **3 marks** in KTU examinations:

- **Minimum Frequency Separation:** typically **≥ 0.630 MHz** between adjacent GSM channels (this real-world constant maps to the *taboo set* in T-coloring theory).
- **Channel Reuse Distance:** the geographic distance beyond which the same frequency can be safely reused (related to the *hexagonal cell cluster size N*).
- **Span of an Assignment:** defined as
  $$\text{Span} = \max_{v \in V} f(v) - \min_{v \in V} f(v) + 1$$
  and is the primary optimization objective in spectrum-efficient frequency planning.
- **Cluster Size N (Cellular Telephony):** the number of cells in a frequency reuse pattern; standard N-patterns include **N = 3, 4, 7, 9, 12**.
- **Lower Bound on Chi:** always satisfies $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$, where $\omega(G)$ is the size of the largest clique in $G$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Reduction Blueprint: From Real World to Graph Coloring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Universal Reduction Principle:** For KTU 2024, every application question will require you to (1) construct a conflict graph, (2) state its coloring constraints, and (3) report the chromatic number / minimum span. Master this three-step blueprint and you will secure full marks.

The canonical reduction pipeline is:

1. **Identify the entities** that need an assignment (transmitters, exams, jobs, variables).
2. **Identify the conflicts** (electromagnetic interference, common student, shared machine, simultaneous live range).
3. **Build the conflict graph** $G = (V, E)$ where vertices are entities and edges are conflicts.
4. **Apply the coloring** — the chromatic number $\chi(G)$ is the minimum number of channels / time slots / registers required.

### 2.2 Variants of the Frequency Assignment Problem

#### 2.2.1 Classical FAP (T-Coloring)

In T-coloring, each edge $(u, v)$ has an associated *taboo set* $T_{uv} \subseteq \mathbb{Z}_{>0}$ of forbidden frequency differences. A valid T-coloring is a function $c: V \to \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ such that
$$\forall (u, v) \in E: \vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \notin T_{uv}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetric Taboo Sets:** In most KTU problems, $T_{uv}$ is taken symmetric: $T_{uv} = T_{vu} = \{1, 2, \ldots, d-1\}$ for some positive integer $d$. This corresponds to the realistic scenario where frequencies of two interfering transmitters must differ by **at least $d$ channels**.

The simplest symmetric case $d = 2$ reduces exactly to ordinary graph coloring (the taboo set is $\{1\}$).

#### 2.2.2 L(h, k) Labeling (Distance-Constrained Labeling)

L(h, k) labeling is the most studied modern variant of FAP, introduced by Griggs & Yeh (1992). For two vertices $u, v$ with graph distance $d_G(u, v)$:
- If $d_G(u, v) = 1$, then $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \geq h$
- If $d_G(u, v) = 2$, then $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \geq k$

with $h \geq k \geq 1$. The objective is to minimize the **span** $\lambda_h^k(G) = \max_v c(v) - \min_v c(v)$. The most common case is **L(2, 1) labeling**, which corresponds to *second-order co-channel interference*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance of L(2,1):** In L(2,1) labeling, $h = 2$ models the *primary* (adjacent-cell) co-channel interference constraint, while $k = 1$ models the *secondary* (two-hop neighbor) interference. This two-tier constraint dramatically improves call quality in dense urban cellular networks.

#### 2.2.3 List Coloring / Channel Availability Coloring

In modern cognitive radio and dynamic spectrum access networks, the available channels at each transmitter may differ due to licensing, geography, or hardware. Each vertex $v$ has an allowed list $L(v) \subseteq \mathbb{N}$, and we must find $c(v) \in L(v)$ such that the constraints hold.

### 2.3 Variants of the Scheduling Problem

| Scheduling Variant | Graph Object | Color Represents | Objective |
|---|---|---|---|
| Exam Timetabling | Conflict graph of courses | Exam day | Minimize number of days |
| Classroom Allocation | Conflict graph of sections | Classroom | Minimize classrooms |
| CPU Register Allocation | Interference graph of live ranges | Physical register | Minimize spills |
| Parallel Job Scheduling | Resource conflict graph | Time slot on machine | Minimize makespan |
| Edge Coloring for Job Shop | Multigraph of jobs vs. machines | Time slot | Chromatic index $\chi'(G)$ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you need for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Memorize this table.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Meaning | Standard KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chromatic Number | $\chi(G) = $ min colors needed | Minimum channels / slots | 2 marks |
| Clique Lower Bound | $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$ | Lower bound from largest clique | 2 marks |
| Brooks' Upper Bound | $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$ | Upper bound from max degree | 3 marks |
| Chromatic Index (Edge Coloring) | $\chi'(G) = $ min edge colors | Time slots for parallel machines | 3 marks |
| Vizing's Theorem | $\chi'(G) \in \{\Delta(G), \Delta(G)+1\}$ | Tight bounds on edge coloring | 3 marks |
| Span of L(h,k) | $\lambda_h^k(G) = \max c - \min c + 1$ | Total spectrum used | 3 marks |
| L(h,k) Adjacent Constraint | $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \geq h$ for $d_G(u,v)=1$ | Primary interference | 2 marks |
| L(h,k) Distance-2 Constraint | $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \geq k$ for $d_G(u,v)=2$ | Secondary interference | 2 marks |
| T-Coloring Taboo | $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \notin T_{uv}$ | Forbidden frequency diffs | 3 marks |
| Tree Coloring | $\chi(\text{tree}) = 2$ | Any tree is bipartite | 1 mark |
| Planar Graph (4-Color Thm) | $\chi(G) \leq 4$ for planar $G$ | Frequency reuse on planar networks | 2 marks |
| Span Lower Bound | $\lambda_h^k(G) \geq h \cdot (\chi(G) - 1) + k \cdot (\omega(G) - 1) + 1$ | Hard lower bound for L(h,k) | 5 marks |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Use Cases Worth Mentioning in KTU Answers:**

- **Telecom giants** (Ericsson, Nokia, Huawei) formulate frequency assignment as L(2,1) labeling to plan 4G LTE and 5G NR networks — minimizing the spectrum span saves billions of rupees in spectrum auction costs.
- **Compiler back-ends** (GCC, LLVM) use *interference graph coloring* (Chaitin's algorithm) for register allocation — every saved register accelerates compiled code.
- **Universities worldwide** (KTU included) use graph-coloring-based automated timetabling systems (e.g., the open-source FET software) to schedule lakhs of exams without clashes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Frequency Assignment Problem to Graph Coloring

**Given:** A wireless network with $n$ transmitters. Two transmitters $i$ and $j$ interfere if they are within distance $d_{ij}$. Each transmitter must be assigned a frequency channel.

**Step 1 — Identify Entities as Vertices:**
Each transmitter $T_i$ corresponds to a vertex $v_i$ in the conflict graph $G = (V, E)$. Hence $\vert V \vert = n$.

**Step 2 — Identify Conflicts as Edges:**
For every pair of transmitters $(T_i, T_j)$ that interfere, draw an undirected edge $e = (v_i, v_j) \in E$.

**Step 3 — Map Channels to Colors:**
Let the set of available frequency channels be $\{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$. Each $c_\ell$ corresponds to a *color*. A valid frequency assignment is a function
$$c: V \to \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$$
such that for every edge $(v_i, v_j) \in E$, we have $c(v_i) \neq c(v_j)$.

**Step 4 — Equivalence:**
The minimum $k$ for which such a coloring exists is precisely $\chi(G)$, the chromatic number of the conflict graph. This proves the reduction.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Computing the Span Lower Bound for L(2,1) Labeling

**Claim:** For any connected graph $G$ of order $n \geq 2$, $\lambda_2^1(G) \geq h + k \cdot (\omega(G) - 1) + 1$ when $h = 2, k = 1$, i.e., $\lambda_2^1(G) \geq \omega(G) + 2$.

**Proof:**

Let $C$ be a maximum clique in $G$ with $\vert C \vert = \omega(G)$. Order the vertices of $C$ as $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_{\omega}$ such that $c(v_1) \leq c(v_2) \leq \cdots \leq c(v_\omega)$.

Since every pair in $C$ is adjacent (i.e., $d_G = 1$), by the L(2,1) constraint:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c(v_{i+1}) - c(v_i) &\geq 2, \quad \text{for all } i = 1, \ldots, \omega - 1 \\
\text{Therefore, } c(v_\omega) - c(v_1) &\geq 2 \cdot (\omega - 1) \\
\text{Span } \lambda_2^1(G) &= c(v_\omega) - c(v_1) + 1 \geq 2(\omega - 1) + 1 = 2\omega - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stronger Bound (using both $h$ and $k$):** Choose a vertex $v_1 \in C$ such that $c(v_1) = \min_{v \in C} c(v)$. Then for every other $v_i \in C \setminus \{v_1\}$, since $d_G(v_1, v_i) = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c(v_i) - c(v_1) &\geq h = 2 \\
\text{Hence, } c(v_1) + 1 &\text{ cannot be reused within } C \\
\text{Additionally, the second constraint } (\text{distance 2}) &\text{ forbids color } c(v_1) + 1 \text{ on neighbors of } v_1 \\
\text{So the span satisfies } \lambda_2^1(G) &\geq 2 \cdot (\omega(G) - 1) + 1 = 2\omega - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:** Take $G = K_4$ (a complete graph on 4 vertices). Then $\omega(G) = 4$. The minimum L(2,1) span is $2(4-1)+1 = 7$. One optimal assignment is $c(v_1) = 0, c(v_2) = 2, c(v_3) = 4, c(v_4) = 6$, giving span $= 7$. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3: From Exam Scheduling to Graph Coloring

**Given:** A university has 6 courses $C_1, \ldots, C_6$ and 5 students with enrollments as listed.

| Student | Courses Enrolled |
|---|---|
| $S_1$ | $C_1, C_3$ |
| $S_2$ | $C_2, C_4, C_6$ |
| $S_3$ | $C_1, C_5$ |
| $S_4$ | $C_2, C_3, C_5$ |
| $S_5$ | $C_4, C_6$ |

**Step 1 — Build the Conflict Edge Set:**

- $S_1$ creates conflict $\{C_1, C_3\}$
- $S_2$ creates conflicts $\{C_2, C_4\}, \{C_2, C_6\}, \{C_4, C_6\}$
- $S_3$ creates conflict $\{C_1, C_5\}$
- $S_4$ creates conflicts $\{C_2, C_3\}, \{C_2, C_5\}, \{C_3, C_5\}$
- $S_5$ creates conflict $\{C_4, C_6\}$

**Step 2 — Accumulate the Edge Set:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E = \{ & \{C_1, C_3\}, \{C_2, C_4\}, \{C_2, C_6\}, \{C_4, C_6\}, \{C_1, C_5\}, \\
& \{C_2, C_3\}, \{C_2, C_5\}, \{C_3, C_5\} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total: **8 edges**.

**Step 3 — Identify Maximum Clique:**

Consider $\{C_2, C_3, C_5\}$. The pairs are $\{C_2, C_3\}, \{C_2, C_5\}, \{C_3, C_5\}$, all in $E$. So $\{C_2, C_3, C_5\}$ is a clique of size 3. Hence $\omega(G) \geq 3$, giving the lower bound $\chi(G) \geq 3$.

**Step 4 — Attempt a 3-Coloring:**

Try: $c(C_2) = 1, c(C_3) = 2, c(C_5) = 3$.

- $C_1$ conflicts with $C_3, C_5$: choose $c(C_1) = 1$.
- $C_4$ conflicts with $C_2, C_6$: choose $c(C_4) = 2$.
- $C_6$ conflicts with $C_2, C_4$: choose $c(C_6) = 3$.

Check all edges:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{C_1, C_3\}: c = 1, 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{C_2, C_4\}: c = 1, 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{C_2, C_6\}: c = 1, 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{C_4, C_6\}: c = 2, 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{C_1, C_5\}: c = 1, 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{C_2, C_3\}: c = 1, 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{C_2, C_5\}: c = 1, 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{C_3, C_5\}: c = 2, 3 \quad &\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All constraints satisfied with **3 colors**. Hence $\chi(G) = 3$, and the university needs **exactly 3 exam days** for these 6 courses. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of FAP vs Scheduling Models

| Dimension | Frequency Assignment | Scheduling |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical Model | Vertex coloring with distance constraints | Vertex coloring with conflict constraints |
| Constraint Type | $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \geq \delta_{uv}$ (numerical) | $c(u) \neq c(v)$ (categorical) |
| Special Variant | L(h, k) labeling | Edge coloring for parallel machines |
| Optimization Metric | Minimize span | Minimize time slots (makespan) |
| NP-Hard Variant | L(h, k) for general $h, k$ | Examination timetabling |
| Polynomial Variant | Trees, paths, cycles, bipartite graphs | Interval graphs, comparability graphs |
| Classical Result | $\lambda_2^1(K_n) = 2n - 1$ | $\chi(K_n) = n$ |
| Engineering Subfield | Wireless cellular networks | Operations Research, Compilers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Universal Reduction Pipeline (FAP + Scheduling → Graph Coloring)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Real World Engineering Problem"] --> B["Identify Entities as Vertices"]
    B --> C["Identify Conflicts as Edges"]
    C --> D["Construct Conflict Graph G"]
    D --> E["Apply Vertex or Edge Coloring"]
    E --> F["Optimal Frequency or Schedule Assignment"]
    F --> G["Validate Constraints"]

    A1["FAP: Base Stations"] -.-> A
    A2["Scheduling: Exams or Jobs"] -.-> A
    A3["Compiler: Variables"] -.-> A

    G --> G1["No two adjacent vertices share color"]
    G --> G2["Frequency span is minimized"]
    G --> G3["No student has two same-day exams"]

    stepA["Vertex Set V"] --> D
    stepB["Edge Set E"] --> D
    stepC["Color Function c"] --> E
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Sample Wireless Network with Frequency Assignment

```mermaid
graph LR
    BTS1["BTS-A Freq 1"] --- BTS2["BTS-B Freq 2"]
    BTS2 --- BTS3["BTS-C Freq 1"]
    BTS3 --- BTS4["BTS-D Freq 3"]
    BTS1 --- BTS3
    BTS4 --- BTS5["BTS-E Freq 2"]
    BTS5 --- BTS2
    BTS1 --- BTS5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each node (BTS-A through BTS-E) is a Base Transceiver Station. Edges denote interference pairs. The labels show the assigned frequency. Notice that BTS-A and BTS-C are non-adjacent, so they safely reuse Frequency 1. The span is max(3) − min(1) + 1 = **3 channels**.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Exam Conflict Graph (6 Courses, 8 Conflicts)

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1["Course 1 Day A"] --- C3["Course 3 Day B"]
    C2["Course 2 Day A"] --- C4["Course 4 Day B"]
    C2 --- C6["Course 6 Day C"]
    C4 --- C6
    C1 --- C5["Course 5 Day C"]
    C2 --- C3
    C2 --- C5
    C3 --- C5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Three colors (Day A, Day B, Day C) suffice. The clique $\{C_2, C_3, C_5\}$ on Day A, Day B, Day C respectively proves $\chi(G) \geq 3$. Since 3 colors work, $\chi(G) = 3$ — exactly **3 exam days** are needed.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of FAP Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input["Input: Network Topology and Interference Matrix"] --> parser["Network Parser Module"]
    parser --> graphbuild["Conflict Graph Builder"]
    graphbuild --> adjmat["Adjacency Matrix Generator"]
    adjmat --> coloring["Greedy or DSATUR Coloring Engine"]
    coloring --> kvalue["Compute Chromatic Number Chi G"]
    kvalue --> validate["Constraint Validator L h k or T Coloring"]
    validate -->|Valid| output["Output: Frequency Plan with Span"]
    validate -->|Invalid| refine["Refine Coloring and Backtrack"]
    refine --> coloring
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Frequency Assignment Problem (FAP) in wireless networks. How is it modeled as a graph coloring problem?*

**Model Answer:**
The Frequency Assignment Problem (FAP) is the problem of assigning a radio frequency (channel) to each transmitter in a wireless network such that the electromagnetic interference between nearby transmitters is avoided and the total spectrum span used is minimized.

It is modeled as a graph coloring problem as follows:

1. Each transmitter in the network corresponds to a vertex $v_i \in V$ of a conflict graph $G = (V, E)$.
2. An edge $(v_i, v_j) \in E$ exists if and only if transmitters $i$ and $j$ interfere with each other (e.g., they are within the minimum channel reuse distance).
3. Each available frequency channel corresponds to a distinct color.
4. A valid frequency assignment is a proper vertex coloring $c: V \to \mathbb{N}$ such that $c(v_i) \neq c(v_j)$ for all edges $(v_i, v_j) \in E$.
5. The minimum number of frequencies (channels) required equals the chromatic number $\chi(G)$.

Hence, FAP reduces to finding the chromatic number of the conflict graph.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State and explain Brooks' theorem. Why is it important for frequency assignment?*

**Model Answer:**
**Brooks' Theorem:** For any connected undirected graph $G$ that is neither a complete graph $K_n$ nor an odd cycle, the chromatic number satisfies
$$\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$$
where $\Delta(G)$ is the maximum vertex degree.

For the exceptional cases, $\chi(K_n) = n = \Delta(K_n) + 1$ and $\chi(C_{2k+1}) = 3 = \Delta(C_{2k+1}) + 1$.

**Importance for FAP:** In a wireless network, the degree of a base station vertex represents the number of *interfering neighbors*. Brooks' theorem provides an **upper bound on the number of frequencies** required. So if the maximum interference degree in a network is $\Delta$, then at most $\Delta$ frequencies (or $\Delta + 1$ in worst case) are needed. This is critical for engineers planning spectrum-efficient networks without performing full chromatic number computation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q. A wireless cellular network has 6 base stations $B_1, B_2, B_3, B_4, B_5, B_6$. The following pairs of base stations interfere with each other and must have a minimum frequency separation of 2 channels:**
$\{B_1, B_2\}, \{B_1, B_3\}, \{B_2, B_3\}, \{B_2, B_4\}, \{B_3, B_5\}, \{B_4, B_5\}, \{B_4, B_6\}, \{B_5, B_6\}$

**(a)** Construct the conflict graph. Determine the minimum number of channels required and provide a valid frequency assignment.

**(b)** Model the conflict graph as an L(2,1) labeling problem and compute the minimum span.

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Build the Conflict Graph:**
Vertices: $V = \{B_1, B_2, B_3, B_4, B_5, B_6\}$
Edges: $E = \{\{B_1, B_2\}, \{B_1, B_3\}, \{B_2, B_3\}, \{B_2, B_4\}, \{B_3, B_5\}, \{B_4, B_5\}, \{B_4, B_6\}, \{B_5, B_6\}\}$

Total vertices: 6. Total edges: 8.

**Step 2 — Identify the Maximum Clique:**

Examine triangle $\{B_1, B_2, B_3\}$ — all three edges $\{B_1, B_2\}, \{B_1, B_3\}, \{B_2, B_3\}$ are in $E$. Hence $\{B_1, B_2, B_3\}$ is a clique of size 3. So $\omega(G) = 3$, giving lower bound $\chi(G) \geq 3$.

Also examine triangle $\{B_4, B_5, B_6\}$ — all three edges $\{B_4, B_5\}, \{B_4, B_6\}, \{B_5, B_6\}$ are in $E$. So another triangle exists.

**Step 3 — Maximum Degree:**
Degrees: $\deg(B_1) = 2, \deg(B_2) = 3, \deg(B_3) = 2, \deg(B_4) = 3, \deg(B_5) = 3, \deg(B_6) = 2$. So $\Delta(G) = 3$.

**Step 4 — Attempt a 3-Coloring:**

Since $G$ contains triangles but no $K_4$, and is not an odd cycle, Brooks' theorem gives $\chi(G) \leq 3$.

Try: $c(B_1) = 1, c(B_2) = 2, c(B_3) = 3$ (handles first triangle).

- $B_4$ conflicts with $B_2$: try $c(B_4) = 1$.
- $B_5$ conflicts with $B_3$ and $B_4$: try $c(B_5) = 2$.
- $B_6$ conflicts with $B_4$ and $B_5$: try $c(B_6) = 3$.

Verify all 8 edges:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{B_1, B_2\}: 1 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{B_1, B_3\}: 1 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{B_2, B_3\}: 2 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{B_2, B_4\}: 2 \neq 1 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{B_3, B_5\}: 3 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{B_4, B_5\}: 1 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{B_4, B_6\}: 1 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{B_5, B_6\}: 2 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All constraints satisfied. Hence $\chi(G) = 3$ and **3 channels are required**.

**Valid Assignment (Minimum Channel Separation = 1):**
$B_1 = 1, B_2 = 2, B_3 = 3, B_4 = 1, B_5 = 2, B_6 = 3$.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**L(2,1) Modeling:**
For L(2,1) labeling, we require:
- $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \geq 2$ if $d_G(u, v) = 1$
- $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \geq 1$ if $d_G(u, v) = 2$

**Step 1 — Identify Distance-2 Pairs:**

For each edge (distance 1), identify vertices at distance 2.

- $B_1$ (neighbors: $B_2, B_3$): distance-2 vertices are $B_4$ (via $B_2$).
- $B_2$ (neighbors: $B_1, B_3, B_4$): distance-2 vertices are $B_5, B_6$ (via $B_4$).
- $B_3$ (neighbors: $B_1, B_2, B_5$): distance-2 vertices are $B_4$ (via $B_2$).
- $B_4$ (neighbors: $B_2, B_5, B_6$): distance-2 vertices are $B_1, B_3$ (via $B_2$), also $B_2$.
- $B_5$ (neighbors: $B_3, B_4, B_6$): distance-2 vertices include $B_2$ (via $B_4$).
- $B_6$ (neighbors: $B_4, B_5$): distance-2 vertex is $B_2$ (via $B_4$).

**Step 2 — Apply Lower Bound Formula:**

Using $\lambda_2^1(G) \geq 2\omega(G) - 1$ with $\omega(G) = 3$:
$$\lambda_2^1(G) \geq 2(3) - 1 = 5$$

**Step 3 — Attempt L(2,1) Assignment with Span 5:**

Use channels $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$. For triangle $\{B_1, B_2, B_3\}$: assign $c(B_1) = 0, c(B_2) = 2, c(B_3) = 4$ (separation = 2).

- $B_4$ conflicts with $B_2$: try $c(B_4) = 4$. But $B_4$ at distance 2 from $B_1$ requires $\vert 4 - 0 \vert \geq 1$ ✓.
- $B_5$ conflicts with $B_3$ and $B_4$: try $c(B_5) = 0$. Check distance 2 with $B_2$: $\vert 0 - 2 \vert = 2 \geq 1$ ✓.
- $B_6$ conflicts with $B_4$ and $B_5$: try $c(B_6) = 2$. Check distance 2 with $B_2$: $\vert 2 - 2 \vert = 0 < 1$ ✗. Try $c(B_6) = 1$. Check $\{B_4, B_6\}: \vert 4 - 1 \vert = 3 \geq 2$ ✓. Check $\{B_5, B_6\}: \vert 0 - 1 \vert = 1 < 2$ ✗. Try $c(B_6) = 3$. Check $\{B_4, B_6\}: \vert 4 - 3 \vert = 1 < 2$ ✗. Try $c(B_6) = 0$. Check $\{B_4, B_6\}: \vert 4 - 0 \vert = 4 \geq 2$ ✓. Check $\{B_5, B_6\}: \vert 0 - 0 \vert = 0 < 2$ ✗.

This span-5 attempt fails for $B_6$. So span must be larger than 5.

**Step 4 — Try Span 6 with channels $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$:**

- $c(B_1) = 0, c(B_2) = 2, c(B_3) = 4$
- $c(B_4) = ?$: not 2 (conflict with $B_2$). Try $c(B_4) = 5$. Check distance 2 to $B_1$ (via $B_2$): $\vert 5 - 0 \vert = 5 \geq 1$ ✓.
- $c(B_5) = ?$: not 4 (conflict with $B_3$), not 5 (conflict with $B_4$). Try $c(B_5) = 1$. Check distance 2 to $B_2$ (via $B_4$): $\vert 1 - 2 \vert = 1 \geq 1$ ✓.
- $c(B_6) = ?$: not 5 (conflict with $B_4$), not 1 (conflict with $B_5$). Try $c(B_6) = 3$. Check distance 2 to $B_2$ (via $B_4$): $\vert 3 - 2 \vert = 1 \geq 1$ ✓.

**Final L(2,1) Assignment:**

$$c(B_1) = 0, \; c(B_2) = 2, \; c(B_3) = 4, \; c(B_4) = 5, \; c(B_5) = 1, \; c(B_6) = 3$$

$$\text{Span} = \max(5) - \min(0) + 1 = 6$$

Therefore, $\lambda_2^1(G) = 6$.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**Q. A university has 7 courses $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_7$ to schedule. The following student enrollments create conflicts:**
- $S_1$: $\{E_1, E_2, E_3\}$
- $S_2$: $\{E_2, E_4\}$
- $S_3$: $\{E_3, E_5, E_6\}$
- $S_4$: $\{E_1, E_5\}$
- $S_5$: $\{E_4, E_6\}$
- $S_6$: $\{E_2, E_7\}$

**(a)** Build the conflict graph and find the minimum number of days required for examinations. Justify using the chromatic number.

**(b)** Explain how the same graph can model a frequency assignment problem with a T-coloring constraint where the taboo set is $T = \{1\}$ on every edge.

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Conflict Edges from Each Student:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &: \{E_1, E_2\}, \{E_1, E_3\}, \{E_2, E_3\} \\
S_2 &: \{E_2, E_4\} \\
S_3 &: \{E_3, E_5\}, \{E_3, E_6\}, \{E_5, E_6\} \\
S_4 &: \{E_1, E_5\} \\
S_5 &: \{E_4, E_6\} \\
S_6 &: \{E_2, E_7\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total edges: $\vert E \vert = 10$.

**Step 2 — Identify Maximum Cliques:**

Triangle $\{E_1, E_2, E_3\}$ from $S_1$. Triangle $\{E_3, E_5, E_6\}$ from $S_3$. So $\omega(G) \geq 3$.

Is there a 4-clique? Need 4 mutually adjacent vertices. $\{E_3, E_5, E_6\}$ — is $E_2$ adjacent to all three? $E_2 \sim E_3$ yes, but $E_2 \sim E_5$? No. So no $K_4$. Hence $\omega(G) = 3$.

**Step 3 — Lower Bound on Days:** $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G) = 3$.

**Step 4 — Try 3-Coloring:**

Strategy: Process the two triangles first.

- Triangle $\{E_1, E_2, E_3\}$: assign $c(E_1) = 1, c(E_2) = 2, c(E_3) = 3$.
- Triangle $\{E_3, E_5, E_6\}$: $E_3 = 3$, so $c(E_5), c(E_6) \in \{1, 2\}$. Set $c(E_5) = 1, c(E_6) = 2$.
- $E_4$ conflicts with $E_2, E_6$: cannot be 2. Try $c(E_4) = 1$.
- $E_7$ conflicts with $E_2$: cannot be 2. Try $c(E_7) = 1$.

Verify all 10 edges:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{E_1, E_2\}: 1 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_1, E_3\}: 1 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_2, E_3\}: 2 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_2, E_4\}: 2 \neq 1 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_3, E_5\}: 3 \neq 1 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_3, E_6\}: 3 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_5, E_6\}: 1 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_1, E_5\}: 1 \neq 1 \quad &\boldsymbol{\times} \; \text{CONFLICT!}
\end{aligned}
$$

Try fix: change $c(E_5)$ to 2. Check: $\{E_5, E_6\}: 2 \neq 2$ ✗. Try $c(E_5) = 1, c(E_1) = 2$. New: $c(E_1) = 2, c(E_2) = 1, c(E_3) = 3$.

Now $E_5$ conflicts with $E_3, E_1$: try $c(E_5) = 1$. Verify $\{E_5, E_6\}$: need $c(E_6) \neq 1, c(E_6) \neq 3$, so $c(E_6) = 2$.

- $E_4$ conflicts with $E_2 = 1, E_6 = 2$: try $c(E_4) = 3$.
- $E_7$ conflicts with $E_2 = 1$: try $c(E_7) = 2$.

Final assignment: $c(E_1) = 2, c(E_2) = 1, c(E_3) = 3, c(E_4) = 3, c(E_5) = 1, c(E_6) = 2, c(E_7) = 2$.

Verify all 10 edges:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\{E_1, E_2\}: 2 \neq 1 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_1, E_3\}: 2 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_2, E_3\}: 1 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_2, E_4\}: 1 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_3, E_5\}: 3 \neq 1 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_3, E_6\}: 3 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_5, E_6\}: 1 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_1, E_5\}: 2 \neq 1 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_4, E_6\}: 3 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\
\{E_2, E_7\}: 1 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All satisfied! Hence $\chi(G) = 3$. **Minimum 3 days needed.**

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**T-Coloring Reformulation:**

The T-coloring problem with $T = \{1\}$ on every edge requires that for each edge $(u, v)$, $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \notin \{1\}$. Equivalently:
$$\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \geq 2 \quad \text{for all edges}$$

This is **not** ordinary graph coloring (which requires only $c(u) \neq c(v)$, i.e., $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \geq 1$).

**Construction:** The conflict graph $G$ is identical to that in part (a). The taboo set $T_{uv} = \{1\}$ on every edge enforces a *minimum channel separation of 2*.

**Lower Bound on Channels:** Any two adjacent vertices must be at least 2 apart. For a triangle $\{E_1, E_2, E_3\}$, the optimal assignment is $c(E_1) = 0, c(E_2) = 2, c(E_3) = 4$ (or any arithmetic progression with common difference 2).

**Attempt an L(2, 1)-Style Assignment:**

Using channels $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$:
- $c(E_1) = 0, c(E_2) = 2, c(E_3) = 4$.
- $c(E_5)$ conflicts with $E_1, E_3$: must avoid 0 and 4. Try $c(E_5) = 2$. But $\{E_2, E_5\}$ is not an edge (no common student), so 2 is allowed. Try $c(E_5) = 6$. Try $c(E_5) = 2$ (since $\{E_2, E_5\} \notin E$).
- $c(E_6)$ conflicts with $E_3, E_5$: avoid 4 and 2. Try $c(E_6) = 0$. But $\vert 0 - 2 \vert = 2 \geq 2$ ✓ for $E_2$ (no edge), $\vert 0 - 4 \vert = 4 \geq 2$ ✓ for $E_3$. But $c(E_6) = 0 = c(E_5)$ is OK if $\{E_5, E_6\}$ edge requires separation 2: $\vert 0 - 2 \vert = 2 \geq 2$ ✓.

Wait — reconsider: $E_5$ and $E_6$ are adjacent ($\{E_5, E_6\}$ is an edge from $S_3$). With $c(E_5) = 2$ and $c(E_6) = 0$: $\vert 2 - 0 \vert = 2 \geq 2$ ✓.

- $c(E_4)$ conflicts with $E_2 = 2, E_6 = 0$: avoid 0 and 2. Try $c(E_4) = 4$.
- $c(E_7)$ conflicts with $E_2 = 2$: avoid 2. Try $c(E_7) = 4$.

Final T-coloring assignment:
$$c(E_1) = 0, \; c(E_2) = 2, \; c(E_3) = 4, \; c(E_4) = 4, \; c(E_5) = 2, \; c(E_6) = 0, \; c(E_7) = 4$$

$$\text{Span} = \max(4) - \min(0) + 1 = 5$$

Hence the T-coloring with taboo $T = \{1\}$ requires **5 channels** (a span of 5) for the network of 7 base stations (or 7 exams in this reformulated interpretation).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Students often forget to verify ALL edges in the conflict graph after proposing a coloring. KTU examiners strictly deduct 2 marks for every missed verification row.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–3 marks):** Confusing ordinary graph coloring with L(2,1) coloring. If the problem states a minimum separation of 2 channels, it is **NOT** ordinary graph coloring. The formula $\vert c(u) - c(v) \vert \geq 2$ must be used, not $c(u) \neq c(v)$.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–2 marks):** Failing to identify the maximum clique before applying Brooks' theorem or attempting a coloring. The clique bound is your first step in every problem.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–3 marks):** Reporting the chromatic number without proving that a smaller number of colors is impossible. You must show $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G) \geq k$ before showing a $k$-coloring exists.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (–1 mark):** Not drawing the conflict graph. Even a rough sketch earns you the diagram mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic:**

- **FAP** reduces to **vertex coloring**; the chromatic number $\chi(G)$ is the minimum channels.
- **L(h, k) labeling** is the modern variant of FAP used in cellular networks; the objective is to minimize the **span** $\lambda_h^k(G)$.
- **L(2, 1) labeling** constraint: distance 1 needs separation $\geq 2$, distance 2 needs separation $\geq 1$.
- **T-coloring** allows custom *taboo sets* on each edge; ordinary graph coloring is the special case $T = \{1\}$.
- **List coloring** restricts each vertex to a private palette (used in cognitive radio networks).
- **Brooks' theorem** gives $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G)$ unless $G$ is $K_n$ or an odd cycle.
- **Lower bound:** always compute $\omega(G)$ first; $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$.
- **Examination scheduling** is the textbook scheduling application of vertex coloring.
- **Edge coloring** with $\chi'(G)$ colors solves parallel-machine scheduling.
- **Vizing's theorem:** $\chi'(G) \in \{\Delta(G), \Delta(G) + 1\}$ (Class 1 or Class 2).
- **Compiler register allocation** uses interference graph coloring (Chaitin's algorithm).
- **NP-hardness:** vertex coloring, L(h,k), and FAP are all NP-hard in general.
- **Polynomial cases:** trees, bipartite graphs, interval graphs, chordal graphs, outerplanar graphs.
- **Engineering industries:** Telecom (Ericsson, Nokia), Compilers (GCC, LLVM), Universities (FET software), Airlines (crew rostering).
- **Span formula for K_n in L(2,1):** $\lambda_2^1(K_n) = 2n - 1$.
- **Span formula for paths in L(2,1):** $\lambda_2^1(P_n) = 4$ for $n \geq 4$.
- **Maximum degree bound on L(2,1) span:** $\lambda_2^1(G) \leq \Delta(G)^2 + 2\Delta(G)$ (Griggs-Yeh conjecture proves tighter bound $\Delta^2 + \Delta$ is conjectured).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assignment: Job Assignment Optimization using Edmonds' Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assignment: Job Assignment Optimization using Edmonds' Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edmonds' Algorithm (1965)**, also called the **Blossom Algorithm**, is a polynomial-time procedure that computes a **Maximum Cardinality Matching (MCM)** in a **general (non-bipartite) graph** $G = (V, E)$. A *matching* is a subset $M \subseteq E$ such that no two edges in $M$ share a common endpoint; a *maximum* matching is one whose cardinality cannot be increased by adding another edge.

For a **Job Assignment Optimization** problem, we model workers, jobs, and skill/temporal constraints as vertices and edges of a graph $G$. When the constraint graph is **non-bipartite** (i.e., contains **odd cycles** — for example, when workers may rotate between roles, or when multi-skilled jobs share sub-tasks), the classical Hungarian/Hopcroft–Karp methods fail because they assume bipartite structure. Edmonds' Blossom Algorithm is the canonical remedy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Berge's Theorem (1957):** A matching $M$ in $G$ is maximum *if and only if* $G$ contains **no $M$-augmenting path**. Edmonds' algorithm repeatedly searches for, and exploits, such paths while surgically removing odd cycles (called **blossoms**) that would otherwise obscure them.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **placement coordinator** at an engineering college who must assign final-year project groups to industry mentors. Each student can work with only one mentor, each mentor can guide only one group, and *some mentors are interchangeable* — meaning a mentor and a backup-mentor can swap roles mid-project. This swap chain can form a triangle (mentor A ↔ student X ↔ mentor B ↔ student Y ↔ mentor A) — a **3-cycle** — which standard bipartite matching cannot handle.

The Blossom Algorithm proceeds like a **persistent placement officer**:
1. **Greedily pair** students with mentors (initial matching $M$).
2. **Probe outward** from an unpaired (free) student along alternating *unassigned / assigned* linkages.
3. If a free mentor is reached, **commit the new pairing** (augment $M$).
4. If an *odd cycle* of length 5, 7, 9, … is discovered, **collapse it into a single super-mentor** (blossom contraction), continue probing, then **re-expand** the cycle at the end.

The result is a maximum number of successfully matched student–mentor pairs — equivalent to the largest job assignment that respects all constraints.

## 1.3 Job Assignment Optimization — Mathematical Setup

Given a general graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V = W \cup J$ (workers $W$, jobs $J$), with possible auxiliary dependency vertices,
- $E$ encodes feasible (worker, job) assignments *and* internal dependency edges that may produce **odd cycles**,

the optimization is:

$$\nu(G) \;=\; \max_{M \subseteq E} \; \vert M \vert \quad \text{subject to:} \quad \forall\, u \in V:\ \deg_M(u) \le 1$$

The quantity $\nu(G)$ is the **matching number** — the largest feasible job-to-worker assignment count.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick sanity check:** If $G$ is bipartite, $\nu(G)$ is reachable by Hopcroft–Karp in $O(\sqrt{V}\,E)$. If $G$ contains **any odd cycle**, only Edmonds' algorithm guarantees a polynomial-time solution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Building Blocks

The Blossom Algorithm rests on five tightly coupled constructs. Each is required by the KTU 2024 Module-3 rubric.

### 2.1.1 Alternating and Augmenting Paths
- **Alternating path** $P$: edges of $P$ alternate between $E \setminus M$ and $M$.
- **Augmenting path**: an alternating path whose **both endpoints are unmatched** (free) vertices.
- **Length parity**: any augmenting path has an *odd* number of edges.

### 2.1.2 Blossoms (Odd Cycles)
A **blossom** $B$ with **base** $v_0$ is an odd-length cycle in the alternating BFS tree where the root $v_0 \in B$ is the unique vertex of $B$ closest to the BFS root on the alternating tree, and every alternate vertex of $B$ (other than $v_0$) is matched within $B$.

$$B \;=\; \{v_0, v_1, \dots, v_{2k}\} \quad \text{with} \quad (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E \quad \text{and} \quad \vert B \vert \text{ odd}$$

### 2.1.3 Shrinking (Contraction)
Replace $B$ by a single **super-vertex** $b$, and replace every edge $(u, v)$ with $u \in B,\ v \notin B$ by $(b, v)$. The matching restricted to $B$ collapses naturally because $v_0$ is free.

### 2.1.4 Lifting (Expansion)
After an augmenting path $P'$ is found in the *contracted* graph $G/B$, locate the unique edge $e \in P'$ that enters $B$ from outside. Traverse $B$ from $v_0$ to the inner endpoint of $e$ along the **odd-length alternating sub-path**, producing the true augmenting path $P$ in $G$.

### 2.1.5 The Hungarian Labeling (BFS State)
Every explored vertex receives a triple:

$$\text{Label}(v) \;=\; (\text{class}, \text{parent}, \text{parentEdgeType})$$

where $\text{class} \in \{\text{E (even)}, \text{O (odd)}\}$ and $\text{parentEdgeType} \in \{M, \bar M\}$.

## 2.2 Algorithmic Phases (Operational Logic)

| Phase | Goal | Decision Logic |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Initialization** | Start from a free vertex $r$ | If none exists, $M$ is already maximum |
| **BFS Expansion** | Grow alternating tree $T$ rooted at $r$ | Labels assigned E/O alternately |
| **Augment Check** | Detect free vertex at E level | Symmetric-difference $M \leftarrow M \oplus P$ |
| **Blossom Detection** | Two E-vertices connected by an edge | Locate base via LCA, contract |
| **Termination** | No augmenting path exists in any contracted graph | Return $M$ as maximum |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table of Key Quantities, Conditions, and Complexity Bounds**

| Symbol / Concept | Definition / Formula | KTU Significance |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Matching number | $\nu(G) = \max \vert M \vert$ over all matchings | Output of the algorithm |
| Vertex cover number | $\tau(G) = \min \vert C \vert$ with $C$ covering all edges | König's theorem fails for non-bipartite |
| Tutte–Berge formula | $\nu(G) = \dfrac{1}{2}\big(\vert V \vert - \max_S \big(o(G \setminus S) - \vert S \vert\big)\big)$ | Characterizes optimum combinatorially |
| Augmenting path length | $\vert P \vert \in \{1, 3, 5, 7, \dots\}$ | Always odd |
| Blossom size | $\vert B \vert \in \{3, 5, 7, \dots\}$ | Always odd cycle length |
| Edmonds' time complexity | $O\big(\vert V \vert^2 \cdot \vert E \vert\big)$ | Original 1965 implementation |
| Micali–Vazirani bound | $O\big(\sqrt{\vert V \vert}\,\vert E \vert\big)$ | Optimal theoretical |
| Hopcroft–Karp (bipartite) | $O\big(\sqrt{\vert V \vert}\,\vert E \vert\big)$ | Faster, but bipartite only |
| Berge's condition | $M$ max $\iff$ no $M$-augmenting path | Algorithmic termination criterion |
| Path augmentation gain | $\vert M \oplus P \vert = \vert M \vert + 1$ | Each iteration increases size by exactly 1 |
| Hungarian cost | $\displaystyle \min \sum_{(i,j) \in M} c_{ij}$ | When all weights are unit, reduces to MCM |

> [!TIP]
> **Avoid the trap:** König's theorem $\nu(G) = \tau(G)$ is valid **only for bipartite graphs**. In non-bipartite graphs, $\nu(G) < \tau(G)$ is possible — this is precisely why Edmonds' algorithm must handle blossoms.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Crew rostering in airlines / railways** — non-bipartite due to qualification cross-overs.
- **VLSI channel routing** — pin pairing with shared nets can form odd cycles.
- **DNA fragment assembly** — overlapping read pairs.
- **Network design** — pairing redundant routers with cross-domain failover links.
- **Hospital-resident assignment** — non-bipartite when residents can swap specialty blocks.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (Job Assignment Scenario)

**Scenario:** Six entities are involved in a job cluster.

| Label | Entity | Role |
| --- | --- | --- |
| $v_1$ | Worker-1 | Trainee engineer |
| $v_2$ | Worker-2 | Senior engineer (free) |
| $v_3$ | Job-A | Database migration |
| $v_4$ | Job-B | API integration |
| $v_5$ | Job-C | Testing (assigned to $v_1$) |
| $v_6$ | Job-D | Deployment (free) |

Edges (feasibility + cross-dependencies):
$E = \{ (v_1,v_2),\, (v_1,v_5),\, (v_2,v_3),\, (v_3,v_4),\, (v_4,v_5),\, (v_4,v_6) \}$

Initial matching: $M_0 = \{ (v_1,v_5),\ (v_3,v_4) \}$.

**Free vertices:** $v_2$ and $v_6$.

> [!NOTE]
> This graph contains the **odd cycle** $v_2$–$v_1$–$v_5$–$v_4$–$v_3$–$v_2$ (length **5**), guaranteeing that a blossom will appear during BFS.

---

### 3.2 Phase 1 — Alternating BFS from $v_2$

Root the BFS at the free vertex $v_2$. Label $v_2$ as **E (even)**, level 0.

$$
\text{Level 0:}\quad \text{Label}(v_2) = (\text{E},\ \text{null},\ -)
$$

Expand via **unmatched** edges from $v_2$:
- $(v_2, v_1)$ — unmatched $\Rightarrow$ label $v_1$ as **O**, parent $v_2$.
- $(v_2, v_3)$ — unmatched $\Rightarrow$ label $v_3$ as **O**, parent $v_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 1:}\quad &\text{Label}(v_1) = (\text{O},\ v_2,\ \bar M) \\
&\text{Label}(v_3) = (\text{O},\ v_2,\ \bar M)
\end{aligned}
$$

From each **O**-vertex, follow the unique **matched** edge:
- $(v_1, v_5) \in M \Rightarrow$ label $v_5$ as **E**, parent $v_1$.
- $(v_3, v_4) \in M \Rightarrow$ label $v_4$ as **E**, parent $v_3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 2:}\quad &\text{Label}(v_5) = (\text{E},\ v_1,\ M) \\
&\text{Label}(v_4) = (\text{E},\ v_3,\ M)
\end{aligned}
$$

From $v_5$ and $v_4$ (both **E**), expand along unmatched edges:
- $(v_5, v_4)$ — unmatched. **$v_4$ is already E** ⇒ *blossom candidate*.
- $(v_4, v_6)$ — unmatched. $v_6$ is **free** $\Rightarrow$ label $v_6$ as **O**, parent $v_4$.

$$
\text{Level 3:}\quad \text{Label}(v_6) = (\text{O},\ v_4,\ \bar M)
$$

### 3.3 Phase 2 — Blossom Detection

We have two E-labeled vertices $v_5$ and $v_4$ connected by an *unmatched* edge. Trace back to their **lowest common ancestor (LCA)**:

- $v_5 \leftarrow v_1 \leftarrow v_2$ (root)
- $v_4 \leftarrow v_3 \leftarrow v_2$ (root)

**LCA** $= v_2$ (the root). Hence the **blossom** $B$ has base $v_2$ and consists of all vertices on the two paths from $v_2$ to $v_5$ and $v_4$, plus the closing edge $(v_5, v_4)$.

$$
B \;=\; \{v_2,\, v_1,\, v_5,\, v_4,\, v_3\}
$$

The defining cycle is:
$$
v_2 \xrightarrow{\bar M} v_1 \xrightarrow{M} v_5 \xrightarrow{\bar M} v_4 \xrightarrow{M} v_3 \xrightarrow{\bar M} v_2
$$

Length $= 5$ (odd) — confirmed as a blossom.

### 3.4 Phase 3 — Contraction $G \mapsto G/B$

Replace $B$ by super-vertex $b$. The only edge with exactly one endpoint in $B$ is $(v_4, v_6)$, so:

$$
G/B \;=\; \big(\{b,\, v_6\},\ \{(b, v_6)\}\big)
$$

The matching inside $B$ collapses (since the base $v_2$ was free):
$$
M/B \;=\; \varnothing
$$

### 3.5 Phase 4 — Augmenting Path in $G/B$

In $G/B$, the unique path $b$–$v_6$ is itself augmenting (both endpoints free). Apply:

$$
M/B \;\leftarrow\; (M/B) \oplus \{(b, v_6)\} \;=\; \{(b, v_6)\}
$$

### 3.6 Phase 5 — Lifting (Expansion)

The augmenting path enters $B$ through edge $(v_4, v_6)$. Inside $B$, find the alternating sub-path from base $v_2$ to $v_4$ that **ends with the matched edge $(v_3, v_4)$** (so it composes with $(v_4, v_6)$ into a proper alternating chain):

$$
v_2 \xrightarrow{\bar M} v_3 \xrightarrow{M} v_4 \xrightarrow{\bar M} v_6
$$

This sub-path has 2 internal edges + 1 external = alternating structure U-M-U. ✓

### 3.7 Phase 6 — Symmetric-Difference Augmentation in $G$

Original matching: $M_0 = \{ (v_1,v_5),\ (v_3,v_4) \}$.

Augmenting path edges:
$$
P \;=\; \{(v_2,v_3),\ (v_3,v_4),\ (v_4,v_6)\}
$$

Apply:
$$
\begin{aligned}
M^\star \;&=\; M_0 \oplus P \\
&=\; \big\{(v_1,v_5),\ (v_3,v_4)\big\} \,\triangle\, \big\{(v_2,v_3),\ (v_3,v_4),\ (v_4,v_6)\big\} \\
&=\; \big\{(v_1,v_5),\ (v_2,v_3),\ (v_4,v_6)\big\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.8 Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check disjointness:} \quad &(v_1,v_5),\ (v_2,v_3),\ (v_4,v_6) \\
&\text{Endpoints: } \{v_1,v_5\}\cup\{v_2,v_3\}\cup\{v_4,v_6\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{all 6 vertices distinct.} \\
\text{Cardinality:} \quad &\vert M^\star \vert \;=\; 3 \;=\; \big\lfloor \vert V \vert / 2 \big\rfloor \;=\; \text{perfect matching.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Job Assignment:**
> - Worker-1 ($v_1$) → Job-C ($v_5$)
> - Worker-2 ($v_2$) → Job-A ($v_3$)
> - Job-B ($v_4$) → Job-D ($v_6$) (cross-task dependency resolved)
>
> All 6 entities are utilized — the blossom trick unlocked a perfect pairing that a naïve greedy assignment would have missed.

### 3.9 Analytical Decision Matrix (Why Each Choice Was Made)

| Step | Alternatives Considered | Why We Chose the Selected One |
| --- | --- | --- |
| BFS root | Start from $v_6$ vs $v_2$ | Both valid; starting from $v_2$ exposes blossom immediately |
| Path inside blossom $B$ | $v_2$–$v_1$–$v_5$–$v_4$ (3 edges) vs $v_2$–$v_3$–$v_4$ (2 edges) | Selected the one **ending with matched edge at $v_4$**, ensuring alternation with $(v_4, v_6)$ |
| Contraction target | Whole $B$ vs sub-cycle | Contract only the full blossom to preserve maximum-matching equivalence |
| Termination | Augmentation found vs no augmenting path | Augmentation found $\Rightarrow$ restart BFS from a new free vertex (none remain) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Graph G and Matching M]) --> chkFree{Free vertex r exists?}
    chkFree -- No --> outMax([Output: M is maximum])
    chkFree -- Yes --> bfsInit[BFS from r using alternating edges]
    bfsInit --> chkAug{Free E-vertex found?}
    chkAug -- Yes --> augStep[Symmetric difference: M = M xor P]
    augStep --> chkFree
    chkAug -- No --> chkBloom{Two E-vertices adjacent?}
    chkBloom -- Yes --> contract[Identify blossom B via LCA]
    contract --> shrink[Contract B to super-vertex b]
    shrink --> recurseB[Recurse: BFS in contracted graph G/B]
    recurseB --> chkFree
    chkBloom -- No --> exploreMore{Unlabeled neighbor reachable?}
    exploreMore -- Yes --> bfsInit
    exploreMore -- No --> outMax
```

## 4.2 Original Graph $G$ (Worked Example)

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1((v1)) --- v2((v2))
    v1((v1)) --- v5((v5))
    v2((v2)) --- v3((v3))
    v3((v3)) --- v4((v4))
    v4((v4)) --- v5((v5))
    v4((v4)) --- v6((v6))
```

> **Legend:** Solid lines = edges in $E$. In the worked example, edges $(v_1,v_5)$ and $(v_3,v_4)$ form the initial matching $M_0$.

## 4.3 BFS Alternating Tree (after Phase 1)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root((v2 E root)) --- v1n((v1 O))
    root((v2 E root)) --- v3n((v3 O))
    v1n((v1 O)) --- v5n((v5 E))
    v3n((v3 O)) --- v4n((v4 E))
    v4n((v4 E)) --- v6n((v6 O free))
    v4n((v4 E)) -.- v5n((v5 E))
    v5n((v5 E)) -.- v4n((v4 E))
```

> The dashed connection between $v_5$ and $v_4$ (both E) triggers **blossom detection**; the unique edge into $v_6$ is a candidate for lifting.

## 4.4 Contracted Graph $G/B$

```mermaid
graph LR
    superB((B blossom)) --- v6c((v6 free))
```

> Trivial structure: a single edge between super-vertex $B$ (free, since base $v_2$ was free) and $v_6$ (free) — immediately an augmenting path.

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1f((v1)) --- v5f((v5))
    v2f((v2)) --- v3f((v3))
    v4f((v4)) --- v6f((v6))
```

> Three disjoint edges — a **perfect matching** of size 3 — confirming the maximum job-assignment count for the scenario.
