# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define Edmonds' Blossom Algorithm. State the precise problem it solves and mention its asymptotic time complexity.

**Model Answer:**
Edmonds' Blossom Algorithm (1965) finds a **Maximum Cardinality Matching (MCM)** in a **general (non-bipartite) graph** $G = (V, E)$ in polynomial time. The matching number $\nu(G)$ is the size of the largest edge-disjoint subset of $E$. The original algorithm runs in $O(\vert V \vert^2 \cdot \vert E \vert)$; the Micali–Vazirani variant achieves the optimal $O(\sqrt{\vert V \vert}\,\vert E \vert)$. The algorithm repeatedly searches for $M$-augmenting paths via alternating BFS, contracting any odd cycle (**blossom**) encountered, until none exists — at which point Berge's theorem certifies $M$ as maximum.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Sessional-II]**
What is a *blossom*? Why does it cause trouble for bipartite matching techniques?

**Model Answer:**
A **blossom** is an **odd cycle** $B$ of length $3, 5, 7, \dots$ in the graph such that during an alternating BFS, all but one vertex (the **base**) of $B$ are matched among themselves. The base is the unique vertex of $B$ reachable from the BFS root via the shortest alternating path. Blossoms defeat bipartite techniques because **König's theorem fails** on non-bipartite graphs: $\nu(G) \ne \tau(G)$ in general, and any augmenting path that *passes through* the blossom would have its parity flipped upon entering and exiting, so it appears "blocked" to algorithms that assume the graph is 2-colorable. Edmonds' algorithm overcomes this by **contracting** the blossom into a single vertex, recursively solving, then **lifting** the augmenting path back through the cycle using the unique edge that alternates correctly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

#### (a) Explain the Blossom Algorithm step-by-step with the role of Berge's theorem and the blossom contraction–lifting procedure. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pre-requisites (Berge's Theorem):**
A matching $M$ in $G = (V, E)$ is maximum if and only if there exists **no** $M$-augmenting path. This converts the global "find the largest matching" question into the local "find any augmenting path" sub-problem.

**Step 2 — Initialization:**
Start with an arbitrary matching $M$ (e.g., greedy). Maintain a queue of free vertices as BFS seeds.

**Step 3 — Alternating BFS:**
From each free vertex $r$, run a BFS that alternates between unmatched ($\bar M$) and matched ($M$) edges. Label vertices as **E (even)** when reached via an unmatched edge and **O (odd)** when reached via a matched edge. Record parent and the type of edge used.

**Step 4 — Augmentation Check:**
If a free vertex is reached at the **E** level, an augmenting path $P$ has been found. Update:
$$M \;\leftarrow\; M \oplus P$$
Then restart from any remaining free vertex.

**Step 5 — Blossom Detection:**
If two **E**-level vertices $u$ and $v$ are adjacent, an odd cycle exists. Find their **lowest common ancestor** $b$ in the BFS tree; the blossom consists of all vertices on the two paths from $b$ to $u$ and $b$ to $v$, plus the edge $(u, v)$.

**Step 6 — Contraction:**
Collapse the blossom into a super-vertex, preserving edges crossing the boundary. Recurse to Step 3 on the smaller graph.

**Step 7 — Lifting:**
Once augmentation succeeds in the contracted graph, trace the path back through the blossom: from the base $b$, walk along the alternating sub-cycle to the vertex incident to the edge that exits the blossom. The composition is the augmenting path in $G$.

**Step 8 — Termination:**
When no free vertex remains or no augmenting path can be found in any contracted graph, return $M$. By Berge's theorem, $M$ is maximum.

**Complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert^2 \cdot \vert E \vert)$ for the original implementation.

#### (b) Apply Edmonds' Algorithm to the graph $G = (\{1,2,3,4,5,6\},\, \{(1,2),(1,5),(2,3),(3,4),(4,5),(4,6)\})$ with $M_0 = \{(1,5),(3,4)\}$. Show all augmenting steps and state the final matching. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:** *(Identical to the canonical worked example in Section 3 of these notes.)*

| Phase | Action | Outcome |
| --- | --- | --- |
| BFS from 2 | Alternating labels E/O/M | Levels: 2(E), 1(O),3(O), 5(E),4(E), 6(O) |
| Blossom detection | Edge (4,5) joins two E-vertices; LCA is 2 | $B = \{1,2,3,4,5\}$, base $= 2$ |
| Contraction | $G/B$ has vertices $\{B,6\}$ and edge $(B,6)$ | $M/B = \varnothing$ |
| Augment in $G/B$ | Single edge $B$–6 is augmenting | $M/B = \{(B,6)\}$ |
| Lifting | Use path $2$–$3$–$4$ inside blossom (alternating U-M) | Augmenting path $P = 2$-$3$-$4$-$6$ |
| Symmetric difference | $M^\star = \{(1,5),(2,3),(4,6)\}$ | Perfect matching, $\vert M^\star \vert = 3$ |

**Final answer:** $M^\star = \{(1,5),\, (2,3),\, (4,6)\}$ — a perfect matching of size **3**.

---

### ⭐ Question B — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Alternative]**

#### (a) Discuss the connection between Berge's theorem, alternating paths, and the correctness of Edmonds' algorithm. Why is Berge's theorem **insufficient on its own** for non-bipartite graphs? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Berge's Theorem (1957):**
For any matching $M$ in a graph $G$, $M$ is maximum $\iff$ $G$ contains no $M$-augmenting path. This gives a *necessary and sufficient condition* but is **purely existential** — it does not provide a procedure to find such paths efficiently.

**Why it works in bipartite graphs:**
In bipartite $G = (U, V, E)$, every alternating path has endpoints in different parts. Hence any alternating path's length parity is fixed, and augmenting paths can be found via Hopcroft–Karp layered BFS in $O(\sqrt{\vert V \vert}\,\vert E \vert)$. König's duality also gives $\nu = \tau$, enabling vertex-cover-based verification.

**Why it is insufficient in general graphs:**
In a non-bipartite graph, an alternating walk can revisit vertices and **even-length cycles** can be entered and exited indefinitely. More critically, **odd cycles** can produce configurations where a free vertex is reachable from the root only by *passing through* an odd cycle — but every such passage would either:
- Re-encounter the root's blossom (parity inversion), or
- Create an "even-length alternating walk" that ends at an O-vertex instead of an E-vertex (which is not augmenting).

Hence Berge's condition may hold in $G$ yet no *vertex-simple* augmenting path may exist at the surface level. Edmonds' insight was to **observe that what appears blocked is, after blossom contraction, an *augmenting path in a smaller graph* satisfying Berge's condition**. Thus Berge's theorem remains the correctness anchor — but its witness is sought in *contracted* graphs.

**Conclusion:** Edmonds' algorithm is essentially "Berge's theorem with a recursive shrinking mechanism" — the correctness follows because (i) shrinking preserves the matching number and (ii) any augmenting path found in $G/B$ lifts to one in $G$.

#### (b) A job assignment has 5 workers $\{W_1,\dots,W_5\}$ and 4 jobs $\{J_1,\dots,J_4\}$. Edges (feasibility + cross-skills) are: $(W_1,J_1),\,(W_1,J_2),\,(W_2,J_1),\,(W_2,J_2),\,(W_2,J_3),\,(W_3,J_3),\,(W_3,J_4),\,(W_4,J_2),\,(W_4,J_4),\,(W_5,J_3),\,(W_5,J_4)$. Initial matching $M_0 = \{(W_1,J_1),(W_3,J_3)\}$. Find the **maximum** assignment using Edmonds' algorithm. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

> **Note:** Inspection shows this graph is bipartite (workers $\cup$ jobs with no edges inside either part) ⇒ no blossom appears. Edmonds' reduces to standard alternating BFS.

Free vertices: $W_2, W_4, W_5, J_2, J_4$.

**BFS from $W_2$ (free, label E):**
- Unmatched edge $(W_2, J_2)$: $J_2$ labeled O.
- $J_2$ has matched partner $W_4$ (assuming we extend $M_0$ later); but $W_4$ is currently free. So $J_2$ O has no matched edge to push. Restart needed.

**BFS from $W_4$ (free, label E):**
- Unmatched edge $(W_4, J_4)$: $J_4$ labeled O.
- $J_4$ has matched partner $W_3$ via $\cdots$ — actually $W_3$ matched to $J_3$, not $J_4$. So $J_4$ O is dead-end here.

**Augment directly:** Pair $W_4$–$J_4$.

Updated $M_1 = \{(W_1,J_1),(W_3,J_3),(W_4,J_4)\}$.

**BFS from $W_2$ again:**
- $(W_2, J_2)$ unmatched: $J_2$ labeled O.
- $J_2$ matched to $W_4$ (now in $M_1$? No — we just added $W_4$–$J_4$, not $W_4$–$J_2$.) So $J_2$ has no matched edge. Dead-end.
- $(W_2, J_3)$ unmatched: but $J_3$ is matched to $W_3$. $J_3$ labeled O.
- $J_3$ O has matched edge $(W_3, J_3)$: $W_3$ labeled E, parent $J_3$.
- $W_3$ E: unmatched edge $(W_3, J_4)$: $J_4$ labeled O. $J_4$ is already matched (to $W_4$), so $J_4$ O has matched edge $(W_4, J_4)$: $W_4$ labeled E, parent $J_4$.
- $W_4$ E: unmatched edge $(W_4, J_2)$: $J_2$ already O. Skip.

No free vertex reached at E level. But wait — $W_2$ is the *root* (free). So path $W_2$–$J_3$–$W_3$–$J_4$–$W_4$–$J_2$ ends at $J_2$ which is *not* free (it's reached via $(W_4, J_2)$). Not augmenting.

**Try BFS from $W_5$:**
- Unmatched $(W_5, J_3)$: $J_3$ labeled O. Already explored above.
- $J_3$ O $\to W_3$ E via $(W_3, J_3)$.
- $W_3$ E $\to J_4$ O via $(W_3, J_4)$. $J_4$ matched $\to W_4$ E.
- $W_4$ E $\to J_2$ O via $(W_4, J_2)$. $J_2$ matched? Not yet in $M_1$.
- $J_2$ has no matched edge. Dead-end.

Restart BFS: $J_2$ is free, label $J_2$ as E root.
- Unmatched $(W_1, J_2)$: $W_1$ labeled O. $W_1$ matched to $J_1$ via $(W_1, J_1)$: $J_1$ labeled E.
- $J_1$ E: unmatched $(W_2, J_1)$: $W_2$ labeled O. $W_2$ free ⇒ **FOUND AUGMENTING PATH** $J_2$–$W_1$–$J_1$–$W_2$ (length 3, alternating U-M-U).

**Augment:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
M_2 \;&=\; M_1 \oplus \{(J_2,W_1),\,(W_1,J_1),\,(J_1,W_2)\} \\
      &=\; \{(W_1,J_1),(W_3,J_3),(W_4,J_4)\} \oplus \{(W_1,J_2),(W_1,J_1),(W_2,J_1)\} \\
      &=\; \{(W_3,J_3),(W_4,J_4),(W_1,J_2),(W_2,J_1)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Free vertices remaining: $W_5, J_3$ (since $J_3$ is matched to $W_3$). Actually all workers except $W_5$ are matched; jobs $J_1, J_2, J_4$ matched, $J_3$ matched.

**Final check:** $\vert M_2 \vert = 4$, $\vert V \vert = 9$. No more augmenting paths exist.

$$
\boxed{M^\star \;=\; \{(W_1,J_2),\,(W_2,J_1),\,(W_3,J_3),\,(W_4,J_4)\}}
$$

Worker $W_5$ remains unassigned — no job feasible from the remaining options because $J_3$ is already taken. Maximum job assignment size = **4**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing matching with vertex cover:** König's equality $\nu = \tau$ holds **only for bipartite** graphs. Writing it for non-bipartite $G$ will cost 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Wrong parity in lifting:** When lifting the augmenting path through a blossom, students often choose the *even-length* path inside the blossom instead of the *odd-length* one, breaking alternation. Always verify the last edge of the inner sub-path is matched if the bridge edge is unmatched (and vice versa).
> 3. **Forgetting to restart BFS after augmentation:** Once $M$ is augmented, the previous labels are stale. Re-running BFS from a new free vertex is mandatory.
> 4. **Mixing up $M \oplus P$ (symmetric difference) with $M \cup P$:** The former is correct; the latter produces an invalid multi-graph with shared vertices.
> 5. **Skipping the "free E-vertex" check:** A free vertex reached as **O** is *not* augmenting — only free vertices reached at **E** level (including the root) certify an augmenting path.
> 6. **Ignoring the second free vertex:** After exhausting BFS from one free vertex, always try the remaining free vertices before declaring $M$ maximum.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 High-Density Bullet Summary — Print Before Exam**

- **Algorithm name:** Edmonds' Blossom Algorithm (1965).
- **Problem solved:** Maximum Cardinality Matching in **general (non-bipartite)** graphs.
- **Foundational theorem:** **Berge (1957)** — $M$ max $\iff$ no $M$-augmenting path.
- **Key construct 1 — Alternating path:** edges alternate $E\setminus M$, $M$, $E\setminus M$, …
- **Key construct 2 — Augmenting path:** alternating path whose *both* endpoints are free vertices; has **odd length**.
- **Key construct 3 — Blossom:** odd cycle in the alternating BFS tree, identified when two E-vertices share an edge; **base** = lowest common ancestor in BFS tree.
- **Operation 1 — Contraction:** collapse blossom $B$ to super-vertex $b$; preserves matching number.
- **Operation 2 — Lifting:** trace back from base along alternating sub-cycle to bridge edge, then complete augmenting path.
- **Symmetric difference update:** $M \leftarrow M \oplus P$ grows matching by exactly 1.
- **Time complexity:** Original Edmonds' $O(n^2 m)$; Micali–Vazirani $O(\sqrt{n}\,m)$.
- **Bipartite special case:** When $G$ is bipartite, no blossoms occur; reduces to Hopcroft–Karp.
- **König's theorem** $\nu = \tau$: **bipartite only**; fails in general graphs.
- **Tutte–Berge formula:** $\nu(G) = \tfrac12(\vert V \vert - \max_S(o(G\setminus S) - \vert S \vert))$ — useful as a *theoretical* check.
- **Job Assignment mapping:** workers $\cup$ jobs $\cup$ cross-tasks $\to$ general graph; Edmonds' yields maximum feasible assignment.
- **Examiner's hot keywords:** *Berge's theorem, augmenting path, blossom, contraction, lifting, symmetric difference, König's theorem limitation, MCM*.
- **Most common error:** choosing wrong parity during lifting — always end the inner blossom path on a matched edge iff the bridge edge is unmatched.
- **Always restart BFS** after every augmentation; stale labels are invalid.
- **Always try every free vertex** as BFS root before terminating.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assignment: Frequency Assignment using Graph Coloring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assignment: Frequency Assignment using Graph Coloring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Frequency Assignment Problem (FAP): Formal Definition

The **Frequency Assignment Problem (FAP)** is a well-known combinatorial optimization problem in wireless communications engineering. It asks:

> *Given a set of transmitters (or radio cells) and a limited spectrum of available frequencies, assign a frequency to every transmitter such that electromagnetic interference is minimised or eliminated, while satisfying engineering and regulatory constraints.*

When formulated mathematically, FAP maps directly onto the **Graph Coloring Problem**. The mapping is one of the most elegant and industrially important applications of graph theory.

**Formal Mapping:**
- **Vertices** $\to$ **Transmitters / Radio Cells / Communication Nodes**
- **Edges** $\to$ **Interference relationships** (two transmitters that interfere with each other if they are too close, geographically or topologically)
- **Colors** $\to$ **Available frequencies** (channel numbers)

A proper graph coloring of the interference graph then corresponds to a *feasible* frequency plan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECST595 / Module 3):**  
> The Frequency Assignment Problem is listed as a canonical *application* of vertex coloring. Examiners expect students to (a) model a given FAP as a graph, (b) apply a coloring heuristic, (c) report the minimum number of frequencies (chromatic number $\chi(G)$) and (d) explain the type of constraints handled.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Radio Station" Picture

Imagine you are the chief engineer of a city that must license **AM radio stations**. The electromagnetic spectrum of your region is divided into a fixed pool of frequency "slots" (say, the integers $\{1, 2, 3, \ldots, k\}$). You must assign **one slot to each station**.

Now, two stations that broadcast on **identical** frequencies and whose antennas are physically close will produce *co-channel interference* — a loud whistle/buzz that ruins both signals. Similarly, stations whose frequencies are **very close** (say, differ by only 1 or 2) but are neighbours geographically cause *adjacent-channel interference*.

So your job is:

> *Colour each station with a number (frequency) such that neighbouring stations get numbers that differ by at least some minimum gap, using as few distinct numbers (frequencies) as possible.*

This is *exactly* a graph coloring problem with constraints on the **label differences** across edges.

## 1.3 Engineering Origin & Real-World Importance

The FAP has industrial significance in:

| Domain | Why Frequency Assignment Matters |
|---|---|
| **Mobile Cellular Networks (GSM, LTE, 5G NR)** | Reuse of limited spectrum across cells without co-channel/adjacent-channel interference |
| **FM / AM Radio Broadcasting** | Prevent inter-station whistles in dense urban spectra |
| **Satellite Communication** | Avoid uplink/downlink crosstalk |
| **Military / Tactical Radio** | Spectrum deconfliction in battlefield RF environments |
| **Wi-Fi (2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz bands)** | Channel planning in dense deployments (airports, stadiums) |
| **Cognitive Radio / DSA** | Dynamic spectrum assignment to secondary users |

> [!NOTE]
> In modern 4G/5G deployments, the simplified model $\chi(G)$ (basic vertex coloring) is often **extended** to a *T-coloring* or *list-coloring* to handle adjacent-channel constraints, where vertices carry additional lists of forbidden separations.

## 1.4 The Three Standard Constraint Classes

A textbook FAP introduces three constraint levels:

1. **Hard Co-channel Constraint (Edge Separation = 0 forbidden)**:  
   Two interfering transmitters **must not** share the same frequency.  
   Mathematically: if $(u, v) \in E$, then $|f(u) - f(v)| \neq 0$, i.e. $f(u) \neq f(v)$.

2. **Adjacent-channel Constraint (Edge Separation $\geq d$)**:  
   Two interfering transmitters must differ by **at least** $d$ frequency units.  
   Mathematically: $|f(u) - f(v)| \geq d_{uv}$ for every interfering pair.

3. **Co-site Constraint**:  
   A single transmitter site uses multiple frequencies on a single mast; the frequencies used at the *same* site must be sufficiently separated (typically a large value, e.g. $\geq 5$) to avoid internal interference from non-linear amplifiers.

These map directly onto classical coloring variants:
- Class 1 $\Rightarrow$ **Vertex Coloring** (chromatic number $\chi(G)$).
- Class 2 $\Rightarrow$ **T-Coloring** (T is a set of forbidden differences per edge).
- Class 3 $\Rightarrow$ **List Coloring with Pre-assigned Site Lists**.

## 1.5 The Key Performance Metric: Chromatic Number

The number of frequencies required by the *optimal* (minimum-frequency) plan equals the **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$ of the interference graph.

By the **Four Color Theorem** (Appel & Haken, 1976) and its generalisations:

$$\chi(G) \leq 4 \quad \text{for any planar graph } G.$$

But real interference graphs are **not** always planar (consider long-haul microwave links), so the bound may rise. For arbitrary graphs:

$$\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1,$$

where $\Delta(G)$ is the maximum degree — **Brooks' Theorem** (1941) refines this bound further.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Model of the FAP

Let:
- $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ be the set of transmitters.
- $f : V \to \mathbb{Z}^+$ be a frequency assignment function.
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ be the set of interference pairs (edges in the interference graph $G = (V, E)$).
- For each edge $e = (u, v)$, a forbidden-difference set $T_e \subset \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ is given. Typically $0 \in T_e$ (co-channel forbidden) and may include other small integers (adjacent-channel forbidden).

The **Minimum Span FAP** is then:

$$\text{Minimise } \quad \max_{v \in V} f(v) - \min_{v \in V} f(v)$$

$$\text{subject to} \quad |f(u) - f(v)| \notin T_{uv}, \quad \forall (u, v) \in E.$$

This is **NP-hard** in general (Hale, 1980), which is exactly why graph coloring heuristics are used.

## 2.2 Reduction to Standard Graph Coloring

If every $T_{uv} = \{0\}$ (only co-channel forbidden), the FAP is precisely **Vertex Coloring**:

> *Assign to each vertex the smallest possible positive integer such that no two adjacent vertices share an integer.*

The minimum number of distinct integers needed is $\chi(G)$.

If $T_{uv}$ contains additional values (e.g. $\{0, 1, 2\}$), the problem becomes a **T-Coloring**, which is harder but closer to engineering reality.

## 2.3 Types of Coloring Variants Used in FAP

| Variant | Definition | Engineering Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertex Coloring** | Adjacent vertices differ in color | Pure co-channel interference |
| **Edge Coloring** | Incidence edges at a vertex differ | Multi-radio assignment on one tower |
| **List Coloring** | Each vertex has its own permitted color list | Pre-allocated operator bands |
| **T-Coloring** | Forbidden difference sets on edges | Adjacent-channel interference modelling |
| **L(p, q)-Labeling** | Difference at distance 1 is $\geq p$, at distance 2 is $\geq q$ | Two-tier interference (cell + neighbour-of-neighbour) |
| **Distance-2 Coloring** | Adjacent AND vertices at distance 2 differ | Strong co-channel + neighbour-cell protection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU Module 3 viva, the most-likely question variation is: *"Convert the given transmitter network into an interference graph and apply Welsh-Powell."* Be ready to draw the graph, identify $\chi(G)$, and write the assignment table.

## 2.4 Core Algorithms for the FAP

### 2.4.1 Greedy Coloring (First-Fit)

Visit vertices in some order; assign the **smallest colour not used by its already-coloured neighbours**.

- Time complexity: $O(V + E)$.
- Uses at most $\Delta(G) + 1$ colours.
- *Optimal orderings* (e.g. largest-degree-first) yield the **Welsh-Powell** algorithm.

### 2.4.2 Welsh-Powell Algorithm (1967)

1. Sort vertices in **non-increasing order of degree**.
2. Take the first (highest-degree) uncoloured vertex. Assign it **Colour 1**.
3. Scan the ordered list; assign Colour 1 to every vertex that is **not adjacent** to a vertex already coloured 1.
4. Repeat steps 2–3 with Colour 2, 3, … until all vertices are coloured.

### 2.4.3 DSATUR (Brélaz, 1979)

A smarter heuristic that, at each step, picks the vertex with the **highest saturation degree** — i.e. the vertex that already has the most distinct colours among its neighbours. Ties broken by total degree. Produces near-optimal results on many real benchmark graphs.

### 2.4.4 Frequency Assignment Heuristics

For T-coloring variants, specialised algorithms are used:
- **T-COLOR** (Costa, 1998) — greedy with forbidden-difference lists.
- **Tabu Search / Simulated Annealing** — meta-heuristics used for minimum-span FAP in real cellular networks.
- **Constraint Programming (CP)** — modern industrial solver approach.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\chi(G)$ | Chromatic number (minimum colours for $G$) | Dimensionless integer |
| $\omega(G)$ | Size of the maximum clique | Lower bound: $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$ |
| $\Delta(G)$ | Maximum vertex degree | Upper bound: $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$ |
| $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$ | Trivial upper bound (greedy) | Brooks' theorem: equality unless $G \cong K_n$ or odd cycle |
| $\chi(G) \leq 4$ | Four Color Theorem bound | Holds for planar graphs |
| $f : V \to \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Frequency assignment function | Integer frequency |
| $\vert f(u) - f(v) \vert \notin T_{uv}$ | T-coloring feasibility constraint | Per-edge forbidden difference set |
| $L(p, q)$-labeling span | $\max f - \min f$ under distance constraints | Integer |
| Cost = $\sum_{(u,v) \in E} w_{uv} \cdot \mathbb{1}\!\left[\vert f(u) - f(v) \vert \in T_{uv}\right]$ | Penalised interference objective | Often used in optimisation solvers |
| Span | $\text{span}(f) = \max_v f(v) - \min_v f(v)$ | Smaller span ⇒ efficient spectrum use |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Note for KTU:** In LaTeX table cells, always write `\vert f(u) - f(v) \vert` — never use `|f(u) - f(v)|` because the literal pipe character breaks the markdown table parser. This same rule applies to your answer sheets if you write LaTeX inside cells.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of the System

The FAP-graph-coloring reduction has three deep engineering implications:

1. **Spectrum is scarce** — usable radio spectrum is a finite public resource. Reducing the span by even **one frequency unit** may allow an additional user in a dense urban area. Optimising $\chi(G)$ literally translates to billions of rupees of operator revenue.

2. **Interference is non-linear** — practical receivers experience harmful interference from very-near frequencies (adjacent-channel) as well as identical ones. T-coloring captures this physics.

3. **Re-planning is expensive** — once a national cellular operator rolls out a frequency plan, re-assigning channels across millions of cells is a multi-month, multi-crore exercise. Hence *initial* planning relies on graph-theoretic optimisation, not trial-and-error.

The economic and engineering stakes make FAP-graph-coloring one of the highest-impact application areas of combinatorial optimisation in industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Modelling an FAP as a Graph

**Problem Statement (Sample):**

A regional telecom operator has **six transmitters** $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_5, T_6$ in a valley. Interference measurements produce the following pairs that cannot share a frequency:

$$\{T_1,T_2\},\ \{T_1,T_3\},\ \{T_1,T_4\},\ \{T_2,T_3\},\ \{T_2,T_5\},\ \{T_3,T_4\},\ \{T_3,T_6\},\ \{T_4,T_6\},\ \{T_5,T_6\}.$$

Find the **minimum number of frequencies** and assign them.

### Step 1 — Construct the interference graph $G = (V, E)$:

| Vertex | Degree | Adjacency list |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 3 | $T_2, T_3, T_4$ |
| $T_2$ | 3 | $T_1, T_3, T_5$ |
| $T_3$ | 4 | $T_1, T_2, T_4, T_6$ |
| $T_4$ | 3 | $T_1, T_3, T_6$ |
| $T_5$ | 2 | $T_2, T_6$ |
| $T_6$ | 3 | $T_3, T_4, T_5$ |

So $\Delta(G) = 4$ (achieved at $T_3$).

### Step 2 — Detect a Clique (Lower Bound)

Observe that $\{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4\}$ form a **clique of size 4** because every pair among them is connected:
- $(T_1, T_2) \in E$, $(T_1, T_3) \in E$, $(T_1, T_4) \in E$, $(T_2, T_3) \in E$, $(T_3, T_4) \in E$, and **$(T_2, T_4)$** is missing!

So $\{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4\}$ is **not** a 4-clique. Let us recheck carefully:

Edges among $\{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4\}$:
- $T_1 - T_2$ ✓
- $T_1 - T_3$ ✓
- $T_1 - T_4$ ✓
- $T_2 - T_3$ ✓
- $T_2 - T_4$ ✗ (not listed)
- $T_3 - T_4$ ✓

Thus $\{T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4\}$ is **not** a clique; the largest clique is $\{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$ (a triangle) and also $\{T_1, T_3, T_4\}$ (another triangle) and $\{T_3, T_4, T_6\}$.

Therefore $\omega(G) = 3$.

This gives us a lower bound $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G) = 3$.

### Step 3 — Apply Welsh-Powell Algorithm

**Sort by degree (descending):**
$$T_3 \,(4) > T_1 \,(3) = T_2 \,(3) = T_4 \,(3) = T_6 \,(3) > T_5 \,(2).$$

Break ties by vertex label.

**Colour 1:** Start with $T_3$.
- Assign Colour 1 to $T_3$.
- Scan list: $T_1$ (adj to $T_3$) ✗; $T_2$ (adj to $T_3$) ✗; $T_4$ (adj to $T_3$) ✗; $T_6$ (adj to $T_3$) ✗; $T_5$ (not adj to $T_3$) ✓ → assign Colour 1.

**Colour 2:** First uncoloured is $T_1$.
- Assign Colour 2 to $T_1$.
- Scan: $T_2$ (adj to $T_1$) ✗; $T_4$ (adj to $T_1$) ✗; $T_6$ (not adj to $T_1$) ✓ → assign Colour 2.

**Colour 3:** First uncoloured is $T_2$.
- Assign Colour 3 to $T_2$.
- Scan: $T_4$ (not adj to $T_2$) ✓ → assign Colour 3.
- $T_6$ (not adj to $T_2$, but check neighbours with already-coloured? $T_6$ adj to $T_4$? Yes, but $T_4$ is Colour 3 — same as $T_2$. $T_6$ is not adjacent to $T_2$, so Colour 3 is allowed.) → assign Colour 3.

**Resulting Assignment Table:**

| Transmitter | Frequency | Colour |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | $f_2$ | 2 |
| $T_2$ | $f_3$ | 3 |
| $T_3$ | $f_1$ | 1 |
| $T_4$ | $f_3$ | 3 |
| $T_5$ | $f_1$ | 1 |
| $T_6$ | $f_3$ | 3 |

### Step 4 — Verification

Check every edge for properness:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|T_1 - T_2| &= |2-3| = 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_1 - T_3| &= |2-1| = 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_1 - T_4| &= |2-3| = 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_2 - T_3| &= |3-1| = 2 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_2 - T_5| &= |3-1| = 2 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_3 - T_4| &= |1-3| = 2 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_3 - T_6| &= |1-3| = 2 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_4 - T_6| &= |3-3| = 0 \quad \text{✗ CONFLICT!}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Re-Examination Required.** The greedy assignment above gave a conflict on $(T_4, T_6)$. Both received Colour 3. We must back-track. The issue arose because we allowed $T_6$ to take Colour 3 even though $T_4$ (a Colour-3 neighbour) was uncoloured at that moment — but later $T_4$ got Colour 3 and now conflicts. The Welsh-Powell algorithm **as stated in some textbooks** avoids this by only colouring a vertex when ALL its neighbours that are already assigned a colour do not conflict; let us redo carefully.

### Step 3 (Corrected) — Strict Welsh-Powell

**Colour 1:** $T_3$ gets 1. Scan: $T_5$ is not adjacent to $T_3$, so $T_5$ gets 1. Others all adjacent to $T_3$.

**Colour 2:** First uncoloured is $T_1$ (degree 3). $T_1$ gets 2. Scan: $T_2$ (adj to $T_1$) skip; $T_4$ (adj to $T_1$) skip; $T_6$ (NOT adj to $T_1$) → $T_6$ gets 2.

**Colour 3:** First uncoloured is $T_2$. $T_2$ gets 3. Scan: $T_4$ — check neighbours of $T_4$ that are already coloured: $T_1$ is Colour 2 (≠3 ✓), $T_3$ is Colour 1 (≠3 ✓), $T_6$ is Colour 2 (≠3 ✓). So $T_4$ gets Colour 3.

All six transmitters now coloured. **Result:**

| Transmitter | Frequency |
|---|---|
| $T_1$ | $f_2$ |
| $T_2$ | $f_3$ |
| $T_3$ | $f_1$ |
| $T_4$ | $f_3$ |
| $T_5$ | $f_1$ |
| $T_6$ | $f_2$ |

**Verification (corrected):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
|T_1 - T_2| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \quad |T_1 - T_3| = 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_1 - T_4| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \quad |T_2 - T_3| = 2 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_2 - T_5| &= 2 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \quad |T_3 - T_4| = 2 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_3 - T_6| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \quad |T_4 - T_6| = 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

All nine edges verified. **Number of frequencies used = 3 = $\chi(G)$.** Since $\omega(G) = 3$ and we achieved 3 colours, $\chi(G) = \omega(G) = 3$ is optimal.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — T-Coloring with Adjacent-Channel Constraint

**Problem:** Same six transmitters, but now the constraint is that any interfering pair must differ by **at least 2** (i.e. $|f(u) - f(v)| \geq 2$). This models adjacent-channel interference.

### Formulation
$$T_{uv} = \{0, 1\} \quad \forall (u,v) \in E.$$

We must find a labelling with minimum span.

### Approach — Greedy with gap enforcement

Colour 1 group: $T_3 \to 1$, $T_5 \to 3$ (gap $\geq 2$ from $T_3$ ✓, and $T_5$ not adjacent to $T_3$ ✓).

Colour 2 group: $T_1 \to 2$, $T_6 \to 4$ (gap from $T_1$ ✓, not adjacent to $T_1$ ✓).

Colour 3 group: $T_2 \to 5$? Check gap with $T_1 = 2$: $|5-2| = 3 \geq 2$ ✓; with $T_3 = 1$: $|5-1| = 4 \geq 2$ ✓; with $T_5 = 3$: $|5-3| = 2 \geq 2$ ✓. So $T_2$ can use 5.

$T_4 \to 6$? Check with $T_1 = 2$: $|6-2| = 4 \geq 2$ ✓; with $T_3 = 1$: $|6-1| = 5 \geq 2$ ✓; with $T_6 = 4$: $|6-4| = 2 \geq 2$ ✓. So $T_4$ can use 6.

**Final T-Coloring Assignment:**

| Transmitter | Frequency | Span Check |
|---|---|---|
| $T_3$ | 1 | min |
| $T_1$ | 2 | gap to $T_3$ = 1 ✗ CONFLICT! |

The pair $(T_1, T_3)$ requires gap $\geq 2$ but $|2-1| = 1 < 2$. We must re-assign.

### Corrected T-Coloring

$T_3 \to 1$, $T_5 \to 3$ (gap 2 from $T_3$ ✓).
$T_1 \to 4$ (gap from $T_3 = 1$ is 3 ≥ 2 ✓).
$T_2 \to 6$ (gap from $T_1 = 4$ is 2 ✓; gap from $T_3 = 1$ is 5 ✓; gap from $T_5 = 3$ is 3 ✓).
$T_6 \to 6$? Check gap from $T_3 = 1$ is 5 ✓; but $T_6$ adjacent to $T_4$ and $T_3$, not to $T_2$. So $T_6 = 6$ is allowed only if $T_4 \neq 6$ or $|6 - f(T_4)| \geq 2$.
$T_4 \to 8$ (gap from $T_1 = 4$ is 4 ✓; from $T_3 = 1$ is 7 ✓; from $T_6 = 6$ is 2 ✓).

**Final table:**

| Transmitter | Frequency |
|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 4 |
| $T_2$ | 6 |
| $T_3$ | 1 |
| $T_4$ | 8 |
| $T_5$ | 3 |
| $T_6$ | 6 |

**Span = 8 − 1 = 7.** So with adjacent-channel gap $\geq 2$, we need 7 frequency units (vs. 3 in pure co-channel).

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Time Complexity | Solution Quality | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| First-Fit (Greedy) | $O(V + E)$ | May use up to $\Delta(G) + 1$ colours; often suboptimal | Quick estimate; small networks |
| Welsh-Powell | $O(V^2)$ | Good; often matches $\chi(G)$ on small dense graphs | KTU exam scenarios (manual, traceable) |
| DSATUR | $O(V^2)$ | Very good; near-optimal on most benchmark graphs | Production FAP solvers for medium networks |
| Backtracking + Pruning | Exponential | Optimal | Small critical networks (≤ 25 cells) |
| Tabu Search / SA | Configurable | Near-optimal, scales to thousands of cells | Real nationwide cellular planning |
| CP / ILP Solver | Solver-dependent | Optimal or proven lower bound | Operator planning tools |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 14-mark answers**, the examiner expects you to (a) state the algorithm, (b) apply it step-by-step showing the assignment table at each iteration, and (c) verify the final colouring. Skipping the verification step costs 2 marks on average.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The FAP-to-Coloring Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Real Network Scenario"] --> B["Identify Transmitters as Vertices"]
    B --> C["Measure or Compute Interference Pairs"]
    C --> D["Construct Interference Graph G(V,E)"]
    D --> E{"Constraint Type?"}
    E -->|"Co-channel only"| F["Apply Vertex Coloring: chi(G)"]
    E -->|"Adjacent-channel gap d"| G["Apply T-Coloring with T_e = {0,1,...,d-1}"]
    E -->|"Pre-allocated site lists"| H["Apply List Coloring"]
    F --> I["Run Welsh-Powell or DSATUR"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Output: Frequency Assignment Table"]
    J --> K{"All Edges Satisfied?"}
    K -->|"Yes"| L["FEASIBLE PLAN: Report span and chi"]
    K -->|"No"| M["Back-track / Re-assign"]
    M --> I
    L --> N["END: Deploy Plan to Operator Network"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Interference Graph of Worked Example 1

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1["T1 (degree 3)"] --- n2["T2 (degree 3)"]
    n1 --- n3["T3 (degree 4)"]
    n1 --- n4["T4 (degree 3)"]
    n2 --- n3
    n3 --- n4
    n3 --- n6["T6 (degree 3)"]
    n4 --- n6
    n2 --- n5["T5 (degree 2)"]
    n5 --- n6
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Welsh-Powell Execution Trace

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Step 1: Compute degrees of all vertices"]
    s2["Step 2: Sort vertices by degree descending"]
    s3["Step 3: Assign Colour 1 to first vertex"]
    s4["Step 4: Scan sorted list; assign Colour 1 to non-adjacent vertices"]
    s5["Step 5: Repeat with Colour 2, 3, ... until done"]
    s6["Step 6: Verify against all edges"]
    s7{"Any conflict?"}
    s8["Output final frequency assignment"]
    s9["Back-track: re-assign last colour group"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7
    s7 -->|"No"| s8
    s7 -->|"Yes"| s9 --> s3
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — FAP Solver Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        I1["Transmitter Coordinates DB"]
        I2["Propagation / Interference Matrix Engine"]
        I3["Regulatory Frequency Pool"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["Processing Layer"]
        P1["Graph Builder Module"]
        P2["Constraint Classifier"]
        P3["Heuristic Engine: Welsh-Powell / DSATUR"]
        P4["T-Coloring Module"]
        P5["Verifier & Span Calculator"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        O1["Frequency Assignment Table"]
        O2["Visual Spectrum Map"]
        O3["Interference Cost Report"]
        O4["Operator Action Queue"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    P5 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
    O1 --> O4
```

## 4.5 Schematic — Frequency Reuse Pattern (Cellular Inspiration)

```mermaid
graph TD
    cellA["Cell Cluster A: Freq f1"]
    cellB["Cell Cluster B: Freq f2"]
    cellC["Cell Cluster C: Freq f3"]
    cellD["Cell Cluster D: Freq f4"]
    note["Note: In a real cellular N=7 reuse pattern, the same f1 reappears only after 7 cell hops, controlled by the hexagonal tessellation graph which has chi = 7."]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real cellular systems** use a hexagonal cell tessellation as the canonical interference graph. For a cluster size of $N$ cells per frequency-reuse pattern, the chromatic number (and thus the number of distinct frequencies) equals $N$. Classical values are $N = 3, 4, 7, 9, 12$ corresponding to different cluster geometries.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the Frequency Assignment Problem (FAP). How is it reduced to a graph coloring problem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Frequency Assignment Problem (FAP) is the problem of assigning frequencies (or channels) to a set of transmitters such that electromagnetic interference between them is avoided, while the total spectrum used (the span) is minimised.

It is reduced to a graph coloring problem as follows:
1. Construct an **interference graph** $G = (V, E)$ where each vertex $v_i \in V$ represents a transmitter.
2. Place an edge $(v_i, v_j) \in E$ if and only if transmitters $v_i$ and $v_j$ interfere (cannot share / be near in frequency).
3. The **available frequencies** correspond to **colours**.
4. A **proper vertex coloring** of $G$ then yields a co-channel-interference-free frequency assignment.
5. The minimum number of frequencies required equals the **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$.

For adjacent-channel interference, the problem generalises to a **T-coloring** where the constraint becomes $|f(u) - f(v)| \notin T_{uv}$.

*(Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for graph construction, 1 for color mapping & chromatic number link.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**State Brooks' Theorem. How does it give an upper bound on the number of frequencies needed for an FAP?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Brooks' Theorem (1941):** For any connected undirected graph $G$ that is neither a complete graph $K_n$ nor an odd cycle, the chromatic number satisfies:

$$\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G),$$

where $\Delta(G)$ is the maximum vertex degree.

**Application to FAP:** The interference graph's maximum degree $\Delta(G)$ is determined by the transmitter with the most interfering neighbours. Brooks' theorem guarantees that the number of frequencies required is **at most** $\Delta(G)$ (or $\Delta(G) + 1$ in the trivial greedy case). In practical terms, no transmitter interferes with more than $\Delta(G)$ other transmitters, so $\Delta(G) + 1$ frequencies are always sufficient for a feasible assignment, and frequently fewer suffice.

*(Marks: 1 for statement, 1 for condition, 1 for FAP interpretation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Module-3 Internal Choice Pattern:** Two completely independent choices (Q-A or Q-B). Each carries sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model, Adapted]**

A wireless ISP has seven base stations $B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_7$. The interference pairs (where two stations cannot share a frequency) are:
$$\{B_1, B_2\},\ \{B_1, B_3\},\ \{B_1, B_4\},\ \{B_2, B_5\},\ \{B_2, B_6\},\ \{B_3, B_5\},\ \{B_3, B_7\},\ \{B_4, B_6\},\ \{B_5, B_7\},\ \{B_6, B_7\}.$$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Construct the interference graph. Identify the maximum clique $\omega(G)$ and the maximum degree $\Delta(G)$. Hence state a lower bound and an upper bound on $\chi(G)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Build adjacency list:**

| Vertex | Neighbours | Degree |
|---|---|---|
| $B_1$ | $B_2, B_3, B_4$ | 3 |
| $B_2$ | $B_1, B_5, B_6$ | 3 |
| $B_3$ | $B_1, B_5, B_7$ | 3 |
| $B_4$ | $B_1, B_6$ | 2 |
| $B_5$ | $B_2, B_3, B_7$ | 3 |
| $B_6$ | $B_2, B_4, B_7$ | 3 |
| $B_7$ | $B_3, B_5, B_6$ | 3 |

So $\Delta(G) = 3$.

**Step 2 — Detect maximum clique:**

Try $\{B_2, B_5, B_7\}$: edges $(B_2,B_5)$ ✓, $(B_2,B_7)$? Not in list ✗ — not a triangle.

Try $\{B_3, B_5, B_7\}$: edges $(B_3,B_5)$ ✓, $(B_3,B_7)$ ✓, $(B_5,B_7)$ ✓ → **Triangle!** So $\omega(G) \geq 3$.

Try extending to a 4-clique: add $B_6$? $(B_6, B_3)$? Not in list ✗. Add $B_2$? $(B_2, B_7)$? Not in list ✗. No 4-clique.

Hence $\omega(G) = 3$.

**Step 3 — State bounds:**

- **Lower bound:** $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G) = 3$.
- **Upper bound:** $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1 = 4$.

*(Marks: 2 for graph/list, 2 for clique detection, 2 for bounds, 1 for clarity.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Apply the Welsh-Powell algorithm to obtain a valid frequency assignment. Verify that all interference constraints are satisfied and report the minimum span.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sort by degree descending (ties broken by label):**
$$B_1, B_2, B_3, B_5, B_6, B_7, B_4.$$

**Step 2 — Colour 1:** Assign Colour 1 to $B_1$. Scan: $B_2$ (adj to $B_1$) ✗; $B_3$ (adj) ✗; $B_5$ (not adj to $B_1$) ✓; $B_6$ (not adj) ✓; $B_7$ (not adj) ✓; $B_4$ (adj to $B_1$) ✗.

Colour 1 set: $\{B_1, B_5, B_6, B_7\}$.

**Step 3 — Colour 2:** First uncoloured is $B_2$. Assign Colour 2. Scan: $B_3$ (not adj to $B_2$) ✓ → Colour 2; $B_4$ (not adj to $B_2$) ✓ → Colour 2.

Colour 2 set: $\{B_2, B_3, B_4\}$.

**All vertices coloured.**

**Assignment Table:**

| Station | Frequency |
|---|---|
| $B_1$ | $f_1$ |
| $B_2$ | $f_2$ |
| $B_3$ | $f_2$ |
| $B_4$ | $f_2$ |
| $B_5$ | $f_1$ |
| $B_6$ | $f_1$ |
| $B_7$ | $f_1$ |

**Step 4 — Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
|B_1 - B_2| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|B_1 - B_3| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|B_1 - B_4| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|B_2 - B_5| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|B_2 - B_6| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|B_3 - B_5| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|B_3 - B_7| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|B_4 - B_6| &= 1 \neq 0 \quad \checkmark \\
|B_5 - B_7| &= 0 \quad \text{✗ CONFLICT! Both } f_1.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conflict detected** on $(B_5, B_7)$. Re-run with strict rule: when scanning for the same colour, ensure none of the vertex's *already-coloured* neighbours (with the SAME proposed colour) conflicts.

**Re-coloured carefully:**

Colour 1: $B_1, B_5, B_6, B_7$ — Wait, $B_5 - B_7$ is an edge. So they cannot share. Choose only one of $\{B_5, B_7\}$ for Colour 1.

Re-run: Colour 1 → $B_1, B_6$ (since $B_6$ is not adjacent to $B_1$). Skip $B_5$ (no Colour 1 conflict with $B_1$, but $B_5$ is uncoloured; we only colour non-adjacent to all currently-Colour-1 vertices; $B_5$ not adj to $B_1$, not adj to $B_6$, so $B_5$ gets Colour 1; but $B_7$ is adj to $B_5$, so $B_7$ skip Colour 1).

Refined Colour 1: $\{B_1, B_6, B_5\}$.

Colour 2: First uncoloured = $B_2$. $B_2$ gets 2. Scan: $B_3$ (not adj $B_2$) ✓ → 2; $B_7$ (not adj $B_2$) ✓ → 2; $B_4$ (not adj $B_2$) ✓ → 2.

Final Assignment:

| Station | Frequency |
|---|---|
| $B_1$ | $f_1$ |
| $B_2$ | $f_2$ |
| $B_3$ | $f_2$ |
| $B_4$ | $f_2$ |
| $B_5$ | $f_1$ |
| $B_6$ | $f_1$ |
| $B_7$ | $f_2$ |

Re-verify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|B_1-B_2|=1,\; |B_1-B_3|=1,\; |B_1-B_4|=1,\\
|B_2-B_5|=1,\; |B_2-B_6|=1,\; |B_3-B_5|=1,\\
|B_3-B_7|=0 \quad \text{✗ CONFLICT! } B_3 \text{ and } B_7 \text{ both } f_2.
\end{aligned}
$$

Hmm, $B_3 - B_7$ is an edge. Re-run yet more carefully:

Colour 1: $B_1$ gets 1. Non-adj to $B_1$: $B_5, B_6, B_7$ (also $B_3$? No, $B_3$ is adj to $B_1$). Pick $B_5, B_6, B_7$ — but $B_5-B_7$ is an edge! So we can only include non-mutually-adjacent vertices. Pick $B_6$ (not adj $B_1$, will check later), skip $B_5$ and $B_7$ to avoid their internal edge. Actually the Welsh-Powell rule is: scan ordered list, assign current colour to any vertex that is not adjacent to ANY vertex already coloured with this colour.

- $B_1 = $ Colour 1.
- $B_2$: adj to $B_1$ ✗.
- $B_3$: adj to $B_1$ ✗.
- $B_5$: NOT adj to $B_1$ ✓ → Colour 1.
- $B_6$: NOT adj to $B_1$ ✓ → BUT $B_6$ adj to $B_5$? Not in edge list — no. So $B_6$ → Colour 1.
- $B_7$: NOT adj to $B_1$ ✓ → $B_7$ adj to $B_5$ ✓ edge exists → SKIP.
- $B_4$: adj to $B_1$ ✗.

Colour 1 set: $\{B_1, B_5, B_6\}$.

Colour 2: First uncoloured = $B_2$. → Colour 2.
- $B_3$: NOT adj $B_2$ ✓ → Colour 2.
- $B_7$: NOT adj $B_2$ ✓ → Colour 2. ($B_7$ adj to $B_3$? Yes, edge $(B_3, B_7)$ exists! → SKIP $B_7$.)
- $B_4$: NOT adj $B_2$ ✓ → Colour 2. ($B_4$ adj to $B_3$? No. $B_4$ adj to $B_7$? No. So $B_4$ → Colour 2.)

Colour 2 set: $\{B_2, B_3, B_4\}$.

Colour 3: First uncoloured = $B_7$. → Colour 3.

**Final Assignment:**

| Station | Frequency |
|---|---|
| $B_1$ | $f_1$ |
| $B_2$ | $f_2$ |
| $B_3$ | $f_2$ |
| $B_4$ | $f_2$ |
| $B_5$ | $f_1$ |
| $B_6$ | $f_1$ |
| $B_7$ | $f_3$ |

Verify all 10 edges:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|B_1-B_2|=1,\; |B_1-B_3|=1,\; |B_1-B_4|=1,\\
|B_2-B_5|=1,\; |B_2-B_6|=1,\; |B_3-B_5|=1,\; |B_3-B_7|=1,\\
|B_4-B_6|=1,\; |B_5-B_7|=2,\; |B_6-B_7|=2.
\end{aligned}
$$

All non-zero. ✓ **Span = $f_3 - f_1$ = 2 frequency units. Used $\chi(G) = 3$ frequencies.** Since $\omega(G) = 3$, this is **optimal**.

*(Marks: 2 algorithm statement, 3 trace iterations, 2 verification table — total 7.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model, Adapted]**

A telecom operator plans five transmitters $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4, T_5$ with the following *adjacent-channel* constraint: any two interfering transmitters must differ by **at least 2** in frequency. The interference pairs are $\{T_1, T_2\}, \{T_1, T_3\}, \{T_2, T_4\}, \{T_3, T_5\}, \{T_4, T_5\}, \{T_2, T_5\}$.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Formulate this as a T-coloring problem. Construct the graph, identify the maximum clique and the lower bound on the span.**

**Model Solution:**

**Formulation:**
$$T_{uv} = \{0, 1\} \quad \forall (u,v) \in E.$$

We seek a labelling $f : V \to \mathbb{Z}^+$ minimising $\text{span}(f) = \max f - \min f$ subject to $|f(u) - f(v)| \notin \{0, 1\}$, i.e. $|f(u) - f(v)| \geq 2$.

**Adjacency list:**

| Vertex | Neighbours | Degree |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | $T_2, T_3$ | 2 |
| $T_2$ | $T_1, T_4, T_5$ | 3 |
| $T_3$ | $T_1, T_5$ | 2 |
| $T_4$ | $T_2, T_5$ | 2 |
| $T_5$ | $T_2, T_3, T_4$ | 3 |

$\Delta(G) = 3$.

**Maximum clique:** Look at $\{T_2, T_4, T_5\}$: $(T_2, T_4)$ ✓, $(T_2, T_5)$ ✓, $(T_4, T_5)$ ✓ → Triangle! Extend: $\{T_1, T_2, T_3\}$: $(T_1, T_2)$ ✓, $(T_1, T_3)$ ✓, $(T_2, T_3)$? Not in list ✗. So $\omega(G) = 3$.

**Lower bound on span:**
- Minimum span $\geq 2(\omega - 1) = 2(3-1) = 4$ (a clique of size $k$ under gap-$\geq 2$ requires labels spanning at least $2(k-1)$).
- Plus 1 for indexing offset: span $\geq 4$.

*(Marks: 2 formulation, 2 graph/clique, 2 lower bound derivation, 1 setup — total 7.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Apply a greedy T-coloring algorithm to find a feasible assignment with minimum span. Show your work step-by-step.**

**Model Solution:**

We assign frequencies sequentially, enforcing $|f(u) - f(v)| \geq 2$ for every edge.

**Order by degree:** $T_2, T_5$ (degree 3), then $T_1, T_3, T_4$ (degree 2).

**Step 1:** Assign $T_2 = 1$.
**Step 2:** Assign $T_5$. Must satisfy $|f(T_5) - 1| \geq 2$ → $f(T_5) \geq 3$. Pick smallest = 3.
**Step 3:** Assign $T_1$. Adj to $T_2$ only. Need $|f(T_1) - 1| \geq 2$ → $f(T_1) \geq 3$. Pick $f(T_1) = 3$.
- Check: $T_1$ also adj to $T_3$ (not coloured yet) — fine.
**Step 4:** Assign $T_3$. Adj to $T_1$ and $T_5$. Need $|f(T_3) - 3| \geq 2$ (from $T_1$ AND from $T_5$). So $f(T_3) \leq 1$ or $f(T_3) \geq 5$. Cannot be 1 (would conflict with $T_2$? $T_3$ not adj to $T_2$). Actually check: $T_3$ not adj $T_2$, so $f(T_3) = 1$ is OK with $T_2$. But with $T_1$ (adj): $|1-3| = 2 \geq 2$ ✓. With $T_5$ (adj): $|1-3| = 2 \geq 2$ ✓. So $f(T_3) = 1$.
**Step 5:** Assign $T_4$. Adj to $T_2$ and $T_5$. Need $|f(T_4) - 1| \geq 2$ → $f(T_4) \geq 3$. But $T_5 = 3$, and $T_4$ adj $T_5$: $|f(T_4) - 3| \geq 2$ → $f(T_4) \leq 1$ or $f(T_4) \geq 5$. Combined: $f(T_4) \geq 5$.

**Final T-Coloring:**

| Transmitter | Frequency |
|---|---|
| $T_1$ | 3 |
| $T_2$ | 1 |
| $T_3$ | 1 |
| $T_4$ | 5 |
| $T_5$ | 3 |

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
|T_1-T_2| &= |3-1| = 2 \geq 2 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_1-T_3| &= |3-1| = 2 \geq 2 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_2-T_4| &= |1-5| = 4 \geq 2 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_3-T_5| &= |1-3| = 2 \geq 2 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_4-T_5| &= |5-3| = 2 \geq 2 \quad \checkmark \\
|T_2-T_5| &= |1-3| = 2 \geq 2 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**All six constraints satisfied.** Span = $5 - 1 = 4$ — meets the lower bound of 4. **Optimal.**

*(Marks: 2 for algorithm, 3 for step-by-step assignment, 2 for verification — total 7.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU FAP Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the edge verification step** — Examiners give 2 marks explicitly for checking every edge after colouring. A colouring without verification is considered "unfinished work."
>
> 2. **Confusing co-channel with adjacent-channel constraint** — Many students write "$f(u) \neq f(v)$" even when the problem states a *gap* constraint like $|f(u) - f(v)| \geq 3$. Read the question carefully.
>
> 3. **Not stating $\omega(G)$ and $\Delta(G)$ before applying the algorithm** — KTU expects you to report a lower bound ($\chi \geq \omega$) and an upper bound ($\chi \leq \Delta + 1$) before launching into the colouring. Skipping this costs 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Misnaming the algorithm** — "First-Fit Greedy" and "Welsh-Powell" are **different** algorithms. Welsh-Powell specifically sorts by degree *before* colouring. If you sort but don't state it, you may be marked down.
>
> 5. **Forgetting that $\chi(G)$ may exceed $\omega(G)$** — E.g. the Mycielski graph has $\omega = 2$ but arbitrarily large $\chi$. Always confirm with actual colouring; the lower bound alone is not the answer.
>
> 6. **Using $|\ldots|$ inside markdown tables** — Always escape vertical bars in answer sheets where the platform parses tables. Use `\vert` or write "absolute value" in words.
>
> 7. **Skipping the explanation of WHY a vertex gets a particular colour** — In 14-mark answers, the trace must explain each step. "Assign colour 2 to $T_4$" alone is insufficient; add "because $T_4$ is not adjacent to any vertex already coloured 2."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **FAP Definition:** Assign frequencies to transmitters minimising interference and spectrum span.
- ✅ **Graph Reduction:** Transmitters → Vertices; Interference pairs → Edges; Frequencies → Colours.
- ✅ **Chromatic Number $\chi(G)$** = minimum number of frequencies needed for co-channel-free plan.
- ✅ **Maximum Clique $\omega(G)$** gives lower bound: $\chi(G) \geq \omega(G)$.
- ✅ **Maximum Degree $\Delta(G)$** gives upper bound: $\chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1$ (greedy) or $\leq \Delta(G)$ (Brooks, if $G \ne K_n$ or odd cycle).
- ✅ **Four Color Theorem:** $\chi(G) \leq 4$ for planar $G$.
- ✅ **Three Constraint Types:**
  - Co-channel (gap ≥ 1) → Vertex coloring.
  - Adjacent-channel (gap ≥ $d$) → T-coloring with $T_e = \{0, 1, \ldots, d-1\}$.
  - Co-site → Large-gap constraint at a single tower.
- ✅ **Welsh-Powell Algorithm:**
  - Sort by degree descending.
  - Assign Colour 1 to first vertex, then to all non-adjacent-to-currently-coloured vertices.
  - Repeat for Colour 2, 3, … until done.
- ✅ **DSATUR:** Dynamic saturation — pick vertex with most distinct colours in neighbourhood.
- ✅ **Complexity:** Vertex coloring is NP-hard in general; heuristics are used in practice.
- ✅ **Industrial Tools:** Tabu search, simulated annealing, CP/ILP solvers, meta-heuristics for large-scale cellular networks (GSM/LTE/5G).
- ✅ **Verification Step:** Always check every edge after producing a coloring — examiners require it.
- ✅ **Optimality Confirmation:** If $\chi(G)$ achieved = $\omega(G)$, the plan is provably optimal.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Viva Tip:** If asked "Why is FAP NP-hard?", answer: *"Because vertex coloring on general graphs is NP-hard (Karp, 1972). Since the FAP with co-channel constraints is a direct reduction of vertex coloring, FAP inherits NP-hardness. With additional adjacent-channel constraints, the problem becomes at least as hard as T-coloring, which is also NP-complete."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graph Partitioning and Community Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Graph Partitioning and Community Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Graph Partitioning

**Graph Partitioning** is the problem of dividing the vertex set $V$ of a graph $G = (V, E)$ into $k$ disjoint subsets (partitions) $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_k$ such that $V_1 \cup V_2 \cup \ldots \cup V_k = V$, subject to optimization constraints. The objective is typically to **minimize the number of edges crossing between partitions** (the *cut size*) while maintaining **balanced partition sizes**.

Formally, for a $k$-way partition with $n = \vert V \vert$ vertices:

$$\text{Balance Constraint: } \vert V_i \vert \approx \frac{n}{k} \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$$

$$\text{Objective: } \min \sum_{i<j} \mid E(V_i, V_j) \mid$$

where $E(V_i, V_j)$ denotes the set of edges with one endpoint in $V_i$ and another in $V_j$.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Community Detection

**Community Detection** is the identification of densely connected subgroups (communities) within a network, where vertices within the same community exhibit **higher internal connectivity** than expected by chance. Unlike classical partitioning, community detection does *not* typically require pre-specifying the number of communities or enforcing strict balance constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (High-Yield KTU Point):**
> - **Graph Partitioning**: Number of parts $k$ is *fixed a priori*; balance is enforced; objective is to minimize inter-part edges.
> - **Community Detection**: Number of communities is *discovered automatically*; balance is not mandatory; objective maximizes intra-community density (e.g., via **modularity**).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — "Friends in a College Campus":**

Imagine a college campus where students are connected by friendships. If you want to **partition** students into exactly 4 hostels (fixed $k = 4$, balanced sizes), you must cut some friendships, and you want to cut as few as possible — that is **graph partitioning**.

On the other hand, if you simply *observe* the natural cliques — say, the cricket team hangs out together, the music club sticks together, the coding club forms its own group — and you want the algorithm to *find* these groups automatically, that is **community detection**. The number of cliques is not pre-decided.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modularity ($Q$)** is the standard quality metric for community detection, ranging typically from $-0.5$ to $1$. Higher modularity implies stronger community structure. Values above **0.3** are usually considered significant in real-world networks.

## 1.4 Why These Problems Matter (Engineering Relevance)

- **VLSI Circuit Design**: Partitioning gates across chips to minimize inter-chip wiring.
- **Parallel Computing**: Load balancing across processors.
- **Social Network Analysis**: Identifying influencer clusters, recommendation systems.
- **Bioinformatics**: Protein-protein interaction modules, gene regulatory networks.
- **Image Segmentation**: Computer vision applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cut-Based Quality Metrics

### 2.1.1 Simple Cut (Edge Cut)

For a 2-way partition $(S, V \setminus S)$:

$$\text{Cut}(S) = \sum_{i \in S, j \in V \setminus S} A_{ij}$$

where $A$ is the adjacency matrix. This metric **favors degenerate solutions** — it is minimized by isolating a single vertex, so it is rarely used alone.

### 2.1.2 Ratio Cut (Hagen & Kahng, 1992)

$$\text{RatioCut}(S) = \frac{\text{Cut}(S)}{\vert S \vert} + \frac{\text{Cut}(S)}{\vert V \setminus S \vert}$$

This penalizes unbalanced partitions by normalizing each side by its size.

### 2.1.3 Normalized Cut (Shi & Malik, 2000)

$$\text{NCut}(S) = \frac{\text{Cut}(S)}{\text{Vol}(S)} + \frac{\text{Cut}(S)}{\text{Vol}(V \setminus S)}$$

where $\text{Vol}(S) = \sum_{i \in S} d_i$ is the sum of degrees inside $S$. This normalizes by **edge volume (degree sum)**, not vertex count — crucial for graphs with skewed degree distributions.

## 2.2 The Graph Laplacian

The unnormalized Laplacian matrix:

$$L = D - A$$

where $D$ is the degree matrix (diagonal with $D_{ii} = d_i$). The Laplacian is **symmetric positive semi-definite** with the following key properties:

- $L \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{0}$ (zero eigenvalue with eigenvector $\mathbf{1}$)
- All eigenvalues $\lambda_0 \leq \lambda_1 \leq \ldots \leq \lambda_{n-1}$ are real and non-negative
- The number of connected components equals the **multiplicity of eigenvalue 0**

The normalized Laplacian variants are:

$$L_{\text{sym}} = D^{-1/2} L D^{-1/2} = I - D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}$$

$$L_{\text{rw}} = D^{-1} L = I - D^{-1} A$$

## 2.3 Spectral Graph Partitioning

The **Fiedler vector** is the eigenvector corresponding to the second-smallest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$ of $L$, also called the *algebraic connectivity*. Partitioning via the Fiedler vector:

1. Compute $L = D - A$.
2. Compute the eigenvector $v_1$ for $\lambda_1 > 0$.
3. Sort vertices by $v_1(i)$ and split at the median (or threshold at zero).

> [!NOTE]
> The algebraic connectivity $\lambda_1$ measures how well-connected the graph is. Higher $\lambda_1$ ⇒ harder to disconnect.

## 2.4 Modularity — The Community Detection Gold Standard

**Modularity $Q$** compares the actual edge density within communities to the expected density in a *null model* (random graph preserving degree sequence):

$$Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i,j} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{d_i d_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)$$

where:
- $m = \vert E \vert$ is the total number of edges
- $d_i$ is the degree of vertex $i$
- $c_i$ is the community assignment of vertex $i$
- $\delta(c_i, c_j) = 1$ if $i$ and $j$ are in the same community, else $0$
- The term $\frac{d_i d_j}{2m}$ is the expected number of edges between $i$ and $j$ in the configuration model

Rewriting in matrix form using the modularity matrix $B$:

$$B_{ij} = A_{ij} - \frac{d_i d_j}{2m}$$

$$Q = \frac{1}{2m} \text{Tr}(S^T B S)$$

where $S$ is the community assignment matrix (column per community, indicator rows).

## 2.5 The Girvan–Newman Algorithm (Edge Betweenness)

**Edge Betweenness** of an edge $e$ = number of shortest paths between all pairs of vertices that pass through $e$.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Compute edge betweenness for all edges.
2. Remove the edge(s) with the highest betweenness.
3. Recompute betweenness on the modified graph.
4. Repeat until the graph is disconnected.
5. The dendrogram records the order of edge removals; the partition with maximum modularity is selected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complexity Warning:** Computing betweenness naively is $O(nm)$ per iteration, and there are $O(m)$ iterations, giving $O(nm^2)$ total — impractical for large networks. Brandes' algorithm (2001) optimizes this to $O(nm)$ for *each* betweenness computation, yielding total $O(nm^2)$ or $O(m^2 n)$ for dense graphs.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula** | **Boundary / Range** | **Use Case** |
|-------------|-------------|----------------------|--------------|
| Simple Cut | $\text{Cut}(S) = \sum_{i \in S, j \notin S} A_{ij}$ | $\geq 0$ | Trivial measure, avoid alone |
| Ratio Cut | $\text{RCut} = \frac{\text{Cut}(S)}{\vert S \vert} + \frac{\text{Cut}(S)}{\vert V \setminus S \vert}$ | $[0, n)$ | Balanced 2-way partition |
| Normalized Cut | $\text{NCut} = \frac{\text{Cut}(S)}{\text{Vol}(S)} + \frac{\text{Cut}(S)}{\text{Vol}(\bar S)}$ | $[0, 2)$ | Image segmentation, degree-skewed graphs |
| Laplacian | $L = D - A$ | $\lambda_0 = 0$, $\lambda_i \geq 0$ | Spectral methods |
| Algebraic Connectivity | $\lambda_1(L)$ | $\lambda_1 > 0$ for connected $G$ | Robustness metric |
| Modularity | $Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{ij} \left[ A_{ij} - \frac{d_i d_j}{2m} \right] \delta(c_i, c_j)$ | $[-0.5, 1]$ | Community quality |
| Edge Betweenness | $C_B(e) = \sum_{s \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(e)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | $\geq 0$ | Hierarchical communities |
| Conductance | $\phi(S) = \frac{\text{Cut}(S)}{\min(\text{Vol}(S), \text{Vol}(\bar S))}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Local cluster quality |
| Modularity Matrix | $B_{ij} = A_{ij} - \frac{d_i d_j}{2m}$ | $\sum_{ij} B_{ij} = 0$ | Spectral modularity optimization |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spectral partitioning** underlies Google's **PageRank-era** graph algorithms and is foundational in **mesh partitioning for FEM simulations**.
- **Modularity optimization** powers the **Louvain method** (Blondel et al., 2008), used by Twitter, LinkedIn, and Facebook for community analysis at billion-node scale.
- **Louvain's** greedy modularity maximization runs in $O(n \log n)$ amortized, making it the de-facto industry standard.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Modularity from Configuration Model

We derive modularity as a *Pearson-style chi-square deviation* of observed edges from the configuration-model expectation.

**Step 1 — Expected edges in null model.**

In the configuration model, each edge stub (half-edge) is randomly paired. The probability that a randomly chosen stub from vertex $i$ connects to one from vertex $j$ is:

$$P(i \leftrightarrow j) = \frac{d_i}{2m} \cdot \frac{d_j}{2m - 1} \approx \frac{d_i d_j}{(2m)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Expected number of edges $i \to j$.**

Since there are $2m$ total stubs, the expected number of edges between $i$ and $j$ is:

$$\mathbb{E}[A_{ij}] = 2m \cdot \frac{d_i d_j}{(2m)^2} = \frac{d_i d_j}{2m}$$

(The factor 2m is the number of possible edge slots; the case $i = j$ is a self-loop that we approximate as two stubs meeting.)

**Step 3 — Defining modularity as a sum of deviations.**

We sum the deviation between observed $A_{ij}$ and expected $\frac{d_i d_j}{2m}$ over all pairs in the same community:

$$Q = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i,j} \left( A_{ij} - \frac{d_i d_j}{2m} \right) \delta(c_i, c_j)$$

This represents the **fraction of edges within communities** minus the **expected fraction under the null model**.

**Step 4 — Range verification.**

- **Upper bound**: $Q \leq 1$ when all edges are within communities and the expected value is zero.
- **Lower bound**: $Q \geq -\frac{1}{2}$ when edges concentrate between communities against null expectation.
- Random graphs give $Q \approx 0$.

**Step 5 — Working example.**

Consider a graph with 4 nodes in 2 communities: $C_1 = \{1, 2\}$ and $C_2 = \{3, 4\}$, with edges $(1,2), (3,4), (1,3)$, i.e., 2 internal edges and 1 cut edge. $m = 3$, degrees $d = (1, 1, 2, 2)$.

Internal edges: $(1,2), (3,4)$. Internal edge count = 2.

$$Q = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 3}\left[ 2 \cdot 2 - \sum_{(i,j) \in C_1 \cup C_2} \frac{d_i d_j}{2m}\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{6}\left[ 4 - \left(\frac{1 \cdot 1}{6} + \frac{1 \cdot 1}{6} + \frac{2 \cdot 2}{6} + \frac{2 \cdot 2}{6}\right) \right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{6}\left[4 - \left(\frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{4}{6} + \frac{4}{6}\right)\right] = \frac{1}{6}\left[4 - \frac{10}{6}\right] = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{14}{6} = \frac{14}{36} \approx 0.389$$

Modularity is positive, confirming community structure.

## 3.2 Derivation: Spectral Bisection via Rayleigh Quotient

We want to minimize $\text{RCut}(S) = \frac{x^T L x}{x^T x}$ subject to $x_i \in \{+1, -1\}$ indicating partition membership.

**Step 1 — Encode the partition.**

Let $x \in \{-1, +1\}^n$ where $x_i = +1$ if $i \in S$ else $x_i = -1$. Then $\mathbf{1}^T x = \vert S \vert - \vert \bar S \vert$.

**Step 2 — Express the cut in terms of $x$.**

$$\text{Cut}(S) = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{i,j} A_{ij}(x_i - x_j)^2 = \frac{1}{4} x^T L x$$

(This works because $(x_i - x_j)^2 = 4$ when an edge crosses and $0$ when it doesn't.)

**Step 3 — Reformulate the Ratio Cut.**

The continuous relaxation drops the $\pm 1$ constraint and minimizes the Rayleigh quotient:

$$\min_{x \perp \mathbf{1}} \frac{x^T L x}{x^T x}$$

By the Rayleigh–Ritz theorem, this is minimized by the eigenvector of $L$ with the **smallest positive eigenvalue**, which is the Fiedler vector $v_1$.

**Step 4 — Discretization.**

We recover a discrete partition by thresholding $v_1$ at its median (or zero). This is an *approximation*, but works well in practice.

**Worked example — Path graph $P_4$:**

Vertices: $1 - 2 - 3 - 4$. Adjacency: $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$

Degree matrix: $D = \text{diag}(1, 2, 2, 1)$.

$$L = D - A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 & 0 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_0 = 0$, $\lambda_1 = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586$, $\lambda_2 = 2$, $\lambda_3 = 2 + \sqrt{2}$.

Fiedler vector (for $\lambda_1$): proportional to $(-1, 0, 0, 1)$ — sign-based thresholding yields $S = \{1, 4\}$, $\bar S = \{2, 3\}$. Cut = 2 (edges 1-2 and 3-4 cross? No: cuts are (1,2) and (3,4)... wait, both are internal to {1,4}? Re-verify: edge 1-2 has 1 in S, 2 not in S → cut. Similarly 3-4. Total cut = 2. Fiedler threshold on $(-1,0,0,1)$: negative → S = {1, 4}, positive → S = {2, 3}. Cut is 1 (edge 2-3). Hmm — we get a better cut!

The point: Fiedler gives a meaningful partition with cut = 1 for $P_4$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Method** | **Objective** | **Time Complexity** | **Output Type** | **Limitations** |
|------------|---------------|----------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| Spectral Bisection | $\min \frac{x^T L x}{x^T x}$ | $O(n^3)$ for dense, $O(d n)$ sparse | 2-partition (extendable) | Limited to $k = 2$ naively |
| Kernighan–Lin | $\min$ swap-based cut | $O(n^2 \log n)$ per pass | Refinement (needs initial) | Local optima, needs starting state |
| Girvan–Newman | $\max Q$ via edge removal | $O(n m^2)$ | Hierarchical dendrogram | Slow, $O(m^2 n)$ worst case |
| Louvain | $\max \Delta Q$ greedy | $O(n \log n)$ | Multi-level flat + dendrogram | Resolution limit (small communities) |
| Label Propagation | Consensus by voting | $O(n + m)$ per iteration | Flat partition | Stochastic, non-deterministic |
| Spectral Modularity | $\max \text{Tr}(S^T B S)$ | $O(n^3)$ | Flat partition | Continuous relaxation, NP-hard discrete |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Graph Partitioning Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Graph G] --> B[Compute Laplacian L = D - A]
    B --> C[Eigendecomposition of L]
    C --> D[Extract Fiedler Vector v1]
    D --> E[Threshold or Median Split]
    E --> F[Partition S and V minus S]
    F --> G[Compute Cut Size]
    G --> H{Optimal?}
    H -- No --> I[Adjust Threshold / Multi-level Recursion]
    I --> D
    H -- Yes --> J[Output Final Partition]
```

## 4.2 Community Detection: Modularity Maximization (Louvain)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Graph G with m edges] --> B[Initialize each vertex as own community]
    B --> C[For each vertex i]
    C --> D[Compute delta Q for moving i to each neighbor's community]
    D --> E{Max delta Q greater than 0?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Move i to best community]
    F --> C
    E -- No --> G[Keep i in current community]
    G --> C
    C --> H{All vertices visited and stable?}
    H -- No --> C
    H -- Yes --> I[Build new graph: communities become super-nodes]
    I --> J{Modularity converged?}
    J -- No --> C
    J -- Yes --> K[Output Community Assignment]
```

## 4.3 Girvan–Newman Edge Removal Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Graph] --> B[Compute Edge Betweenness]
    B --> C[Identify Edge with Max Betweenness]
    C --> D[Remove Edge from Graph]
    D --> E{New Components Formed?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Record Community Split]
    E -- No --> B
    F --> B
    B --> G{Edges Exhausted?}
    G -- No --> B
    G -- Yes --> H[Compute Modularity for Each Level]
    H --> I[Select Partition with Max Q]
```

## 4.4 Cut-Metric Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Graph G = V, E] --> B{Choose Cut Metric}
    B -- Simple --> C[Cut S = sum of crossing edges]
    B -- Ratio --> D[Cut S / Size of S]
    B -- Normalized --> E[Cut S / Vol S]
    B -- Conductance --> F[Cut S / min Vol S, Vol V minus S]
    C --> G[Apply Spectral or KL Heuristic]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Output Partitioned Graph]
```

## 4.5 Resolution Limit Phenomenon

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network with Dense Cliques] --> B[Apply Louvain with default Resolution = 1]
    B --> C[Small Dense Communities Merged]
    C --> D[Modularity High but Structure Lost]
    D --> E[Solution: Tune Resolution Parameter gamma]
    E --> F[gamma larger yields smaller communities]
    E --> G[gamma smaller yields larger communities]
```
