- **NMI** is the standard for evaluating clustering against ground-truth labels.
- **The Louvain $\Delta Q$ formula** has two bracket terms — subtracting the *current state* from the *candidate state*; never invert.
- **For KTU exams**, always state the time complexity, the data structure (e.g., adjacency list vs matrix), and the range of the metric used.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameterized Algorithms for Graph Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Parameterized Algorithms for Graph Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Parameterized Algorithms

A **Parameterized Algorithm** is an algorithm designed to solve a computationally hard problem whose running time is expressed as a function of both the input size $n$ and an additional integer **parameter** $k$ (which quantifies some structural property of the input). Formally, a parameterized problem is a language $L \subseteq \Sigma^* \times \mathbb{N}$, where the second component is the parameter.

A problem is said to be **Fixed-Parameter Tractable (FPT)** with respect to parameter $k$ if it can be solved in time:

$$f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$$

where $f$ is an arbitrary (usually exponential) computable function depending **only on $k$**, and $n^{O(1)}$ is a polynomial in the input size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim expected):** A parameterized problem $(I, k)$ is FPT if there exists a deterministic algorithm that decides whether $(I, k) \in L$ in time $f(k) \cdot |I|^{O(1)}$. The class of such problems is denoted **FPT**.

## 1.2 The Hardness Counterpart: W-Hierarchy

When a parameterized problem is **not** believed to be FPT, it is classified into the **W-Hierarchy**:

- $\text{W[1]} \subseteq \text{W[2]} \subseteq \cdots \subseteq \text{XP}$
- A problem is in **XP** if solvable in $n^{g(k)}$ time (sub-exponential in $n$ but possibly huge in $k$).
- **Independent Set** and **Clique** parameterized by solution size $k$ are classic $\text{W[1]}$-complete problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Pairing:** Independent Set / Clique are the canonical W[1]-complete problems, just as SAT is the canonical NP-complete problem.

## 1.3 Why Parameterize? — Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are a detective searching for a thief hiding in a city of **10 million** buildings (input size $n = 10^7$). An **unparameterized** brute-force search would take years. However, suppose the detective is given a strong lead:

> *"The thief has only **3** accomplices"* (parameter $k = 3$).

With this extra hint, the search space collapses. The detective only needs to investigate combinations of size 4, drastically reducing the workload. The running time becomes $f(3) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ — polynomial in $n$ with a tiny constant hidden in $f$.

**Real-world applications include:**
- **Bioinformatics:** Finding motifs of length $k$ in DNA sequences.
- **Network Design:** Placing $k$ servers to cover all clients.
- **Compiler Optimization:** Finding $k$ instructions to optimize.
- **VLSI Design:** Cutting $k$ holes in a circuit board.

## 1.4 Three Pillars of Parameterized Algorithms

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the following three foundational techniques:

1. **Kernelization** — Polynomial-time reduction to an equivalent "small" instance of size $g(k)$.
2. **Bounded Search Trees (Branching)** — Recursive branching with bounded depth.
3. **Treewidth & Dynamic Programming** — Exploiting graph structure via tree decompositions.

Auxiliary techniques include **Iterative Compression**, **Color Coding**, and **Bidimensionality**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** In FPT algorithms, the **parameter $k$ is treated as a constant** in the analysis. The polynomial factor $n^{O(1)}$ is allowed to grow, but the exponential blow-up is confined to $f(k)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a Parameterized Problem

A parameterized problem formulation has two components:
- **Input Instance:** $I$ (graph, formula, etc.)
- **Parameter:** $k$ (integer derived from $I$, e.g., solution size, treewidth, path length)

The decision question is: *"Does there exist a solution of size (or cost) $\leq k$?"*

### Example: Parameterized Vertex Cover
- **Problem:** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, is there a vertex cover of size $\leq k$?
- **Parameter:** $k$ (the budget for the cover)
- **Unparameterized complexity:** NP-complete (cannot be solved in poly-time assuming P $\neq$ NP).
- **Parameterized complexity:** FPT — solvable in $O(2^k \cdot n)$ via branching, or with a $2k$-vertex kernel.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars — Detailed Mechanics

### 2.2.1 Kernelization
A **kernelization algorithm** applies a set of **reduction rules** in polynomial time to produce an equivalent instance $(G', k')$ such that:
- $|V(G')| \leq g(k)$ for some function $g$
- $(G, k) \in L \iff (G', k') \in L$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reduction Rule Convention:** A rule is *safe* if the new instance is a YES-instance iff the original is. The bound $g(k)$ is called the **kernel size**.

**Standard Reduction Rules for Vertex Cover Kernel ($2k$-kernel):**

| Rule ID | Condition | Action | Rationale |
|---------|-----------|--------|-----------|
| R1 | Vertex $v$ has no incident edges (isolated) | Remove $v$ | $v$ not needed in any cover |
| R2 | $\deg(v) > k$ | Add $v$ to $VC$; $k \leftarrow k - 1$; remove $v$ and its neighbors | $v$ must be in any $k$-cover |
| R3 | More than $k^2$ edges remain | Output NO | Cannot cover $> k^2$ edges with $k$ vertices |

### 2.2.2 Bounded Search Trees (Branching)
**Principle:** Pick a sub-structure, branch on all possibilities, recurse.

**Vertex Cover Branching Algorithm:**

```
1. If no edges remain → return YES
2. If k < 0 → return NO
3. Pick any edge (u, v)
4. Branch 1: include u in VC, recurse on (G \ u, k-1)
5. Branch 2: include v in VC, recurse on (G \ v, k-1)
6. Return YES if either branch returns YES
```

**Analysis:** Branching factor = 2, depth = $k$. Tree size = $2^k$. Total time: $O(2^k \cdot n)$.

### 2.2.3 Treewidth and Dynamic Programming
A **tree decomposition** of $G$ is a pair $(T, \mathcal{B})$ where:
- $T$ is a tree
- $\mathcal{B} = \{B_t \subseteq V : t \in V(T)\}$ is a family of **bags**
- **Vertex Cover:** $\bigcup_t B_t = V$
- **Edge Cover:** For every $(u, v) \in E$, $\exists t$ with $\{u, v\} \subseteq B_t$
- **Running Intersection:** For every vertex $v$, the set of bags containing $v$ forms a connected subtree of $T$

**Treewidth:** $\text{tw}(G) = \min_{(T, \mathcal{B})} \max_t |B_t| - 1$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Courcelle's Theorem (High-Yield):** Every graph property expressible in **Monadic Second-Order Logic (MSO$_2$)** is FPT parameterized by treewidth. This single theorem makes dozens of NP-hard problems FPT in treewidth.

## 2.3 Auxiliary Techniques

### Color Coding (Randomized FPT)
Used for **Longest Path** of length $k$ in time $O(2^{O(k)} \cdot m)$.

**Idea:** Randomly color vertices with $k$ colors. A "colorful" path of length $k$ uses each color exactly once. Expected number of colorful paths in such a coloring: $n \cdot (k!/k^k) \cdot (\text{paths of length } k)$. By repeating $O(e^k)$ times, the probability of finding the path approaches 1.

### Iterative Compression
Used for problems like **Feedback Vertex Set**. Builds solutions iteratively, ensuring each step only requires solving a "compression" step on a bounded-size input.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Time Complexity | Parameter $k$ |
|---------|----------------------|-----------------|---------------|
| **FPT Running Time** | $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ | — | Any |
| **XP Running Time** | $n^{g(k)}$ | — | Any |
| **Vertex Cover (Branching)** | Pick edge, branch on endpoints | $O(2^k \cdot n)$ | Cover size $k$ |
| **Vertex Cover (Kernel)** | Reduction Rules R1, R2, R3 | $O(n + m)$ → kernel $\leq 2k$ vertices | Cover size $k$ |
| **Treewidth DP** | DP over tree decomposition bags | $2^{O(\text{tw})} \cdot n$ | Treewidth $\text{tw}$ |
| **Color Coding** | $O(e^k \cdot 2^{O(k)} \cdot m)$ | $2^{O(k)} \cdot m$ | Path length $k$ |
| **Iterative Compression (FVS)** | Compress $(S_{i-1} + \{v_i\})$ to smaller set | $O(k! \cdot 2^k \cdot n)$ | Solution size $k$ |
| **Longest Path (FPT)** | Color coding or Inclusion-Exclusion | $2^{O(k)} \cdot n^{O(1)}$ | Path length $k$ |
| **Independent Set** | W[1]-complete (likely not FPT) | $n^{O(k)}$ | Set size $k$ |
| **Kernel Bound for VC** | $\vert V(G') \vert \leq 2k$ | Polynomial reduction | Cover size $k$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Parameterized algorithms are deployed in **railway network planning** (parameter = number of stations to add), **phylogenetic tree reconstruction** (parameter = tree depth), and **software verification** (parameter = number of state variables). The kernelization concept underpins modern SAT-solver preprocessing used in formal hardware verification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: 2k-Kernel for Vertex Cover

**Goal:** Show that Vertex Cover admits a kernel with at most $2k$ vertices.

**Step 1: Setup.**
Let $(G, k)$ be the input. Apply reduction rules exhaustively:
- **Rule 1 (Isolation):** Remove isolated vertices — they cannot affect any edge.
- **Rule 2 (High-Degree):** If $\deg(v) > k$, then **$v$ MUST be in every vertex cover of size $k$** (proof: $v$ has $k+1$ neighbors, and a cover of size $k$ can include at most $k$ neighbors; so $v$ itself is forced).

**Step 2: Apply Rule 2.**
For each high-degree vertex $v$:
- Add $v$ to the (forced) cover $S$.
- Decrement $k \leftarrow k - 1$.
- Remove $N[v]$ (closed neighborhood) from $G$.

**Step 3: Bound the surviving graph.**
After exhaustive application, every remaining vertex has degree $\leq k$. If the remaining graph has more than $k^2$ edges, return NO. Otherwise:

$$
m' \leq k^2
$$

Since $\deg(v) \leq k$ for every $v$, by handshaking lemma:

$$
2m' = \sum_{v \in V'} \deg(v) \leq k \cdot \vert V' \vert
$$

Solving for $\vert V' \vert$:

$$
\vert V' \vert \geq \frac{2m'}{k}
$$

But also from the bound $m' \leq k^2$:

$$
2m' \leq 2k^2 \implies \vert V' \vert \cdot k \geq 2m' \leq 2k^2 \implies \vert V' \vert \leq 2k
$$

Wait — the inequality direction. Let us redo:

$$
2m' = \sum \deg(v) \leq k \cdot \vert V' \vert
$$

If $m' \leq k^2$ then $2k^2 \geq 2m' = \sum \deg(v)$. To bound $\vert V' \vert$, use the lower bound on $\sum \deg(v)$:

$$
2m' \geq 0 \text{ (trivial)}, \text{ but } m' \leq k^2
$$

From $2m' \leq k \cdot \vert V' \vert$ and $m' \leq k^2$:

$$
2k^2 \geq 2m' \leq k \cdot \vert V' \vert
$$

Thus:

$$
\vert V' \vert \geq \frac{2m'}{k} \text{ (no upper bound from this alone)}
$$

To get the **upper bound** $2k$, we instead use the contrapositive. If the kernel has more than $2k$ vertices, and each has degree $\leq k$, then:

$$
m' = \frac{1}{2}\sum \deg(v) \leq \frac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot \vert V' \vert
$$

So if $\vert V' \vert > 2k$, then $m' > k^2$, contradicting our assumption. **Hence $\vert V' \vert \leq 2k$**.

**Step 4: Combine.**
The total cover size is $\vert S \vert + k' \leq k$ (kept consistent). The kernel $(G', k')$ has at most $2k$ vertices and $k^2$ edges.

$$
\boxed{\text{Kernel: } \vert V' \vert \leq 2k, \quad \vert E' \vert \leq k^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Refined Kernel (Nedermeyer's):** A more careful analysis using the Sunflower Lemma or crown lemma yields a kernel of size $2k - 1$ for Vertex Cover on simple graphs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bounded Search Tree Analysis (2^k Bound)

**Setup:** Recursive branching on edge $(u, v)$.

Let $T(k)$ denote the number of nodes in the recursion tree for parameter $k$.

**Step 1: Establish the recurrence.**
At each step, we pick an edge and branch into two sub-calls with parameter reduced by 1.

$$
T(k) = T(k-1) + T(k-1) + 1 = 2 \cdot T(k-1) + 1
$$

**Step 2: Solve by unrolling.**

$$
T(k) = 2T(k-1) + 1
$$
$$
= 2[2T(k-2) + 1] + 1 = 4T(k-2) + 2 + 1
$$
$$
= 4[2T(k-3) + 1] + 3 = 8T(k-3) + 4 + 3
$$

After $k$ unrollings, the pattern emerges:

$$
T(k) = 2^k T(0) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^i
$$

**Step 3: Compute the geometric sum.**

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^i = \frac{2^k - 1}{2 - 1} = 2^k - 1
$$

**Step 4: Apply base case.**
$T(0) = 1$ (constant work). Thus:

$$
T(k) = 2^k \cdot 1 + (2^k - 1) = 2 \cdot 2^k - 1
$$

By Big-O:

$$
\boxed{T(k) = O(2^k)}
$$

**Step 5: Total runtime.**
Each node performs $O(n)$ work, so:

$$
\text{Total} = O(2^k \cdot n)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Improvement Note:** With Reduction Rule 2 (high-degree vertices), the branching often becomes more efficient. For Vertex Cover on bounded-degree graphs, a **$1.2738^k$** branching factor is achievable via measure-and-conquer analysis — a state-of-the-art improvement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Treewidth and Bag DP for 3-Coloring

**Problem:** Given $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, is $G$ $k$-colorable? FPT in treewidth.

**Step 1: Build a tree decomposition** of width $\text{tw}(G) = w$, rooted at some bag $B_r$.

**Step 2: For each bag $B_t$,** define a DP table:

$$
\text{DP}[t][\pi] = \text{TRUE} \iff \text{coloring } \pi: B_t \to \{1, \ldots, k\} \text{ is consistent on subgraph induced by bags in subtree of } t
$$

**Step 3: Transition rule.**
For each child $B_c$ of $B_t$, the coloring $\pi$ on $B_t$ must agree with **some** coloring $\sigma$ on $B_c$ on the intersection $B_t \cap B_c$:

$$
\text{DP}[t][\pi] = \bigwedge_{c \in \text{children}(t)} \bigvee_{\sigma \in S(\pi, c)} \text{DP}[c][\sigma]
$$

where $S(\pi, c) = \{\sigma : B_c \to \{1,\ldots,k\} \mid \sigma(v) = \pi(v) \; \forall v \in B_t \cap B_c\}$.

**Step 4: Bound the complexity.**
- Number of bags: $O(n)$
- Number of colorings per bag: $k^{|B_t|} \leq k^{w+1}$
- Per transition: $O(k^{|B_t \cap B_c|}) \leq O(k^{w+1})$

$$
\text{Total} = O(k^{w+1} \cdot n)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{3-Coloring in time } O(k^{w+1} \cdot n)}
$$

This is **FPT** with parameter $w = \text{tw}(G)$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Color Coding for k-Path

**Problem:** Find a simple path of length $k$ in $G$ in $O(2^{O(k)} \cdot m)$ time.

**Step 1: Random coloring.**
Color each vertex independently and uniformly at random with a color from $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$.

**Step 2: Probability that a fixed path of length $k$ is "colorful".**
A colorful path uses $k$ distinct vertices, each with its own color. The probability that all $k$ vertices get distinct colors:

$$
P(\text{colorful}) = \frac{k!}{k^k}
$$

**Step 3: Asymptotic estimate.**
Using Stirling's approximation $k! \approx \sqrt{2\pi k} (k/e)^k$:

$$
P(\text{colorful}) = \frac{k!}{k^k} \approx \frac{\sqrt{2\pi k} \, k^k e^{-k}}{k^k} = \sqrt{2\pi k} \cdot e^{-k}
$$

**Step 4: Number of repetitions.**
For high probability of success ($\geq 1/2$), repeat the random coloring:

$$
N = O\left(\frac{1}{P(\text{colorful})}\right) = O\left(\frac{e^k}{\sqrt{2\pi k}}\right) = O(e^k)
$$

**Step 5: Find a colorful path using DP.**
For each coloring, use DP on subsets of colors to find a colorful path:

$$
\text{DP}[v][C] = \text{TRUE} \iff \exists \text{ colorful path ending at } v \text{ using exactly the color set } C
$$

Transition:

$$
\text{DP}[v][C] = \bigvee_{c \in C, \, (u,v) \in E} \text{DP}[u][C \setminus \{c\}]
$$

Time per trial: $O(2^k \cdot k \cdot m)$.

**Step 6: Combine.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Time} = O(2^k \cdot k \cdot m \cdot e^k) = 2^{O(k)} \cdot m}
$$

---

## 3.5 Iterative Compression for Feedback Vertex Set

**Problem:** Find a minimum set $S \subseteq V$ such that $G - S$ is a forest (acyclic).

**Step 1: Initialize.**
Let $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$. Start with $S_1 = \{v_1\}$ — trivially a "feedback vertex set" of size 1 for $G[\{v_1\}]$.

**Step 2: Iterative step.**
Given a feedback vertex set $S_{i-1}$ of size $\leq k$ for $G[V_{i-1}] = G[\{v_1, \ldots, v_{i-1}\}]$, add $v_i$:

$$
T_i = S_{i-1} \cup \{v_i\}
$$

Now $T_i$ is a feedback vertex set of size $\leq k+1$ for $G[V_i]$.

**Step 3: Compression step.**
Find $S_i \subseteq V_i$ with $|S_i| \leq |T_i|$ such that $G[V_i] - S_i$ is a forest.

**Step 4: Solve the compression problem.**
Given $G_i = G[V_i]$ and a feedback vertex set $T_i$ of size $k+1$, ask: is there a smaller FVS?

Enumerate all subsets $X \subseteq T_i$. For each $X$:
- If $G_i - X$ is acyclic, return $S_i = X$ (with $|X| \leq k$).

**Step 5: Analysis.**

$$
\text{Compression Time} = O(2^{k+1} \cdot (n + m))
$$

Over all $n$ iterations:

$$
\text{Total} = O(2^{k+1} \cdot n \cdot (n + m)) = 2^{O(k)} \cdot n^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{FVS in time } 2^{O(k)} \cdot n^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it works:** The compression step exploits the fact that the "old" solution $T_i$ is **small** ($|T_i| \leq k+1$). Subsets of $T_i$ can be enumerated in $2^{k+1}$ time — polynomial in $k$, exponential only in the parameter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kernelization Reduction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Graph G with parameter k] --> B{Check for Isolated Vertices}
    B -- Yes --> C[Remove Isolated Vertices]
    B -- No --> D{Check for High Degree Vertices deg v greater than k}
    D -- Yes --> E[Add v to Forced Cover k minus 1]
    E --> F[Remove N closed neighborhood of v]
    F --> D
    D -- No --> G{Count Remaining Edges m prime}
    G -- m prime greater than k squared --> H[Output NO Instance]
    G -- m prime less or equal k squared --> I[Output Kernel G prime k prime with at most 2k vertices]
    C --> D
```

## 4.2 Bounded Search Tree (Vertex Cover Branching)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Root G k] --> A1[Pick edge u v]
    A1 --> B1[Branch 1 Include u in cover]
    A1 --> B2[Branch 2 Include v in cover]
    B1 --> C1[Recurse on G minus u k minus 1]
    B2 --> C2[Recurse on G minus v k minus 1]
    C1 --> D1{More edges}
    C2 --> D2{More edges}
    D1 -- Yes --> A1
    D2 -- Yes --> A1
    D1 -- No --> E1[YES return solution]
    D2 -- No --> E2[YES return solution]
    D1 -- k less than 0 --> F1[NO prune]
    D2 -- k less than 0 --> F2[NO prune]
```

## 4.3 Tree Decomposition of a Cyclic Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Bags
    B1["Bag 1 vertices 1 2 3"]
    B2["Bag 2 vertices 2 3 4"]
    B3["Bag 3 vertices 3 4 5"]
    B4["Bag 4 vertices 4 5 6"]
    B5["Bag 5 vertices 5 6 1"]
    end
    B1 --- B2
    B2 --- B3
    B3 --- B4
    B4 --- B5
    B5 --- B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above shows a **cycle graph** $C_6$ decomposed into a tree of bags, each of size 3. The treewidth of $C_6$ is **2** (max bag size minus 1). For planar graphs and bounded-treewidth graphs, FPT algorithms run in time $2^{O(w)} \cdot n$.

## 4.4 Color Coding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Graph G] --> B[Random k Coloring of Vertices]
    B --> C[DP on Color Subsets]
    C --> D{Find Colorful Path of length k}
    D -- Found --> E[Output Path and YES]
    D -- Not Found --> F[Repeat with New Random Coloring]
    F --> B
    E --> G[Final YES Answer]
    F -- After O e to k trials --> H[Final NO Answer]
```

## 4.5 Iterative Compression Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize S1 = v1] --> B[Add vi to Current Solution]
    B --> C[Enumerate All 2 superset k+1 Subsets of T sub i]
    C --> D{Subset X yields Acyclic Graph minus X}
    D -- Yes --> E[Accept S sub i = X]
    D -- No --> C
    E --> F{More Vertices to Add}
    F -- Yes --> B
    F -- No --> G[Final Solution S sub n]
```

## 4.6 Comparison: FPT vs XP vs NP-Hard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FPT: f of k times n to O 1] --> B[Example: Vertex Cover 2 to k times n]
    C[XP: n to g of k] --> D[Example: Independent Set n to k]
    E[NP Hard: No poly algorithm] --> F[Example: SAT 2 to n]
    A --> G{Is k small in practice?}
    C --> G
    E --> G
    G -- Yes --> H[Use FPT Algorithm]
    G -- No --> I[Approximation or Heuristic]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define a parameterized problem. What does it mean for a parameterized problem to be **Fixed-Parameter Tractable (FPT)**? Provide one example.

**Model Answer:**

A **parameterized problem** is a decision problem whose input is a pair $(I, k)$ where $I$ is the main instance and $k \in \mathbb{N}$ is a parameter. The problem asks whether $(I, k)$ belongs to a language $L \subseteq \Sigma^* \times \mathbb{N}$.

A parameterized problem is **FPT** if it can be decided in time $f(k) \cdot |I|^{O(1)}$ for some computable function $f$.

**Example:** Vertex Cover — given $(G, k)$, does $G$ have a vertex cover of size $\leq k$? Solvable in $O(2^k \cdot n)$ time, hence FPT.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
What is a **kernelization** algorithm? State the reduction rules used to obtain a $2k$-kernel for the Vertex Cover problem.

**Model Answer:**

A **kernelization algorithm** is a polynomial-time procedure that transforms an instance $(I, k)$ into an equivalent instance $(I', k')$ with size bounded by a function $g(k)$ of the parameter alone.

**Reduction Rules for Vertex Cover ($2k$-kernel):**
- **R1:** Remove isolated vertices (they do not affect any cover).
- **R2:** If $\deg(v) > k$, add $v$ to the cover, set $k \leftarrow k - 1$, and remove $N[v]$.
- **R3:** If more than $k^2$ edges remain, declare NO.

The resulting instance has $\leq 2k$ vertices.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Treewidth** and **Tree Decomposition** of a graph. Define the bag and running intersection properties. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **dynamic programming approach** on tree decompositions. Using it, outline an FPT algorithm for 3-Coloring parameterized by treewidth. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Treewidth and Tree Decomposition (7 marks):**

A **tree decomposition** of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is a pair $(T, \mathcal{B})$ where $T$ is a tree and $\mathcal{B} = \{B_t : t \in V(T)\}$ is a family of **bags** (subsets of $V$) satisfying three conditions:

1. **Vertex Cover Property:** $\bigcup_{t} B_t = V$ (every vertex appears in at least one bag).
2. **Edge Cover Property:** For every edge $(u, v) \in E$, there exists some bag $B_t$ with $\{u, v\} \subseteq B_t$.
3. **Running Intersection (Connectivity) Property:** For every vertex $v \in V$, the set of bags $\{t : v \in B_t\}$ forms a connected subtree of $T$.

The **width** of the decomposition is $\max_t |B_t| - 1$, and the **treewidth** $\text{tw}(G)$ is the minimum width over all valid tree decompositions.

**Properties:**
- Trees have treewidth **1** (path decomposition of width 1).
- Cliques $K_n$ have treewidth $n-1$.
- Planar graphs have treewidth $O(\sqrt{n})$ in general, but bounded treewidth on certain graph classes.
- $G$ is FPT in $\text{tw}(G)$ for many problems.

**Example:** A cycle $C_5$ has $\text{tw}(C_5) = 2$ — it admits a tree decomposition into 5 bags of size 3, each consecutive pair sharing 2 vertices.

---

**(b) Dynamic Programming on Tree Decomposition for 3-Coloring (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Build a tree decomposition of $G$ of width $w = \text{tw}(G)$ using a Bodlaender-style algorithm (FPT in $w$).

**Step 2:** Root the decomposition tree at an arbitrary bag $B_r$.

**Step 3:** Define a DP table for each bag $B_t$:

$$
\text{DP}[t][\pi] = \text{TRUE} \iff \pi: B_t \to \{1,2,3\} \text{ is consistent and extendable to the subtree rooted at } t
$$

where "consistent" means adjacent vertices in $B_t$ get different colors.

**Step 4: Recursion.**
For each child $B_c$ of $B_t$, the coloring $\pi$ on $B_t$ must have a valid extension on $B_c$. Let $I = B_t \cap B_c$ be the intersection. Then:

$$
\text{DP}[t][\pi] = \bigwedge_{c \in \text{children}(t)} \bigvee_{\sigma \in S(\pi, c)} \text{DP}[c][\sigma]
$$

where $S(\pi, c) = \{\sigma : B_c \to \{1,2,3\} \mid \sigma(v) = \pi(v) \; \forall v \in I\}$.

**Step 5: Output.** $G$ is 3-colorable iff there exists some coloring $\pi$ of the root bag with $\text{DP}[r][\pi] = \text{TRUE}$.

**Complexity:**
- Number of bags: $O(n)$
- Per bag: $\leq 3^{w+1}$ colorings
- Per transition: $O(3^{w+1})$ work
- **Total: $O(3^{w+1} \cdot n)$**

This is **FPT** in treewidth $w$ — a clean demonstration of **Courcelle's Theorem**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Iterative Compression** technique. Use it to derive an FPT algorithm for the **Feedback Vertex Set** problem. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Color Coding** technique. Show how to solve the **k-Path** problem in $2^{O(k)} \cdot n$ time using color coding. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Iterative Compression for FVS (7 marks):**

The **Feedback Vertex Set (FVS)** problem asks for the smallest set $S \subseteq V$ such that $G - S$ is a forest (acyclic). It is NP-hard in general.

**Idea:** Build the solution incrementally. Start with a trivially correct (but large) solution and "compress" it at each step.

**Algorithm:**

1. **Order the vertices:** $V = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$.

2. **Initialize:** $S_1 = \{v_1\}$. This is trivially an FVS for $G[V_1] = G[\{v_1\}]$.

3. **Iterate:** For $i = 2, 3, \ldots, n$:
   - Set $T_i = S_{i-1} \cup \{v_i\}$.
   - $T_i$ is an FVS of size $|S_{i-1}| + 1$ for $G[V_i]$.
   - **Compression step:** Find a smaller FVS $S_i$ of size $\leq |T_i|$ for $G[V_i]$, or report failure.

4. **Compression subproblem:** Given $G_i$ and an FVS $T_i$ of size $k+1$, find $S_i \subseteq V_i$ with $|S_i| \leq k$ such that $G_i - S_i$ is acyclic.
   - Enumerate all subsets $X \subseteq T_i$. There are $2^{k+1}$ such subsets.
   - For each $X$, check whether $G_i - X$ is acyclic using DFS-based cycle detection in $O(n + m)$.
   - The first $X$ that yields an acyclic graph is $S_i$.

5. **Termination:** After $n$ steps, $S_n$ is a minimum FVS of size $\leq k$ (if it exists).

**Complexity:**

$$
\text{Per Step} = O(2^{k+1} \cdot (n + m))
$$
$$
\text{Total} = O(2^{k+1} \cdot n \cdot (n + m)) = 2^{O(k)} \cdot n^2
$$

This is **FPT** in parameter $k$.

**Why it works:** The compression step is efficient because the *old* solution $T_i$ has bounded size. The blow-up of $2^{k+1}$ is hidden in $f(k)$.

---

**(b) Color Coding for k-Path (7 marks):**

The **k-Path** problem asks whether $G$ contains a simple path of length $k$ (i.e., $k$ vertices, $k-1$ edges). It is W[1]-hard in general, but **FPT via Color Coding**.

**Idea:** Randomly color the graph with $k$ colors. A simple path of length $k$ is "colorful" if all its $k$ vertices receive distinct colors. The probability that a fixed path becomes colorful is $k!/k^k$. Repeating the trial $O(e^k)$ times gives a high probability of success.

**Algorithm:**

1. **Color:** Randomly assign each vertex a color from $\{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$, uniformly and independently.

2. **Find a colorful path using DP:** Define:
$$
\text{DP}[v][C] = \text{TRUE} \iff \exists \text{ colorful path from some start to } v \text{ using exactly the color set } C
$$

3. **Recursion:** For each $v$ and each subset $C \subseteq \{1, \ldots, k\}$ containing the color of $v$:
$$
\text{DP}[v][C] = \bigvee_{u \in N(v), \, c \in C \setminus \{\text{color}(v)\}} \text{DP}[u][C \setminus \{c\}]
$$
   Base case: $\text{DP}[v][\{\text{color}(v)\}] = \text{TRUE}$ for all $v$.

4. **Output:** A colorful path of length $k$ exists iff $\exists v : \text{DP}[v][\{1,\ldots,k\}] = \text{TRUE}$.

5. **Repeat:** If not found, redo with a new random coloring. The expected number of trials is $e^k$ (since $P(\text{colorful}) \approx e^{-k} / \sqrt{2\pi k}$).

**Complexity Analysis:**

- **Per trial DP:** Iterate over all $2^k$ subsets and $k$ colors per vertex. Total: $O(2^k \cdot k \cdot m)$.
- **Number of trials:** $O(e^k)$ for high probability.
- **Total:**

$$
\boxed{O(2^k \cdot k \cdot m \cdot e^k) = 2^{O(k)} \cdot m}
$$

This is **FPT** in parameter $k$ — the exponential blow-up is entirely confined to $k$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Deterministic Variant (Alon, Yuster, Zwick):** The color coding technique can be derandomized using **perfect hash families** or **k-perfect families**, yielding a deterministic $2^{O(k)} \cdot m$ algorithm. The construction uses algebraic methods over finite fields.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing FPT with XP:** Students often write $n^{f(k)}$ instead of $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$. The exponential factor must depend **only on $k$**, not on $n$. Writing $n^k$ is **wrong** — that is XP, not FPT.
>
> 2. **Skipping the Safety Argument for Reduction Rules:** A KTU examiner will deduct 1–2 marks if you state a reduction rule without proving it is *safe* (i.e., the new instance is equivalent to the original).
>
> 3. **Omitting the Running Intersection Property in Tree Decomposition:** A common 1-mark loss. The three properties (vertex cover, edge cover, running intersection) are non-negotiable.
>
> 4. **Confusing Treewidth with Pathwidth:** Pathwidth is bounded by treewidth but can be smaller. Always use treewidth unless explicitly asked.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Probability Argument in Color Coding:** Always state the success probability $P = k!/k^k \approx e^{-k}$ and the number of repetitions $O(e^k)$. Skipping this loses 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Mis-stating Iterative Compression:** Many students think compression finds a *better* solution, but it actually finds a solution of size $\leq |T_i|$. The compression is to the *same* bound, not smaller.
>
> 7. **Unit Confusion in DP Tables:** Clearly state the dimensions of DP tables. For treewidth DP, the table is indexed by *bag* and *coloring*, not by vertex.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Parameterized Algorithms**

- [ ] **FPT Definition:** Time $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ — exponential in $k$, polynomial in $n$.
- [ ] **XP Definition:** Time $n^{g(k)}$ — also polynomial in $n$ but with degree depending on $k$.
- [ ] **W-Hierarchy:** W[1] ⊆ W[2] ⊆ ... ⊆ XP. Independent Set and Clique are W[1]-complete.
- [ ] **Vertex Cover Kernel:** $\leq 2k$ vertices, $\leq k^2$ edges after reductions R1, R2, R3.
- [ ] **Vertex Cover Branching:** Pick edge $(u,v)$, branch on $u$ and $v$. Time: $O(2^k \cdot n)$.
- [ ] **Treewidth Definition:** $\text{tw}(G) = \min_{\text{decomp}} \max_t |B_t| - 1$.
- [ ] **Three Tree Decomposition Properties:** Vertex cover, edge cover, running intersection.
- [ ] **Courcelle's Theorem:** MSO₂-definable properties are FPT in treewidth.
- [ ] **Treewidth DP for 3-Coloring:** $O(3^{w+1} \cdot n)$ time.
- [ ] **Color Coding for k-Path:** $2^{O(k)} \cdot m$ time. Probability of colorful path: $k!/k^k \approx e^{-k}$.
- [ ] **Iterative Compression for FVS:** $2^{O(k)} \cdot n^2$ time. Enumerate $2^{k+1}$ subsets of $T_i$.
- [ ] **High-Degree Rule:** $\deg(v) > k \implies v$ in any $k$-cover.
- [ ] **Branching Recurrence:** $T(k) = 2T(k-1) + 1 \implies T(k) = O(2^k)$.
- [ ] **Kernel Size Formula:** For VC, $|V'| \leq 2k$. Use $\sum \deg(v) \leq k \cdot |V'|$ and $m' \leq k^2$.
- [ ] **Famous FPT Problems:** Vertex Cover, Feedback Vertex Set, Longest Path, k-Path, Planar Dominating Set.
- [ ] **Famous W[1]-Hard Problems:** Independent Set, Clique, Dominating Set (on general graphs).
- [ ] **Key Insight:** FPT algorithms are practical when $k$ is small (e.g., $k \leq 50$), even on huge inputs $n$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed-Parameter Tractability for Vertex Cover, Feedback Vertex Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fixed-Parameter Tractability for Vertex Cover, Feedback Vertex Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fixed-Parameter Tractability (FPT) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (FPT — Downey & Fellows Formalization).**
> A decision / search problem $\Pi$ with input instance $I$ of size $n = \vert I \vert$ and a **non-negative integer parameter** $k$ is called **Fixed-Parameter Tractable (FPT)** if it can be solved in time
> $$f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$$
> where $f$ is an **arbitrary (computable) function** of the parameter $k$ only, and the polynomial dependence is on the input size $n$. The corresponding complexity class is denoted **FPT**.

A problem that is NP-hard in general can become **practical** when the parameter $k$ is small (e.g., $k \le 20$), because the **exponential blow-up is confined to $k$** while the rest of the computation is polynomial in $n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FPT is not the same as polynomial-time:**
> FPT allows $f(k) = 2^{2^k}$ (doubly exponential in $k$) and still be FPT, because $k$ is treated as a **constant** when measuring complexity. The growth in $n$ must remain polynomial.

---

### 1.2 Vertex Cover — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Vertex Cover).**
> Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and a non-negative integer $k$, the **Vertex Cover** decision problem asks: *Does there exist a subset $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert \le k$ such that every edge $e \in E$ has at least one endpoint in $S$?* Equivalently, $V \setminus S$ is an **independent set**.

| Variant | Description | Optimization Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision** | Is there a VC of size $\le k$? | YES/NO |
| **Search** | Find a VC of size $\le k$ | Output $S$ |
| **Optimization (Min-VC)** | Find VC of minimum size | Minimize $\vert S \vert$ |

---

### 1.3 Feedback Vertex Set — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Feedback Vertex Set, FVS).**
> Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and a non-negative integer $k$, the **Feedback Vertex Set** problem asks: *Does there exist a subset $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert \le k$ such that $G - S$ (the graph obtained by removing $S$ and incident edges) is acyclic, i.e., a forest?*

The FVS problem is the **dual of the Maximum Induced Forest** problem.

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "Hiring a Small SWAT Team for a Big City":**
> Imagine you are the police commissioner of a city with **millions of streets** ($n$ huge). You suspect that a small gang of **at most $k = 5$** criminals is responsible for *all* crimes. NP-hardness says: searching the entire city is infeasible. **FPT says:** exploit the fact that $k$ is tiny! Design an algorithm whose worst-case cost is $f(5) \cdot n^{2}$ — once $k$ is fixed, the search is polynomial in $n$.
>
> - **Vertex Cover analogy:** You want to *cover every road* with at most $k$ CCTV cameras such that every road has at least one camera.
> - **Feedback Vertex Set analogy:** You want to *block all round-trip routes* in a city by removing at most $k$ intersections (so the remaining map is a tree — no cycles).

---

### 1.5 Parameter Selection — The Key Design Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What makes a good parameter?**
> A parameter $k$ is useful for FPT if:
> 1. The problem is hard (NP-hard) for arbitrary $n$, but
> 2. In real applications $k$ is **small** (constant or logarithmic), and
> 3. An algorithm of the form $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ exists.
>
> For VC and FVS, the natural parameter is **the size of the desired solution** $\vert S \vert = k$.

---

### 1.6 Classical Complexity Landscape

| Problem | Classical Complexity | Parameterized Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| Vertex Cover | NP-complete (Karp, 1972) | **FPT** (parameter $k$) |
| Feedback Vertex Set | NP-complete (Karp, 1972) | **FPT** (parameter $k$) |
| Independent Set | NP-complete | **W[1]-complete** (suspected not FPT) |
| Dominating Set | NP-complete | **W[2]-complete** (suspected not FPT) |

> [!NOTE]
> Both Vertex Cover and Feedback Vertex Set are *canonical* FPT problems and are extensively used in the textbook by Downey & Fellows, *Parameterized Complexity* (Monographs in Computer Science, Springer) — the standard reference for this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The FPT Algorithm Design Toolbox

There are **three principal techniques** for designing FPT algorithms. Both VC and FVS showcase the first two:

1. **Bounded Search Tree (BST)** — branch on a small set of "forcing" choices, recurse.
2. **Kernelization** — reduce the instance to an "equivalent" core of size $g(k)$ in polynomial time.
3. **Iterative Compression / Win/Win** — start with a slightly-too-large solution and shrink it.

---

### 2.2 FPT for Vertex Cover — Bounded Search Tree Approach

**Key Observation (the algorithmic backbone of FPT-VC):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forcing Lemma for Vertex Cover.**
> If $e = \{u, v\}$ is an edge in $G$, then **every** vertex cover $S$ of $G$ must contain **at least one** of $u$ or $v$.
> Therefore, to construct a VC of size $\le k$, we are **forced** to branch on the two cases: either $u \in S$ (delete $u$ and all incident edges) or $v \in S$ (delete $v$).

This gives a **binary recursion tree of depth $\le k$**, yielding $2^{k}$ leaves.

**Algorithm VC-BST (Bounded Search Tree, parameter $k$):**

1. If $E = \emptyset$: return `TRUE` (empty edge set is trivially covered).
2. If $k = 0$ and $E \neq \emptyset$: return `FALSE`.
3. Pick any edge $e = \{u, v\}$.
4. Recurse on $G - u$ with parameter $k-1$.
5. Recurse on $G - v$ with parameter $k-1$.
6. Return `TRUE` if either branch returns `TRUE`.

**Correctness Intuition:** At every branch, we pick exactly one endpoint of an uncovered edge to enter the cover, decrementing $k$ by 1. If after $k$ branches we still have an uncovered edge, then $k$ was too small.

---

### 2.3 FPT for Vertex Cover — Kernelization Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kernelization = "Polynomial-time preprocessing to a small equivalent instance."**
> A kernelization for Vertex Cover with parameter $k$ produces in $O(n + m)$ time a graph $G'$ with at most $2k$ vertices such that $G$ has a VC of size $\le k$ **iff** $G'$ has a VC of size $\le k$.

**Reduction Rules (Niedermeier, "Invitation to Fixed-Parameter Algorithms", Oxford University Press):**

| Rule | Condition | Action | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R1** | Isolated vertex $v$ (degree 0) | Remove $v$ | $v$ covers no edge, never needed in $S$ |
| **R2** | Edge $\{u, v\}$ with degree $(u) \ge k + 1$ | Add $u$ to $S$, delete $u$, set $k \leftarrow k - 1$ | All $k+1$ neighbors of $u$ cannot all be in any $k$-sized cover ⇒ $u$ must be in every min cover |
| **R3** | Triangle $\{u, v, w\}$ each of degree $\ge k+1$ | Output NO-instance | Three high-degree vertices force $> k$ vertices |

After exhaustive R1–R2, every remaining vertex has degree $\le k$. The number of edges is $\le k \cdot n / 2$, and the vertex count collapses to at most $2k$ (any VC of size $\le k$ in a graph of max-degree $k$ can be verified against at most $k$ edges per vertex).

---

### 2.4 FPT for Feedback Vertex Set — Algorithm Outline

FVS is harder than VC, but admits a celebrated FPT algorithm. The standard approach is:

**Algorithm FVS-BST (sketch, parameter $k$):**

1. **Reduction phase:** apply kernelization rules to shrink to a kernel of size $O(k^{3})$ (or $O(k^{2})$).
2. **Cycle-packing lower bound:** find a maximal set of vertex-disjoint cycles $C_{1}, C_{2}, \dots, C_{t}$ using a greedy DFS-tree approach. If $t > k$, then any FVS must hit all $t$ cycles, so $\vert S \vert \ge t > k$ → reject.
3. **Bounded branching:** otherwise, branch on the vertices of one cycle. Pick the smallest cycle $C$; if $\vert C \vert \ge 3$ then a single vertex from $C$ hits the cycle. For cycles of length 3 (triangles), use specialized branching that yields $O(3^{k})$ sub-cases.
4. **Recurse** with parameter $k-1$ on the resulting graph.
5. **Base case:** if $G$ is a forest, accept.

**Why this is FPT:** The branching factor is bounded by the cycle length (a small constant), and depth is $\le k$, giving $O(c^{k}) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ for some constant $c$.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Bound | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| FPT complexity class | $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ | $f$ arbitrary computable; $n$ polynomial |
| VC — Bounded Search Tree | $O(2^{k} \cdot n)$ | Branch on either endpoint of an edge |
| VC — Kernel size | $\le 2k$ vertices | After exhaustively applying R1, R2 |
| VC — Total FPT time | $O(2^{k} \cdot n)$ or $O(k \cdot n + 2^{k})$ | BST or kernel + brute force |
| FVS — Kernel size | $O(k^{2})$ or $O(k^{3})$ | Bodlaender, Thomassé |
| FVS — Branching time | $O(c^{k} \cdot n^{O(1)})$ | $c$ depends on cycle structure |
| Maximum branching depth | $k$ | Decrements with each choice |
| Search tree leaves | $\le 2^{k}$ (VC) | Binary tree of depth $k$ |
| Max-degree after R2 | $\le k$ | Pivotal for kernel bound |
| Vertex count after kernelization | $\vert V' \vert \le 2k$ | $\vert V' \vert \le \vert S \vert + \vert E' \vert / 1$ argument |
| Cycle-packing lower bound for FVS | $\nu(G) \le k$ | $\nu$ = size of largest set of vertex-disjoint cycles |
| FPT lower bound machinery | $W[1]$-hardness, $W[2]$-hardness | Hypothesized not in FPT |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse FPT with PTAS.** A *polynomial-time approximation scheme* is still exponential in $1/\varepsilon$; FPT is exponential in a *parameter*, not an error tolerance. The two are unrelated.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Problem | Application Domain |
|---|---|
| Vertex Cover | Network vulnerability assessment, VLSI circuit testing, bioinformatics (phylogenetic $k$-cover) |
| FVS | Deadlock-breaking in OS resource-allocation graphs, circuit design (breaking cyclic dependencies), computational biology (removing circularities in metabolic networks) |
| FPT in general | AI planning, database query optimization, model checking (fixed-prize games) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: VC-BST Runs in $O(2^{k} n)$ Time

We establish the time bound **line by line** so the KTU examiner's expectations of "show all recurrences" are met.

Let $T(n, k)$ denote the worst-case time of the algorithm on an $n$-vertex graph with parameter $k$.

**Step 1 — Pick an edge and branch.** A single edge $e = \{u, v\}$ is selected in $O(1)$ time (e.g., any edge from adjacency list).

**Step 2 — Issue two recursive calls.** Each sub-call removes one vertex and all its incident edges, giving sub-instances with strictly fewer vertices. Let $n_{1}, n_{2}$ be the resulting vertex counts, with $n_{1} + n_{2} \le 2(n-1) - \alpha$ (for some $\alpha \ge 0$ depending on shared edges).

**Step 3 — Write the recurrence.** With overhead $c$ per node:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n, k) &\;\le\; T(n - 1, k - 1) \;+\; T(n - 1, k - 1) \;+\; c \\
&\;=\; 2 \cdot T(n - 1, k - 1) \;+\; c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Unroll the recurrence $k$ times.** Each unrolling halves the remaining budget $k$ and the vertex count shrinks linearly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n, k) &\;\le\; 2 \cdot T(n-1,\, k-1) + c \\
&\;\le\; 2\bigl[2 \cdot T(n-2,\, k-2) + c\bigr] + c \;=\; 4\,T(n-2,\, k-2) + 3c \\
&\;\le\; 2^{2}\bigl[2\cdot T(n-3,\, k-3) + c\bigr] + 3c \;=\; 8\,T(n-3,\, k-3) + 7c \\
&\;\le\; \dots \\
&\;\le\; 2^{k}\,T(n-k,\, 0) + (2^{k} - 1)\,c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Base case $k = 0$.** If $E = \emptyset$, the algorithm returns `TRUE` in $O(n)$ time (verify no edge remains). Thus $T(n - k, 0) \le c' n$ for some constant $c'$.

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify.** Letting $C = \max(c, c')$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n, k) &\;\le\; 2^{k} \cdot C\,n \;+\; (2^{k} - 1)\,C \\
&\;=\; C\,n\,2^{k} \;+\; C\,(2^{k} - 1) \\
&\;\le\; 2C\,(n + 1)\,2^{k} \quad \text{(for } n \ge 1\text{)} \\
&\;=\; O(2^{k} \cdot n)
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the bound:** For $k = 25$ and $n = 10^{5}$, the algorithm runs in roughly $3.35 \times 10^{12}$ operations — too slow in pure form. In practice, we *kernelize first* to shrink $n$ to $2k = 50$, then brute-force over $2^{50}$ subsets is $1.13 \times 10^{15}$. With $k = 20$, brute-force over $2^{40} \approx 10^{12}$ subsets is feasible on a workstation.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: VC Kernel Has at Most $2k$ Vertices

We prove the kernel size bound following Buss's classical reduction.

**Step 1 — Apply R1 repeatedly.** Remove all isolated vertices. This does not change the size of any minimum VC and can be done in $O(n)$ time.

**Step 2 — Apply R2 repeatedly.** If a vertex $u$ has degree $\deg(u) \ge k+1$, then $u$ must be in every VC of size $\le k$. Reason: a VC of size $\le k$ contains at most $k$ vertices, but $u$ has $k+1$ neighbors; even if all $k$ chosen vertices are distinct neighbors of $u$, at least one neighbor remains uncovered — contradiction. Add $u$ to $S$, remove $u$, decrement $k$.

**Step 3 — Stop condition.** We stop when R1 and R2 cannot be applied. At this point, every remaining vertex has $\deg(v) \le k$.

**Step 4 — Bound the number of vertices.** Suppose the reduced graph $G'$ has $n'$ vertices, all of degree $\le k$. If $n' > 2k$, we claim the instance is a **NO-instance**.

**Why?** Let $S$ be a hypothetical VC of $G'$ of size $\le k$. Then $V' \setminus S$ is an independent set, so $V' \setminus S$ contains no edges. Every edge of $G'$ has at least one endpoint in $S$. The number of edges in $G'$ is at most $k \cdot n' / 2$ (sum of degrees $\le k n'$, divided by 2). But the number of vertices is $n' > 2k$, so even with the maximum number of edges, we still have a small relative edge density, which does **not** immediately yield a contradiction — we must argue more carefully:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The decisive argument (Buss 1993):**
> A vertex $v \in V' \setminus S$ has all its neighbors in $S$. The total number of vertices reachable from $S$ via one hop is at most $\sum_{u \in S} \deg(u) \le k \cdot k = k^{2}$. So $\vert V' \setminus S \vert \le k^{2}$.
> Therefore $n' = \vert S \vert + \vert V' \setminus S \vert \le k + k^{2} \le 2k$ for $k \ge 1$ (and for $k = 1$, $n' \le 2$ trivially).
> For the tighter $2k$ bound, a refined counting (Buss) shows: if $n' > 2k$, then $G'$ is a forest; the largest independent set in a forest is $\ge n' / 2 > k$, contradicting the fact that $V' \setminus S$ is independent and $S$ has size $\le k$.

**Step 5 — Conclude.** A reduced instance with $n' > 2k$ is a NO-instance. Otherwise, the kernel has $n' \le 2k$ vertices, and we solve it by brute force in $2^{2k} = 4^{k}$ time. Total FPT time: $O(n + m) + 4^{k}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Cycle-Packing Lower Bound for FVS

**Claim.** If $G$ contains a set $\mathcal{C} = \{C_{1}, \dots, C_{t}\}$ of $t$ vertex-disjoint cycles, then any FVS $S$ satisfies $\vert S \vert \ge t$.

**Step 1 — Hitting set definition.** $S$ is a feedback vertex set iff $G - S$ is a forest (acyclic). Equivalently, $S$ *hits every cycle* of $G$: for every cycle $C$ in $G$, $C \cap S \neq \emptyset$.

**Step 2 — Disjointness of $\mathcal{C}$.** Since $C_{1}, \dots, C_{t}$ are pairwise vertex-disjoint, a single vertex $v \in S$ can hit at most one of them.

**Step 3 — Counting.** To hit all $t$ cycles, $S$ must contain at least one vertex from each $C_{i}$. Since the $C_{i}$ are disjoint, the chosen vertices are distinct, hence $\vert S \vert \ge t$.

**Step 4 — Algorithmic use.** Compute a maximal set $\mathcal{C}$ of vertex-disjoint cycles greedily (in polynomial time). If $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert > k$, reject immediately. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — VC-BST on a 5-Vertex Graph

Consider $G$ with vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and edges
$\{\{1,2\}, \{2,3\}, \{3,4\}, \{4,5\}, \{5,1\}, \{1,3\}\}$.
Parameter $k = 2$. Is there a VC of size $\le 2$?

| Step | Action | Remaining $k$ | Subgraph Edges | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pick $e=\{1,3\}$; **branch 1: take 1** | 1 | Remove vertex 1 → $\{\{2,3\}, \{3,4\}, \{4,5\}\}$ | Recurse |
| 1.1 | Pick $e=\{2,3\}$; **branch 1.1: take 2** | 0 | Remove 2 → $\{\{3,4\}, \{4,5\}\}$ | Recurse |
| 1.1.1 | $k=0$, edges remain | — | — | **FALSE** |
| 1.2 | Pick $e=\{2,3\}$; **branch 1.1: take 3** | 0 | Remove 3 → $\{\{4,5\}\}$ | Recurse |
| 1.2.1 | $k=0$, edges remain | — | — | **FALSE** |
| 2 | **branch 2: take 3** | 1 | Remove 3 → $\{\{1,2\}, \{4,5\}, \{5,1\}\}$ | Recurse |
| 2.1 | Pick $e=\{1,2\}$; **take 1** | 0 | Remove 1 → $\{\{4,5\}\}$ | Recurse |
| 2.1.1 | $k=0$, edges remain | — | — | **FALSE** |
| 2.2 | Pick $e=\{1,2\}$; **take 2** | 0 | Remove 2 → $\{\{4,5\}, \{5,1\}\}$ | Recurse |
| 2.2.1 | $k=0$, edges remain | — | — | **FALSE** |

All branches reject ⇒ **NO**, no VC of size 2 exists for this $G$.

(In contrast, $\{1, 2, 4\}$ is a VC of size 3, demonstrating optimality requires $k \ge 3$.)

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — FVS Cycle-Packing

Let $G$ be a graph with 6 vertices forming two disjoint triangles $T_1 = \{1,2,3\}$ and $T_2 = \{4,5,6\}$. Parameter $k = 1$.

**Step 1:** Find maximal vertex-disjoint cycle packing. Greedy DFS: pick $T_1$ → 3 vertices removed. From the remaining graph, pick $T_2$ → 3 vertices removed. Total $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert = 2$.

**Step 2:** $2 > k = 1$ → reject. Correct: to destroy both triangles we need at least one vertex from each, hence at least 2 vertices.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level FPT Paradigm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Instance I of size n, parameter k] --> B{Is k small enough for direct FPT method?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply Bounded Search Tree on k]
    B -- No --> D[Apply Kernelization Reduction Rules]
    C --> E[Recursive Branching depth k]
    D --> F[Reduced Kernel G' of size g k]
    F --> G[Brute Force / Exact Solver on Kernel]
    E --> H[Solution / NO-instance]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Output answer in f k times poly n time]
```

### 4.2 Bounded Search Tree for Vertex Cover

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Root G k with G having an edge] --> B1[Branch: take endpoint u, set k-1]
    R0 --> B2[Branch: take endpoint v, set k-1]
    B1 --> C1{G minus u has edges and k ge 0?}
    B2 --> C2{G minus v has edges and k ge 0?}
    C1 -- Yes --> D1[Pick next edge and recurse]
    C1 -- No --> E1[Return TRUE or FALSE]
    C2 -- Yes --> D2[Pick next edge and recurse]
    C2 -- No --> E2[Return TRUE or FALSE]
    D1 --> D1A[Depth 2 branches]
    D2 --> D2A[Depth 2 branches]
    D1A --> D1B[Continues to depth at most k]
    D2A --> D2B[Continues to depth at most k]
```

### 4.3 Kernelization Pipeline for Vertex Cover

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Graph G n vertices m edges parameter k] --> R1[Rule R1 Remove isolated vertices]
    R1 --> R2[Rule R2 For any vertex of degree ge k+1, add to cover S, decrement k]
    R2 --> CHK{Any more reductions possible?}
    CHK -- Yes --> R1
    CHK -- No --> B[Compute n prime vertex count]
    B --> D{n prime greater than 2k?}
    D -- Yes --> NO[Output NO instance]
    D -- No --> Y[Brute force over 2 to the n prime subsets]
    Y --> Z[Return YES with cover S or NO]
```

### 4.4 FVS Algorithm High-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Graph G parameter k] --> K[Kernelization reduce to O k squared vertices]
    K --> CP[Find maximal vertex disjoint cycle packing]
    CP --> CHK{Packing size t greater than k?}
    CHK -- Yes --> REJ[Reject NO instance]
    CHK -- No --> BR[Branch on one vertex from smallest cycle]
    BR --> RC[Recurse with k minus 1 on G minus that vertex]
    RC --> CHK2{Graph is a forest?}
    CHK2 -- Yes --> ACC[Accept YES]
    CHK2 -- No --> CP
```

### 4.5 Concept Map: VC vs FVS in FPT Landscape

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Parameterized Problems] --> FPT[FPT Class]
    P --> W1[W 1 hard]
    P --> W2[W 2 hard]
    FPT --> VC[Vertex Cover]
    FPT --> FVS[Feedback Vertex Set]
    FPT --> DOM[Dominating Set in planar graphs]
    W1 --> IS[Independent Set]
    W2 --> DOM2[Dominating Set general]
    VC --- VCtech1[BST O 2 to the k n]
    VC --- VCtech2[Kernel O k]
    FVS --- FVStech1[Cycle packing lower bound]
    FVS --- FVStech2[Branching on cycles]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style, Module 4]**

**Q1. Define Fixed-Parameter Tractability. State the FPT complexity bound and explain the role of the parameter $k$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A parameterized problem $\Pi$ with input size $n$ and parameter $k$ is **Fixed-Parameter Tractable (FPT)** if it can be solved in time

$$f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$$

where $f$ is an arbitrary computable function of $k$ only. The polynomial part depends on $n$, while all exponential blow-up is confined to $k$. The parameter $k$ is a *secondary measure of input complexity* chosen so that in practice $k$ is small (often a constant or logarithmic in $n$), making FPT algorithms efficient for relevant instances.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Module 4]**

**Q2. Define the Feedback Vertex Set problem. Why is it NP-hard, and what is its natural FPT parameter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Feedback Vertex Set (FVS)** problem: given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, decide whether there exists $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert \le k$ such that $G - S$ is a forest (acyclic). It is NP-hard because it generalizes NP-hard problems like Hamiltonian-cycle-related reductions (Karp, 1972). The natural FPT parameter is **$k$**, the size of the desired feedback vertex set.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Format: Answer ONE of the following (a) or (b). Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

#### **Question A (14 marks) — KTU University Exam Style, Dec 2024**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Describe the **Bounded Search Tree (BST) algorithm** for the Vertex Cover problem with parameter $k$. Show that the algorithm runs in $O(2^{k} \cdot n)$ time using a clear recurrence and unrolling argument.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Algorithm description (3 marks):** Present the pseudocode of the BST algorithm. State the **Forcing Lemma**: any edge $\{u, v\}$ forces at least one of $u, v$ into every vertex cover. The algorithm picks an arbitrary edge, branches on the two possibilities, and recurses with $k-1$. Base cases: empty edge set (accept) or $k = 0$ with nonempty edge set (reject).

2. **Recurrence (2 marks):** Write
$$T(n, k) \le 2 \cdot T(n-1, k-1) + c.$$

3. **Unrolling and final bound (2 marks):** Unroll to get $T(n, k) \le 2^{k} \cdot T(n-k, 0) + (2^{k}-1) c \le 2^{k} \cdot C \cdot n + (2^{k}-1) c = O(2^{k} n)$.

> **(b)** [7 marks] State and prove the **kernelization result** for Vertex Cover: the instance can be reduced in polynomial time to a kernel of size at most $2k$ vertices.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Reduction Rules R1 and R2 (3 marks):** State both rules with justifications. R1 removes isolated vertices. R2 adds a vertex $u$ with $\deg(u) \ge k+1$ to $S$ and decrements $k$ (proved by the pigeonhole/forcing argument: a $k$-cover cannot cover all $k+1$ neighbors of $u$ without including $u$).

2. **Bound on kernel size (2 marks):** After reductions, max degree is $\le k$. Every vertex in $V' \setminus S$ has all neighbors in $S$, so $\vert V' \setminus S \vert \le \sum_{u \in S} \deg(u) \le k \cdot k = k^{2}$. Hence $n' = \vert S \vert + \vert V' \setminus S \vert \le k + k^{2} \le 2k$ for $k \ge 1$.

3. **Final argument (2 marks):** If after reduction $n' > 2k$, then a $k$-sized VC cannot exist (refined argument: $V' \setminus S$ is independent and has size $> k$, contradicting $\vert S \vert \le k$). Otherwise brute force over $2^{2k}$ subsets gives total time $O(n+m) + 4^{k}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — KTU University Exam Style, May 2024**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Define the **Feedback Vertex Set (FVS)** problem and describe an FPT algorithm for it using **cycle packing and bounded branching**. State the time complexity.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Problem definition (1 mark):** Given $G = (V, E)$ and $k$, find $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert \le k$ such that $G - S$ is a forest.

2. **Cycle-packing lower bound (2 marks):** A maximal vertex-disjoint cycle packing $\mathcal{C}$ provides a polynomial-time computable lower bound: any FVS $S$ must satisfy $\vert S \vert \ge \vert \mathcal{C} \vert$ (hitting-set argument with disjointness). If $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert > k$, reject.

3. **Branching rule (3 marks):** Pick the smallest cycle $C$ in $G$. Since $C$ has at most $\vert C \vert$ vertices, branching on choosing one of them as a member of $S$ creates at most $\vert C \vert$ sub-cases, each with parameter $k-1$. For triangles ($\vert C \vert = 3$), use a refined branching that yields $O(3^{k})$ total leaves.

4. **Time complexity (1 mark):** $O(c^{k} \cdot n^{O(1)})$ for some constant $c$ (e.g., $c = 3$ for the basic version, $c$ much smaller for refined variants).

> **(b)** [7 marks] Consider the graph $G$ with vertices $\{a, b, c, d, e, f\}$ and edges
> $\{a,b\}, \{b,c\}, \{c,a\}, \{c,d\}, \{d,e\}, \{e,f\}, \{f,d\}$.
> Parameter $k = 2$. Using FVS, determine whether a feedback vertex set of size 2 exists. Justify with cycle-packing.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Identify all cycles (2 marks):** The graph contains two vertex-disjoint cycles:
   - Triangle $T_1 = \{a, b, c\}$ with edges $\{a,b\}, \{b,c\}, \{c,a\}$
   - Cycle $T_2 = \{c, d, e, f\}$ — wait, $c$ is shared with $T_1$, so they are not disjoint. Recheck: cycles in this graph include $T_1 = \{a,b,c\}$ and $C_2 = \{d, e, f\}$ with edges $\{d,e\}, \{e,f\}, \{f,d\}$ (a triangle). These are vertex-disjoint.

2. **Compute maximal cycle packing (2 marks):** $\mathcal{C} = \{T_1, C_2\}$, $\vert \mathcal{C} \vert = 2 \le k = 2$. No immediate rejection.

3. **Branch and search (3 marks):** Try $S = \{c, d\}$. After removing $c, d$: remaining edges among $\{a, b, e, f\}$ are none (since all of $a$'s and $b$'s edges involved $c$, and $e, f$'s edges involved $d$). So $G - \{c, d\}$ is an edgeless graph ⇒ a forest. **Answer: YES**, $S = \{c, d\}$ is an FVS of size 2. (Other valid FVSs: $\{a, f\}$, $\{b, f\}$, etc.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Valuator's perspective):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the parameter.** Many students solve VC with a general $n$-vertex algorithm (e.g., brute force over $2^{n}$ subsets) — this earns **zero credit** because the question is about *parameterized* algorithms. Always state $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$.
>
> 2. **Skipping the base case in recurrence unrolling.** KTU evaluators deduct 1–2 marks if the recursion does not show $T(n-k, 0) = O(n)$ or equivalent termination.
>
> 3. **Mis-stating kernelization rules.** Students often write "remove vertices of degree $> n$" instead of "degree $> k$". The parameter $k$ is essential — without it, the rule is wrong and yields no kernel.
>
> 4. **Confusing FPT with PTAS or XP.** $n^{g(k)}$ is in the class **XP**, *not* FPT. Examiners will reject $n^{k}$-time algorithms as the answer to an FPT question.
>
> 5. **Cycle-packing must be maximal or large.** A non-maximal packing can give a *weaker* lower bound, missing the rejection case. State "maximal" explicitly.
>
> 6. **Algorithm must include the YES/NO decision for FVS, not just constructive search.** Both variants exist; KTU usually asks the decision version.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **FPT = $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$** — exponential in $k$, polynomial in $n$. Class is **FPT**.
- **Parameter choice** for VC and FVS = solution size $k$.
- **Vertex Cover** = smallest $S$ hitting all edges; complement is an **independent set**.
- **Feedback Vertex Set** = smallest $S$ whose removal makes the graph acyclic (a forest).
- **Bounded Search Tree for VC** picks an edge $\{u, v\}$ and branches: take $u$ or take $v$. Recurse with $k-1$. Time: $O(2^{k} n)$.
- **Kernelization for VC**: reduction rules R1 (remove isolated vertices) and R2 (high-degree vertex $\ge k+1$ must be in the cover) yield a kernel of $\le 2k$ vertices. Solve the kernel by brute force in $4^{k}$ time.
- **Cycle-packing lower bound** for FVS: vertex-disjoint cycles force $\vert S \vert \ge$ packing size. Reject if packing size exceeds $k$.
- **FVS branching**: pick smallest cycle; branch on its vertices. Cycle structure (e.g., triangles) controls the constant in $c^{k}$.
- **Classical references** (do NOT fabricate editions): Downey & Fellows, *Parameterized Complexity*; Niedermeier, *Invitation to Fixed-Parameter Algorithms*; Cygan et al., *Parameterized Algorithms* (Springer 2015).
- **Complexity landscape**: VC and FVS are FPT; Independent Set is $W[1]$-hard; Dominating Set is $W[2]$-hard. FPT $\subset$ XP.
- **Common exam-traps**: confusing FPT with XP ($n^{k}$ time is **not** FPT); forgetting the parameter; mis-stating the kernel size; omitting base case in recurrences.
- **FPT techniques toolbox**: (1) Bounded Search Tree, (2) Kernelization, (3) Iterative Compression, (4) Color Coding, (5) Win/Win, (6) Treewidth.
- **Why NP-hard + FPT coexist**: NP-hardness says *no $n^{O(1)}$ algorithm* unless P = NP. FPT says *if $k$ is bounded, $f(k) \cdot n^{O(1)}$ suffices.* They are consistent statements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications: Network Security, Bioinformatics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST595 - ADVANCED GRAPH ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications: Network Security, Bioinformatics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Graph-Theoretic Foundations for Real-World Domains

A **graph** $G = (V, E)$ is an ordered pair consisting of a finite set of **vertices** $V$ (nodes) and a set of **edges** $E \subseteq V \times V$ connecting them. In the context of *Network Security* and *Bioinformatics*, abstract graph structures serve as the **mathematical skeleton** upon which all algorithmic analyses are layered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECST595 Module 4):**
> Graph algorithms are applied to two high-impact domains: **(1) Network Security** — modelling network topologies, attack surfaces, intrusion pathways, and traffic flow as graphs; **(2) Bioinformatics** — modelling biological sequences, molecular structures, protein interactions, and evolutionary relationships as graphs.

---

### 1.2 Network Security — Definition

**Network Security Graph Theory** is the discipline of representing computer networks, vulnerabilities, attack paths, trust relationships, and traffic flows as formal graph structures on which classical graph algorithms (shortest path, max-flow, min-cut, MST) are executed to detect, prevent, and analyse security threats.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Castle Defence Problem:**
Imagine a medieval castle with rooms (vertices) connected by corridors (edges). Each corridor has a guard (capacity) and each room has a treasure (value). A graph-based security analysis is exactly this:
- **Vertices** → Hosts, routers, servers, firewalls, network zones.
- **Edges** → Communication links, network connections, trust relationships, attack vectors.
- **Edge weights** → Latency, bandwidth, vulnerability scores (e.g., **CVSS — Common Vulnerability Scoring System**, range **0.0 – 10.0**).
- **Paths** → Possible attack sequences from adversary entry-point to critical asset.

The shortest attack path (minimum-cost path from the *attacker node* to the *target node*) tells the defender *exactly* where to deploy countermeasures.

---

### 1.3 Bioinformatics — Definition

**Bioinformatics Graph Theory** is the discipline of representing biological entities (DNA, RNA, proteins, organisms, molecular interactions) and their relationships as formal graph structures, allowing graph-theoretic algorithms to extract biologically meaningful insights such as sequence similarity, evolutionary ancestry, and functional pathways.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Family Tree of Words:**
Think of a dictionary where each word is connected to every other word by a *similarity score* (a number). A **phylogenetic tree** of language is exactly a graph problem:
- **Vertices** → Genes, proteins, organisms, sequence fragments, molecular compounds.
- **Edges** → Sequence similarity, regulatory interaction, evolutionary descent, physical binding.
- **Edge weights** → Edit distance, alignment score, Hamming distance, binding affinity.
- **Paths** → Evolutionary lineages, biochemical reaction cascades, signal transduction routes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Pillars of Graph-Based Bioinformatics:**
> 1. **Sequence-level graphs** (de Bruijn graphs for genome assembly, edit-graph for sequence alignment).
> 2. **Interaction-level graphs** (Protein-Protein Interaction networks, Gene Regulatory Networks, Metabolic Pathways).

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Across Both Domains

| Metric | Symbol | Domain | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Edit (Levenshtein) Distance** | $d_{edit}(s,t)$ | Bioinformatics | Minimum number of insertions, deletions, substitutions to transform string $s$ into $t$ |
| **Alignment Score** | $S$ | Bioinformatics | Sum of match/mismatch/gap penalties over aligned columns |
| **CVSS Score** | $\sigma$ | Network Security | Vulnerability severity (0.0–10.0) |
| **Path Vulnerability** | $\Pi(v_s, v_t)$ | Network Security | Aggregate vulnerability along an attack path |
| **Phylogenetic Distance** | $\delta(u,v)$ | Bioinformatics | Evolutionary divergence between two taxa |
| **Edge Betweenness** | $C_B(e)$ | Bioinformatics | Fraction of shortest paths through edge $e$ |

---

### 1.5 Why Graphs? — The Unifying Argument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Both domains exhibit the same structural property: complex, relational, combinatorial data with intrinsic connectivity.** Sequences can be aligned (pairwise relations); proteins interact (pairwise relations); network packets traverse (pairwise relations). The graph abstraction provides:
> - **Local reasoning** (neighbourhood analysis) → *motif discovery*.
> - **Global reasoning** (path, flow, connectivity) → *system-level behaviour*.
> - **Algorithmic tractability** (polynomial-time solvers) → *scalable computation* on huge datasets.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Module 4 — Concept Map

```
Module 4: Applications
│
├── 2.1 Network Security Applications
│   ├── 2.1.1 Attack Graph Modelling
│   ├── 2.1.2 Network Flow (Max-Flow / Min-Cut)
│   ├── 2.1.3 Shortest Path for Intrusion Detection
│   ├── 2.1.4 MST for Secure Network Topology Design
│   └── 2.1.5 Bipartite Matching for Access Control
│
└── 2.2 Bioinformatics Applications
    ├── 2.2.1 Sequence Alignment (Edit Graph / DP)
    ├── 2.2.2 De Bruijn Graph for Genome Assembly
    ├── 2.2.3 Phylogenetic Tree Construction
    └── 2.2.4 Protein-Protein Interaction Networks
```

---

### 2.1 Network Security — Deep Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Attack Graphs

An **Attack Graph** $G_A = (V_A, E_A)$ is a state-transition graph used in formal security analysis:
- **Vertices** $V_A$ → tuples $(host, privilege, connectivity)$ representing attacker state.
- **Edges** $E_A$ → single-step exploits (vulnerability exploitation, network reachability).

**Goal:** Find the *minimum-cost* path from an *initial attacker state* $s$ to a *target state* $t$ (e.g., root access on a critical server). This is solved using **Dijkstra's algorithm** if edge costs are non-negative.

**Logical reasoning (operational steps):**
1. Enumerate all network hosts and their configurations.
2. Enumerate known vulnerabilities and their **CVSS scores** $\sigma_i$.
3. Compute the *reachability matrix* $R$ where $R_{ij} = 1$ if host $i$ can connect to host $j$.
4. Build the attack graph by linking vulnerabilities across reachable hosts.
5. Apply shortest-path algorithm to find the cheapest attack chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **exploitability** of a vulnerability $v_i$ is the conditional probability $P(v_i \mid \text{reached})$ of successful exploitation. The path cost of an attack chain $e_1, e_2, \dots, e_k$ is:
> $$C_{\text{path}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \left( -\log P(v_i) \right)$$
> Minimising the sum of $-\log$ probabilities is equivalent to **maximising the joint exploit probability** — a standard shortest-path reduction.

---

#### 2.1.2 Maximum-Flow / Minimum-Cut for Traffic Analysis

The **max-flow min-cut theorem** (Ford-Fulkerson, 1956) states:

> For any network $G = (V, E)$ with source $s$ and sink $t$, the **maximum value of an $s$–$t$ flow** equals the **minimum capacity of an $s$–$t$ cut**.

**Network Security Application:**
- Model a corporate network as a flow network where **edge capacity** $c(u,v)$ is the *maximum bandwidth* between subnets.
- A **min-cut** identifies the *narrowest bottleneck* in the network — the most critical link whose failure maximally disrupts connectivity.
- This guides **defence-in-depth** strategy: harden the cut.

**Ford-Fulkerson Recurrence:** For each augmenting path $P$ with bottleneck capacity $\Delta$:
$$f'(u,v) = f(u,v) + \Delta \quad \text{if } (u,v) \in P$$
$$f'(u,v) = f(u,v) - \Delta \quad \text{if } (v,u) \in P$$

---

#### 2.1.3 Shortest-Path for Routing & IDS

In **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)**, the *signature matching* problem is reduced to shortest-path matching in a graph:
- Build an **automaton graph** of the attack signature.
- Match against live packet stream using **Aho-Corasick** (a trie-based graph traversal).
- Routing protocols (OSPF, IS-IS) use **Dijkstra's algorithm** on weighted network graphs where weights are inverse-bandwidth or delay metrics.

**Dijkstra's Relaxation (key operation):**
$$\text{dist}[v] = \min\Big(\text{dist}[v],\ \text{dist}[u] + w(u,v)\Big)$$

---

#### 2.1.4 MST for Network Topology Design

A **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** of a network of $n$ nodes with edge weights $w(e)$ gives the *cheapest connected topology* — i.e., the **minimum-cost cable layout** to connect all sites.

**Kruskal's Edge-Selection Greedy Rule:**
$$T \leftarrow T \cup \{e^*\} \quad \text{where } e^* = \arg\min_{e \in E \setminus T}\ w(e) \ \text{and}\ \text{cycle-free}(T \cup \{e^*\})$$

**Network-Security Implication:** MST designs are *single-path* between any two nodes, so adding a few *redundant* edges (to form a 2-connected graph) is a standard hardening technique.

---

#### 2.1.5 Bipartite Matching for Access Control

Model **users** and **resources** as two disjoint sets $U$ and $R$ in a bipartite graph $G = (U \cup R, E)$. An edge $(u, r) \in E$ denotes *user $u$ is permitted to access resource $r$*. A **maximum bipartite matching** finds the largest set of *non-conflicting* user-resource assignments.

**Reduction to Max-Flow:** Introduce source $s$ connected to every $u \in U$ (capacity 1) and sink $t$ connected from every $r \in R$ (capacity 1); edges $(u,r)$ have capacity 1. Then *max-flow = max-matching* (by integrality theorem).

---

### 2.2 Bioinformatics — Deep Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Pairwise Sequence Alignment (Edit Graph)

The **edit graph** $G_{edit} = (V_{edit}, E_{edit})$ is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where:
- **Vertices** $V_{edit}$ are grid points $(i,j)$ representing prefixes $S[1..i]$ and $T[1..j]$.
- **Edges** represent one of three operations:
  - **Diagonal** (match/mismatch): $(i-1, j-1) \to (i, j)$ with weight $+s$ (match) or $-p_m$ (mismatch).
  - **Horizontal** (gap in $T$): $(i, j-1) \to (i, j)$ with weight $-g$.
  - **Vertical** (gap in $S$): $(i-1, j) \to (i, j)$ with weight $-g$.

**Dynamic Programming Recurrence (Needleman-Wunsch, 1970):**
$$F(i,j) = \max\begin{cases}
F(i-1, j-1) + \sigma(S_i, T_j) & \text{(match/mismatch)} \\
F(i-1, j) - g & \text{(deletion from } S\text{)} \\
F(i, j-1) - g & \text{(insertion into } S\text{)}
\end{cases}$$
with base case $F(0,0)=0$, $F(i,0) = -i \cdot g$, $F(0,j) = -j \cdot g$.

**Time Complexity:** $O(mn)$ where $m = \vert S \vert$ and $n = \vert T \vert$.
**Space Complexity:** $O(mn)$ (reducible to $O(\min(m,n))$ with Hirschberg's algorithm).

The *alignment score* is $F(m,n)$, and the *traceback* yields the optimal alignment.

---

#### 2.2.2 De Bruijn Graph for Genome Assembly

A **De Bruijn graph** $B(k) = (V, E)$ of order $k$ over an alphabet $\Sigma$:
- **Vertices** $V$ = all $k$-mers (substrings of length $k$) appearing in the reads.
- **Edges** $E$ = all $(k\!+\!1)$-mers, where $u \to v$ if the suffix of $u$ of length $k-1$ equals the prefix of $v$ of length $k-1$.

**Genome Assembly via Eulerian Path:**
> Finding the shortest common superstring containing all reads reduces to finding an **Eulerian path** in the de Bruijn graph.

A graph has an **Eulerian path** iff it is connected and has exactly **0 or 2 vertices of odd degree**. The path traverses every edge exactly once, producing the assembled sequence.

**Pevzner et al. (2001) Theorem:** *Eulerian-path-based assembly* is mathematically equivalent to the older *Overlap-Layout-Consensus* approach but is computationally superior for short reads.

---

#### 2.2.3 Phylogenetic Tree Construction

A **phylogenetic tree** is a rooted (or unrooted) tree whose leaves are taxa and internal nodes represent hypothetical ancestors. Edge lengths represent evolutionary distance.

**Distance-Based Methods (UPGMA — Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean):**

1. Compute pairwise distance matrix $D = [d_{ij}]$ where $d_{ij}$ is the *evolutionary distance* between taxa $i$ and $j$.
2. Initially, each taxon is its own cluster.
3. Repeatedly merge the two closest clusters $C_a, C_b$:
$$d(C_a \cup C_b, C_x) = \frac{n_a \cdot d(C_a, C_x) + n_b \cdot d(C_b, C_x)}{n_a + n_b}$$
4. Record the merge height $h(C_a \cup C_b) = \frac{d(C_a, C_b)}{2}$ for rooted tree.

**Neighbour-Joining (Saitou-Nei, 1987):** Uses a corrected distance $Q_{ij} = (n-2) d_{ij} - \sum_k d_{ik} - \sum_k d_{jk}$ to overcome UPGMA's assumption of a *molecular clock*.

---

#### 2.2.4 Protein-Protein Interaction (PPI) Networks

A PPI network is a graph $G_{PPI} = (P, I)$ where proteins are vertices and verified physical interactions are edges. Key graph metrics used in systems biology:

- **Degree centrality** $C_D(v) = \deg(v)$ — identifies *hub proteins* (essential proteins).
- **Betweenness centrality** $C_B(v) = \sum_{s \neq v \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(v)}{\sigma_{st}}$ — identifies *bottleneck proteins*.
- **Closeness centrality** $C_C(v) = \frac{n-1}{\sum_{u \neq v} d(v,u)}$ — measures information-spread speed.
- **Clustering coefficient** $\gamma(v) = \frac{2 \cdot |\{e \in E : u, w \in N(v)\}|}{\deg(v)(\deg(v)-1)}$ — measures local density.
- **Modularity** $Q = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{ij}\left[A_{ij} - \frac{k_i k_j}{2m}\right]\delta(c_i, c_j)$ — measures community structure.

---

|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{\text{path}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}(-\log P(v_i))$ | Net Sec | Attack-path cost | Path of $k$ exploits |
| 2 | $\max \text{flow}(s,t) = \min \text{cut}(s,t)$ | Net Sec | Ford-Fulkerson | Capacities $\geq 0$ |
